name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage for the event where users can view featured attractions, events, promotions, basic event information, access key flows (tickets, schedule, attractions, merch, map & parking), and perform a global search using the provided interfaces.

core_features: Load homepage data via getHomeContent and expose featured attractions, featured events, featured promotions, and event overview information.
Display featured attractions with key attributes (type, scare level, age rating, price from, venue, kid-friendly flag) and navigate to the attraction detail page on selection.
Display featured events with schedule and pricing information and navigate to the event detail page on selection.
Display featured promotions (promo codes, descriptions, applicability) as informational content, without applying them on this page.
Show an event overview panel with location, address, operating dates, operating hours, and safety notes from getHomeContent.
Provide quick-access actions to tickets, attractions, full schedule, workshops & shows, merch shop, and map & parking using URL-based navigation.
Expose a global search bar that calls globalSearch with a query and optional type filters, then shows categorized search results (attractions, events, merch products).
Allow navigation from search results to the appropriate pages (attractions, events, or merch shop) using URL parameters.
user_workflows: View basic event information: user opens Home, the page calls getHomeContent; event overview component shows the location, address, operating dates/hours, and safety notes; user can click a link within this component to open the map & parking page (map_parking.html).
Buy tickets quickly: user opens Home, sees a primary 'Buy Tickets' action in the quick actions component; on click, the page navigates to tickets.html with URL parameter view=tickets.

Explore attractions: user opens Home, sees both a quick action 'Explore Attractions' and a featured attractions list; clicking the quick action navigates to tickets.html with view=attractions; clicking a specific featured attraction card navigates to attraction.html with attractionId set to the selected attraction_id.

Check schedule and workshops: user opens Home, clicks 'View Full Schedule' to go to schedule.html?view=all_events; alternatively, user clicks a 'Workshops & Shows' action to go to schedule.html?view=workshops; clicking a featured event card on Home navigates directly to event.html with eventId set to the selected event_id.

Browse merch: user opens Home, sees a 'Shop Merch' quick action or a merch teaser within a promotions or search result context; clicking this navigates to shop.html, optionally passing a query parameter used by the shop page to focus a product or category.

Use global search: user types a query into the global search bar and optionally selects what to search (attractions, events, merch); on submit, the page calls globalSearch with query and types; search results are displayed in a panel grouped by type; clicking an attraction result navigates to attraction.html?attractionId=..., clicking an event result navigates to event.html?eventId=..., and clicking a merch product result navigates to shop.html with URL parameters identifying or filtering to that product.
Access cart: user clicks the cart icon in the header; the page navigates to cart.html without calling any additional interface on the Home page.
interactions: On initial page load, a data-loading component automatically calls getHomeContent; while waiting, loading states are shown for the overview, featured attractions, events, and promotions sections; on success, these sections bind to the returned data; on error, an error message component is shown.
Clicking the 'Buy Tickets' quick action button triggers navigation to tickets.html with view=tickets using URL parameters.
Clicking the 'Explore Attractions' quick action button triggers navigation to tickets.html with view=attractions using URL parameters.
Clicking the 'View Full Schedule' quick action button triggers navigation to schedule.html with view=all_events.
Clicking the 'Workshops & Shows' quick action button triggers navigation to schedule.html with view=workshops.
Clicking the 'Shop Merch' quick action button triggers navigation to shop.html, optionally adding a query parameter used by the shop page to pre-filter or highlight items.
Clicking the 'Map & Parking' quick action button (or a link inside the overview panel) triggers navigation to map_parking.html.
Clicking a featured attraction card triggers navigation to attraction.html with the URL parameter attractionId set from the card’s attraction_id.
Clicking a featured event card triggers navigation to event.html with the URL parameter eventId set from the card’s event_id.

Submitting the global search form triggers a call to globalSearch using the current query text and selected types; the results component updates to show categorized results and may update the page URL to include the search query and types as URL parameters.
Clicking a search result for an attraction triggers navigation to attraction.html?attractionId=..., using the attraction_id from the search result.
Clicking a search result for an event triggers navigation to event.html?eventId=..., using the event_id from the search result.

Clicking a search result for a merch product triggers navigation to shop.html with URL parameters that identify or highlight the selected product (e.g., a productId parameter) for the shop page to use.
Clicking the cart icon in the header triggers navigation to cart.html without making any API calls on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not require any incoming URL parameters to function, but it can read and write optional URL parameters to control view state without using localStorage. Optional parameters include: (1) section: a string indicating which main section to focus on when the page loads (e.g., 'overview', 'attractions', 'events', 'promotions', or 'search'); when present, after getHomeContent has successfully returned, the page scrolls or focuses the corresponding section. (2) search: a string representing the search query; if present on load, the global search bar is pre-filled with this query and globalSearch is called automatically with this query and any provided types; the results panel is then opened. (3) searchTypes: a comma-separated list of types passed to globalSearch (e.g., 'attractions,events,merch_products'); if present, the global search bar pre-selects these types and the initial globalSearch call uses them. (4) promoHighlight: an optional identifier used by the promotions component to visually emphasize a specific promotion returned by getHomeContent (if any matches). The page updates the URL (e.g., using history.pushState or similar) when the user submits a new global search, so that search and searchTypes reflect the current search; other state, such as which attraction or event card was last expanded, is kept only in memory and is not stored in localStorage or cookies. All data for featured content is fetched exclusively through getHomeContent, and all search results are obtained exclusively through globalSearch; the URL parameters only affect how these results are presented and do not introduce any additional API calls.

components.functionality: On initial page load, call getHomeContent once, manage loading and error states, and distribute the resulting data to the overview, featured attractions, featured events, and promotions components.

Provide top-level navigation links including a Home link, access to Tickets, Schedule, Shop, Map & Parking (via links to their respective pages), display a cart icon that navigates to cart.html, and contain the global search bar component.

Capture the user’s search query and selected search types, call globalSearch on submit, and propagate search results to the search results component; optionally initialize its state from the search and searchTypes URL parameters and update those parameters when a new search is submitted.

Display categorized search results returned by globalSearch, grouping them by type (attractions, events, merch products), handle empty-result states, and provide click targets that route the user to the appropriate detail or listing pages using URL parameters.

Display core event information from getHomeContent.event_overview, including location name, address, operating dates, operating hours, and safety notes, and expose actions or links to open the map & parking page.

Provide concise, high-priority actions for users to quickly access Buy Tickets, Explore Attractions, View Full Schedule, Workshops & Shows, Shop Merch, and Map & Parking; each action navigates to the corresponding page with the required URL parameters.

Render a list of featured attractions from getHomeContent.featured_attractions, showing key details such as name, attraction type, scare level, age rating, starting price, venue name, and kid-friendly indication; handle loading, empty, and error states and navigate to attraction.html with the appropriate attractionId when an item is clicked.

Render a list of featured events from getHomeContent.featured_events, showing title, start/end datetimes, venue name, and pricing or free indication; handle loading, empty, and error states and navigate to event.html with the appropriate eventId when an item is clicked.

Display promotional information based on getHomeContent.featured_promotions, including promo code, description, and applicability; respond to the optional promoHighlight URL parameter by emphasizing a matching promotion; provide informational text only without applying promo codes on this page.

Interpret the optional section URL parameter on load and after URL changes to determine which major section (overview, attractions, events, promotions, search) to scroll to or focus; coordinate with the data loader to ensure scrolling/focus happens only after data is available.

Show non-intrusive status messages related to getHomeContent and globalSearch (such as errors, empty results, or transient notices); it subscribes to success/error events from the data-loader and search components and does not store any state in localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom layout anchored by a cinematic hero at the top and a dense, image-driven three-column grid beneath it. The hero section merges the event overview, global search, and quick actions into a single, immersive visual block that spans the full content width. Below the hero, the primary content (featured attractions, events, and promotions) is organized into three equal-width columns, mirroring the mockup’s gallery. The section router and global search results appear as full-width bands between the hero and the grid (or temporarily overlay the grid) but do not break the overall linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom layout anchored by a cinematic hero at the top and a dense, image-driven three-column grid beneath it. The hero section merges the event overview, global search, and quick actions into a single, immersive visual block that spans the full content width. Below the hero, the primary content (featured attractions, events, and promotions) is organized into three equal-width columns, mirroring the mockup’s gallery. The section router and global search results appear as full-width bands between the hero and the grid (or temporarily overlay the grid) but do not break the overall linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 45–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full page width (within a max-width content container), emphasizing the event’s imagery. The navigation and notification banner are slim horizontal strips at the top, layered over the hero. Below the hero, the three-column grid fills most of the remaining viewport height, with narrow horizontal gutters between columns and small margins to the page edges. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero, search results panel, gallery) follow a 48–64px rhythm, while internal gaps between components within a section remain tighter (16–32px) to maintain density.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense and image-first. The hero image is large and dominant with text and controls overlaid in compact stacks. The three-column grid packs image cards closely together with narrow black gutters (8–16px), evoking a horror-themed portfolio wall. Text is minimal and layered atop images or within dark, compact overlays. Whitespace is limited to functional breathing room around interactive elements; the black background and card edges provide separation rather than large empty areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first pulled to the center of the hero by the neon-styled event title and overview text. From there, it drops down to the global search bar and quick actions aligned along the vertical center. As the user scrolls or looks further down, attention transitions to the section router and then to the three-column content grid. Within the grid, scanning follows a left-to-right pattern across the top row of cards, then down the columns, mirroring the image gallery behavior from the mockup. Global search results, when visible, interrupt this flow as a focused, full-width dark panel directly below the hero, temporarily becoming the primary focal area above the grid.

layout.overall_description: The homepage reads as a dark, cinematic landing experience. At the very top, a minimal navigation bar and, when needed, a slim notification banner sit as translucent strips overlaying a full-width hero image. The hero dominates the upper half of the viewport, showing a gritty zombie-event scene with the event overview text, global search, and a compact quick-actions row stacked along the vertical center. The background is almost entirely black outside of the imagery, making the neon-lit hero content pop. Immediately beneath this hero, a narrow band holds the section router and, when active, the global search results panel stretches full-width as a dark slab of content. Below this, the page resolves into a tight three-column grid of image-driven cards for attractions, events, and promotions, filling the screen horizontally with thin black gutters and extending downward as the user scrolls. The overall impression is that of a horror-themed poster wall: immersive, dense, and media-heavy, with just enough structure for search, navigation, and quick access actions without breaking the cinematic feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader presents as a full-viewport overlay that sits on top of all other content when active. It is a semi-opaque black veil covering the entire page width and height, dimming the hero and grid beneath. At the absolute center of the viewport (both horizontally and vertically), a small, high-contrast loading indicator or spinner appears, possibly with a faint neon accent to match the horror theme. No gutters or margins are visible for this component; it fully occupies the screen and prevents interaction with underlying elements until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioned at 0,0 covering 100% viewport width and height, sitting at the top of the stacking context when active and hidden otherwise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation bar sits at the very top edge of the page, spanning the full width from left to right with a height of about 64px. It aligns with the hero image beneath, appearing as a translucent, matte-black strip that overlays the top of the hero rather than sitting on a separate background. The event logo or wordmark is anchored on the left within the first 2–3 grid columns, while key nav links (Tickets, Schedule, Attractions, etc.) are clustered on the right across the last 3–4 columns. Vertical padding inside the bar is minimal (around 12–16px), emphasizing a low-profile silhouette. On scroll, the navigation remains pinned to the top of the viewport, creating a constant, slim control bar above the cinematic content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport; overlays the hero background, maintaining its position as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner appears directly below the header nav, forming a second, thinner horizontal strip. It spans the same full content width, running edge to edge, with a height of roughly 40–48px. Its background is a saturated accent (e.g., blood-red or toxic orange) against the black page, making it highly noticeable. Text is centered vertically within the strip and aligned slightly left-of-center horizontally to keep the bar visually anchored beneath the navigation. The banner overlays the upper portion of the hero image just like the nav bar, effectively consuming a thin band of space at the top of the hero while leaving the main hero composition intact below. When dismissed, the hero content visually shifts up, reclaiming this vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a fixed-height strip immediately below the sticky nav; scrolls with the page content but sits on top of the hero and fades out entirely when dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event overview panel is the core of the hero. It is centered horizontally and vertically within the hero image’s safe area, roughly occupying the middle 60% of the content width (about 7–8 of the 12 grid columns). It appears as a text block layered directly atop the hero background, with a subtle, wide gradient or soft dark overlay behind it to enhance legibility without breaking the cinematic image. The event title sits at the top of this block, large and neon-glowing, followed by a short tagline, dates, and location stacked downward with tight vertical spacing (8–16px gaps). The panel itself does not have visible borders; instead, its footprint is defined by the text and the underlying gradient. Above and to the sides, the hero image continues uninterrupted, making the overview feel embedded within the scene rather than boxed in.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, centered within the hero’s vertical height (which takes up ~45–50% of the viewport), and moves out of view as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is positioned just below the main event title and key details within the event overview panel, still inside the hero area. It is horizontally centered on the page and spans approximately 60% of the content width (about 7 of 12 columns), aligning visually with the overview text above. Vertically, it sits in the lower half of the hero so that it remains clearly visible but doesn’t obscure the main title. The input field has a low-profile, pill-like or rectangular shape with a dark charcoal background and subtle neon edge or glow, standing out against the darker hero. Vertical spacing from the preceding text is about 24px, and from the quick actions beneath it about 16–24px, maintaining a tight, stacked composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; when the user scrolls, it moves up and off-screen along with the hero. When focused, the page does not reflow substantially but may slightly dim surrounding hero elements to emphasize the input.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick actions toolbar is a horizontal row of compact buttons placed directly beneath the global search bar, still within the hero’s visual frame. It is centered horizontally to align with the search bar above and constrained to roughly the same width (about 7 of 12 columns), though the buttons themselves are sized to their labels with 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding each. The buttons are arranged left-to-right with tight 8–12px gaps between them, forming a dense, pill-like cluster that sits near the lower third of the hero. This placement makes them feel like part of the hero’s interactive overlay rather than a separate section. There is minimal vertical padding above and below the toolbar, preserving the hero’s image dominance while keeping the actions readily accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section under the search bar. It scrolls away with the hero, but on smaller screens it can wrap to two lines while remaining centered within the same horizontal zone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section router sits immediately below the hero, acting as a subtle transition band between the cinematic hero and the denser three-column grid. It spans the full content width, but its interactive elements (tabs or pill-like filters such as All, Attractions, Events, Promotions) are centered and constrained to the central 50–60% of the page width. The band itself has a solid black background, visually blending with the rest of the page, while the router controls stand out with faint neon outlines or underscores. Vertical padding above and below the controls is modest (16–24px), creating a slim, functional strip. This router visually reads as a control row framing the content grid that follows, with a small gap (about 24px) before the first row of cards begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width horizontal strip positioned directly after the hero section. It scrolls normally, remaining at the top of the viewport only when naturally reached via scrolling and then moving up as the user continues downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search results panel appears as a dark, full-width content slab directly beneath the hero (and the section router) when results are present, temporarily pushing the three-column gallery downward. It spans the same max content width as the hero and grid, with narrow side margins to the browser edge (approximately 5–10% of viewport width on each side). The panel uses a slightly lighter black or charcoal background than the page, framed by soft shadows to distinguish it from the surrounding darkness. Inside, results are arranged in a simplified, image-forward list: either a dense single-column stack or a responsive two- to three-column layout that still respects the main 12-column grid. Vertical padding of 24–32px at the top and bottom of the panel gives the results enough breathing space while retaining a compact, immersive feel. The panel sits as a continuous rectangle, so the user reads hero → search → results before ever reaching the featured grid below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal document flow between the hero/section router and the featured grid, pushing content below it down when visible. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay content once the user has scrolled past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured attractions list occupies the leftmost third of the three-column grid below the section router (and below the search results panel when it is visible). It spans roughly 4 of the 12 grid columns and extends vertically down the page as a stacked column of image-driven cards. The top one or two attraction cards are sized to visually align with the top row of cards in the neighboring event and promotion columns, together forming a coherent 2-row by 3-column mosaic reminiscent of the mockup’s gallery. Cards sit nearly edge-to-edge within the column, separated by 8–12px vertical gaps and using narrow horizontal gutters (about 8–12px) from adjacent columns. Each card features a full-bleed attraction image with a dark overlay and minimal text (title, perhaps a short label) in the lower portion, maintaining the image-centric aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three-column grid. As the user scrolls, this left column moves up and out of view in sync with the other two columns, forming part of a unified masonry-like grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured events list lives in the center third of the three-column grid, immediately to the right of the attractions column and left of the promotions column. It occupies approximately the middle 4 grid columns and is vertically aligned so that its top card sits at the same height as the top cards in the other two lists. Like the attractions list, it is a vertical stack of event cards, each a full-bleed image tile with minimal text overlaid in the bottom-left or center. The first one or two cards match the height of those in the attractions and promotions columns, contributing to the unified 2×3 mosaic effect. Subsequent cards continue downward, maintaining consistent 8–12px vertical spacing. This central placement draws the eye naturally after scanning down from the hero, making it feel like the main continuation of the narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, center column of the three-column grid. It scrolls normally as part of the grid, keeping its cards in strict alignment with adjacent columns at the top of the section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured promotions list occupies the rightmost third of the grid, covering roughly the last 4 of the 12 columns. It mirrors the attractions and events columns in structure: a vertical stack of promotion cards that are image-led with minimal overlaid copy (discount tags, callouts, or short promo titles). The top cards are matched in height with the top cards in the attractions and events lists to complete the first row of the mosaic; the second row of the mosaic is created by the next set of cards in each column where applicable. Cards are separated vertically by 8–12px and horizontally by a narrow black gutter from the events column. This column anchors the right side of the grid, completing the portfolio-like wall of imagery under the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the rightmost column of the grid, scrolling with the other lists. It behaves identically in spacing and alignment to the attractions and events lists to preserve the uniform gallery pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick actions toolbar is already described above, but to restate in this context: it is visually integrated into the hero as a horizontal strip of pill buttons centered under the search bar. It occupies roughly the same width as the search bar (around 60% of the content width) and sits in the lower-middle region of the hero. Each button is compact and tightly spaced, echoing the dense aesthetic of the gallery below while remaining clearly tappable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the hero; it scrolls away with the hero and does not become sticky or detached from the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: As previously described, the section router forms a slim full-width band beneath the hero, holding centered filter controls that align with the three-column content but remain visually light. This band visually and functionally separates the cinematic hero region from the structured grid of content that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width row in normal document flow; scrolls with the page.

name: Tickets & Attractions Listing
title: Tickets & Attractions
description: Browse and filter all ticketed attractions and experiences, switch between tickets-focused and attractions directory views, and navigate to attraction detail and booking pages.

core_features: Determine initial view mode (tickets or attractions) using the URL 'view' parameter, falling back to saved user preference via getPageViewPreference and available modes from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions.
Fetch and display a list of attractions using getTicketsAttractionsListing based on current view mode, date, time-of-day, tags, scare-level range, age ratings, and sort order.
Provide a view-mode switcher that lets users toggle between 'tickets' and 'attractions' views, updating the URL and saving the preference via savePageViewPreference.
In tickets view: provide filters for date (calendar control), time of day, and basic tag filters (from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions) and apply them via getTicketsAttractionsListing.
In attractions view: provide filters for tags, scare-level range, and age ratings using options from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions, and apply them via getTicketsAttractionsListing.
Allow users to change sort order for the listing by setting the 'sort' URL parameter and refetching data via getTicketsAttractionsListing.
Render each attraction as a card with summary information and starting price using data from getTicketsAttractionsListing.
Allow users to mark/unmark attractions as favorites on each card using addAttractionToFavorites and removeAttractionFromFavorites, and reflect the updated favorite state.
Show a favorites banner when at least one listed attraction is marked as favorite, with navigation to favorites.html.

Navigate to attraction.html when an attraction card is clicked, passing the attraction id, current date filter, current time-of-day filter, current sort key, and a 'ref=tickets' parameter as defined in outgoing connections.
Keep all selected filters, view mode, and sort order encoded in the URL parameters so returning from detail pages or shared links restores the same listing state.
user_workflows:
Landing on the page with default or saved view:
1. User opens tickets.html, optionally with URL parameters (e.g., view, date, time_of_day, tags, scare_min, scare_max, age_ratings, sort).
2. Page-init logic calls getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions to retrieve available view modes, time-of-day options, tag filters, scare-level bounds, and age-rating options.
3. Page reads the 'view' URL parameter:
   - If present and valid (matches one of the returned view_modes), that value is used as current view_mode.
   - If absent, the page calls getPageViewPreference with page_code='tickets_page'. If a valid view_mode is returned that exists in view_modes, that is used.
   - If neither URL parameter nor preference is usable, the page uses a default view_mode chosen from the view_modes returned by getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions (the page does not hardcode a specific value).
4. Page parses other URL parameters (date, time_of_day, tags, scare_min, scare_max, age_ratings, sort) into an internal filter state.
5. Page calls getTicketsAttractionsListing with the derived view_mode and filter state.
6. When the response returns, the page renders the filters (with current selections) and the list of attraction cards.

Filtering in tickets view (date, time-of-day, tags):
1. User is in view_mode='tickets'. The tickets-specific filter bar shows controls for date, time_of_day, and tag filters using options from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions.
2. User changes the date using the calendar control.
3. The component updates the internal date state and synchronizes the URL 'date' parameter (YYYY-MM-DD). The browser history is updated (e.g., using pushState/replaceState).
4. The page calls getTicketsAttractionsListing with the updated date and current time_of_day, tags, scare_level_min, scare_level_max, age_ratings, sort, and current view_mode.
5. When the new listing data returns, the attractions list is re-rendered with updated availability/pricing for the selected date.
6. Similarly, when the user changes time_of_day or toggles tag filters in tickets view, the component updates the corresponding URL parameters ('time_of_day', 'tags'), updates internal state, re-calls getTicketsAttractionsListing, and re-renders.

Filtering in attractions view (tags, scare-level range, age ratings):
1. User switches to or lands in view_mode='attractions'. The attractions-specific filter bar shows controls for tag filters, scare-level range (min/max), and age ratings using data from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions.
2. User adjusts the scare-level minimum and maximum values.
3. The component updates internal scare_level_min and scare_level_max state and updates the URL parameters 'scare_min' and 'scare_max'.
4. The page calls getTicketsAttractionsListing with updated scare_level_min/scare_level_max (mapped from 'scare_min'/'scare_max'), and other current filters.
5. When the new listing data returns, the attractions list is re-rendered.
6. Tag and age-rating selections similarly update the 'tags' and 'age_ratings' URL parameters, trigger a new getTicketsAttractionsListing call, and refresh the list.

Switching between tickets and attractions views:
1. User clicks the view-mode switcher control (for example, to change from tickets to attractions view).
2. The switcher updates the internal view_mode to the selected mode, updates the URL 'view' parameter accordingly, and calls savePageViewPreference with page_code='tickets_page' and the new view_mode.
3. The page re-runs getTicketsAttractionsListing with the new view_mode and the existing filter parameters (some filters may be ignored server-side depending on the mode).
4. The UI updates to show the relevant filter bar for the new view_mode and re-renders attraction cards based on the returned data.

Changing sort order:
1. User selects a sort option (e.g., price-based or time-based) from the sort control.
2. The sort control updates internal sort state and sets the 'sort' URL parameter to the selected sort key (values are defined outside this page and understood by the backend).
3. The page calls getTicketsAttractionsListing with the updated sort and current filters and view_mode.
4. Listing is re-rendered based on the new sort order.

Marking and unmarking favorites:
1. User views the list where each attraction card indicates its favorite status using the 'is_favorite' value returned from getTicketsAttractionsListing.
2. When the user clicks the favorite (heart/save) icon on a non-favorited card, the favorites toggle component calls addAttractionToFavorites with the card's attraction_id.
3. On successful response, the card updates its is_favorite state to true and the heart icon appears active. The favorites banner logic reevaluates whether any listed attractions are favorites.
4. When the user clicks the icon on an already favorited card, the component calls removeAttractionFromFavorites with that attraction_id.
5. On successful response, the card updates its is_favorite state to false and the icon reflects the change. The favorites banner is updated as needed.

Navigating to attraction detail and preserving context:
1. User clicks anywhere on an attraction card's primary action area (excluding the favorite icon) to view details or start booking.
2. The card click handler reads the card's attraction_id and the current filter state (date, time_of_day, sort from URL/state).
3. The page navigates to attraction.html with parameters as defined:
   - attractionId set to the card's attraction_id
   - date set to the current date filter (if any)
   - time_filter set to the current time_of_day filter (if any)
   - sort set to the current sort key (if any)
   - ref set to 'tickets'
4. The detail page can provide a 'Back to attractions' action using browser history or the referring URL. Because this page always encodes filters and view_mode into the URL, returning to tickets.html via such a back action restores the same listing and filters.

Using favorites banner to go to Favorites page:
1. After marking one or more attractions as favorites, the favorites banner becomes visible (based on the is_favorite values in the current listing).
2. User clicks the 'View all favorites' link within the banner.
3. The page navigates to favorites.html with no additional parameters as specified in outgoing connections.
interactions: On page load (onPageLoad): parse URL parameters, call getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions, resolve initial view_mode (with optional getPageViewPreference), derive initial filter state, then call getTicketsAttractionsListing and render results.

On URL change (onUrlChange, for example via browser back/forward): re-parse URL parameters into filter and view_mode state, and re-call getTicketsAttractionsListing to reflect the URL state in the UI.

On view-mode switcher click (onClickViewMode): update view_mode, update 'view' URL parameter, call savePageViewPreference, then call getTicketsAttractionsListing with the new mode and current filters.
On date change in tickets view (onChangeDate): update internal date value, update 'date' URL parameter, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.
On time-of-day change in tickets view (onChangeTimeOfDay): update internal time_of_day, update 'time_of_day' URL parameter, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.
On tag filter change (onChangeTagFilter): update selected tags list, serialize to comma-separated 'tags' URL parameter, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.

On scare-level range change in attractions view (onChangeScareRange): update internal scare_level_min and scare_level_max, synchronize to 'scare_min' and 'scare_max' URL parameters, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.

On age-rating filter change in attractions view (onChangeAgeRatings): update selected age_ratings, serialize to comma-separated 'age_ratings' URL parameter, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.
On sort selection change (onChangeSort): update internal sort key, set 'sort' URL parameter, call getTicketsAttractionsListing, re-render listing.
On attraction card click (onClickCard): navigate to attraction.html with attractionId, date, time_filter, sort, and ref=tickets as per outgoing connections.
On favorite icon click when not favorite (onClickFavoriteAdd): call addAttractionToFavorites with attractionId; on success, mark card as favorite and trigger rerender of favorites banner state.
On favorite icon click when already favorite (onClickFavoriteRemove): call removeAttractionFromFavorites with attractionId; on success, mark card as not favorite and update favorites banner state.
On 'View all favorites' click in favorites banner (onClickViewFavorites): navigate to favorites.html.
On header cart icon click (onClickCartIcon): navigate to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page's authoritative state is derived from URL parameters, augmented by backend-provided options and view preferences:

1) URL → Internal State Mapping:
- view: mapped to internal view_mode and passed directly as 'view_mode' to getTicketsAttractionsListing. If 'view' is absent or invalid, the page falls back to getPageViewPreference('tickets_page') and, if still unresolved, to a default from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions.view_modes.
- date: if present, used as the current date filter (string in YYYY-MM-DD) and passed as 'date' to getTicketsAttractionsListing. If absent, the page does not hardcode a default; either the backend applies its own default, or a configured default is used outside this page's logic.
- time_of_day: used as the current time-of-day filter. Passed as 'time_of_day' to getTicketsAttractionsListing as-is.
- tags: a comma-separated string parsed into an array of tag codes, stored in internal state, and passed as 'tags' to getTicketsAttractionsListing.
- scare_min: converted to a numeric value and mapped to internal scare_level_min; when calling getTicketsAttractionsListing, this is passed as 'scare_level_min'.
- scare_max: converted to a numeric value and mapped to internal scare_level_max; when calling getTicketsAttractionsListing, this is passed as 'scare_level_max'.
- age_ratings: a comma-separated string converted into an array of age rating codes, stored in internal state, and passed as 'age_ratings' to getTicketsAttractionsListing.
- sort: stored as the current sort key and passed as 'sort' to getTicketsAttractionsListing.

2) Internal State → URL Synchronization:
- Any user interaction that changes view_mode or filters updates the corresponding URL parameters (view, date, time_of_day, tags, scare_min, scare_max, age_ratings, sort) using navigation APIs that modify the query string without a full reload.
- This ensures that the URL always fully represents the current listing state. Deep links can therefore be shared, and the 'Back to attractions' context from attraction.html can rely on browser history to restore state.

3) API Coordination:
- getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions is called once on page initialization to provide dynamic filter metadata (view_modes, time_of_day_options, tag_filters, scare_level_min/max bounds, age_rating_options). No filter option values are hardcoded on the page; all selectable values are sourced from this response.
- getTicketsAttractionsListing is called whenever relevant URL parameters change or when the page is first initialized, using the mapped parameters. It is the sole source of attraction listing data, including is_favorite flags.
- addAttractionToFavorites and removeAttractionFromFavorites mutate favorite state; after each successful call, the page aligns the UI state with the operation (e.g., toggling is_favorite on the relevant card). If needed, the page may re-call getTicketsAttractionsListing to ensure consistency.
- getPageViewPreference and savePageViewPreference are used strictly for reading and persisting the preferred view_mode, never as the primary state source (the URL remains primary).

4) Return-from-detail Behavior:
- When navigating to attraction.html, the page includes current date, time_of_day (as time_filter), and sort alongside ref=tickets. Because the listing page's state is always encoded in its URL, using browser back or a history-based 'Back to attractions' on the detail page returns the user to tickets.html with the previously encoded query parameters, which the page re-parses to restore view mode, filters, and sort without any localStorage usage.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, view mode resolution, and listing fetch; listens to URL changes and refreshes data accordingly.
Displays available view modes (e.g., tickets, attractions) from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions and allows the user to switch modes, updating the URL and saving the choice.

Shown when view_mode is 'tickets'; provides controls for date selection, time-of-day selection, and basic tag filters using options from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions; updates URL parameters and triggers listing refresh.

Shown when view_mode is 'attractions'; provides tag filters, scare-level range controls, and age-rating filters using options from getTicketsAttractionsViewOptions; updates URL parameters and triggers listing refresh.
Lets users choose the sort order for the listing (using sort keys understood by the backend); updates the 'sort' URL parameter and refreshes the listing via getTicketsAttractionsListing.

Renders the list of attraction cards using data from getTicketsAttractionsListing, reflecting current filters, view_mode, and sort; also controls visibility of the favorites banner based on is_favorite flags.

Displays summary information for a single attraction (name, description snippet, type, scare level, age rating, tags, starting price, venue, status, and favorite state) and handles navigation to the attraction detail page when clicked.
Embedded in each attraction card; reflects whether the attraction is currently a favorite and toggles that state by calling addAttractionToFavorites or removeAttractionFromFavorites.
Appears when at least one attraction in the current listing has is_favorite=true; informs the user that they have saved attractions and offers navigation to the full favorites list.
Provides access to the cart page; clicking navigates to cart.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, cinematic stack: a full-width, image-driven hero at the very top (not part of this component list but assumed), immediately followed by a compact horizontal control band (view mode, favorites toggle, sort, filters), and then a dense, three-column grid of attraction cards filling the rest of the page. Controls are grouped into one contiguous band, not scattered, so the structure still reads as: hero strip on top, then a slim utility strip, then a gallery-like grid below.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, cinematic stack: a full-width, image-driven hero at the very top (not part of this component list but assumed), immediately followed by a compact horizontal control band (view mode, favorites toggle, sort, filters), and then a dense, three-column grid of attraction cards filling the rest of the page. Controls are grouped into one contiguous band, not scattered, so the structure still reads as: hero strip on top, then a slim utility strip, then a gallery-like grid below.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height. The control band directly beneath it is visually slim (about 64–96px total across one or two rows), spanning the full content width. Below that, the tickets-attractions-list takes the majority of the remaining vertical space, arranged as a 3-column grid (each card ≈ 1/3 of the content width). Side gutters are modest—about 24–32px from browser edge to content container—with 8–16px gutters between cards, echoing the tight gallery layout from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderately high: images and cards nearly fill the horizontal space, with narrow black gutters between them. The control band is compact with minimal vertical padding, using tight 8–16px spacing between elements. There is a small vertical gap between hero and controls, and another small gap before the card grid, but the overall feeling is of continuous, immersive dark content rather than airy whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup: the eye first hits the bright, neon-rimmed hero in the top center. As the user scrolls slightly, attention moves to the horizontal control band, reading left-to-right (view mode, favorites, sort, filters). From there, focus drops directly into the first row of cards and then scans left-to-right across each row of the three-column grid, then down to subsequent rows. Sticky behavior on the control band helps maintain a top-to-bottom, then left-to-right scanning rhythm as users explore the listing.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a full-bleed, dark cinematic hero image for the zombie attraction, spanning the entire content width and about half the viewport height, with neon-lit title artwork centered in the frame. Overlaid at the very top is a slim global header where the cart link sits in the top-right corner, subtly glowing above the imagery. As you scroll just past the midpoint of the hero, a dark control band appears anchored under the header: on the left, a compact view-mode switcher lets you flip between Tickets and Attractions; to its right, a small favorites toggle and a sort control cluster toward the upper-right of the content area. Directly beneath this first control row, a full-width filters bar appears—either the tickets filters or attractions filters, depending on the mode—stretching from left to right within the same black backdrop. When active, a narrow favorites banner sits just under the filters, spanning the content width like a warning strip, before giving way to the main grid. The main body of the page is a three-column gallery of attraction cards, each one a dark, high-contrast image tile with a subtle glow, arranged in tight rows with narrow black gutters between them. The cards extend almost to the viewport edges, framed only by small side margins, so the overall experience feels like a wall of horror attractions glowing out of the darkness with minimal chrome and text clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init-controller is non-visual and occupies no visible space on the screen. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page structure, above the hero section and all other components in the DOM hierarchy. It initializes which view (Tickets or Attractions) is active on first load, decides which of the two filters bars should be displayed, applies default sort order, and determines whether the favorites-banner should appear by default. Although invisible, its logical position is at the root of the layout, governing the initial state of the control band and the tickets-attractions-list that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely functional and non-rendered; does not consume layout space, does not scroll, and has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-mode-switcher sits in the first row of the control band immediately below the hero. Within the main content container (which is centered with approximately 24–32px margins from the viewport edges), the switcher is left-aligned and occupies roughly 25–30% of the content width, appearing as a dark, pill-like segmented toggle. It is placed about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero, with 8–12px padding above and below within its control row. Horizontally, it anchors the left side of the controls, with a small 16–24px gap to its right before any adjacent elements (such as the favorites-toggle-button). On larger screens, it remains left-anchored; on smaller screens, it can stretch closer to 50% width while still sitting in the same row. The control band it belongs to spans the full width of the content area and, after the user scrolls past the hero, this band sticks to the top of the viewport beneath the global header, keeping the view-mode switcher always accessible above the grid of cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded in a horizontally spanning control band that becomes sticky to the top of the viewport once the hero scrolls out of view; otherwise scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tickets-filters-bar appears as the second row of the control band, directly beneath the row that contains the view-mode-switcher, favorites-toggle-button, and sort-control. It only becomes visible when the page is in Tickets view. It spans nearly the full width of the content container, starting about 24px from the left viewport edge and ending about 24px from the right, matching the alignment of the card grid below. Within this horizontal strip, filter chips or dropdowns are left-aligned, with about 16–24px of vertical padding inside the bar itself. The bar’s height is modest—roughly 56–72px depending on the number of controls—maintaining the dense feel of the layout. There is a tight vertical gap of about 16px separating this bar from the control row above, and another 16–24px margin below it leading into either the favorites-banner (if visible) or directly into the first row of the grid. Visually, it reads as a dark, slightly raised band against the black background, keeping the cinematic focus on imagery while still being clearly legible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same sticky behavior as the rest of the control band—remaining pinned under the global header once scrolled into place—so filters stay in view while the user scrolls through the card grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The attractions-filters-bar occupies the exact same spatial zone as the tickets-filters-bar: it forms the second row of the control band, immediately below the row with the view-mode-switcher, favorites-toggle-button, and sort-control. It is mutually exclusive with the tickets-filters-bar, so only one is visible at a time. When the page is in Attractions view, this bar spans the full width of the content container, with its left edge aligned to the left edge of the card grid and its right edge aligned to the right edge, leaving roughly 24–32px margins to the browser edge. Filter chips or dropdowns are left-aligned, and any overflow filters can scroll horizontally within the bar but remain visually constrained to its height. Vertical padding is kept tight (around 12–16px) to preserve a dense, cinematic feel, with approximately 16px of vertical spacing above (from the control row) and 16–24px below (before the favorites-banner or grid). It blends into the dark background with only subtle borders or glow, so it doesn’t compete with the image-heavy grid beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Like the tickets-filters-bar, it is part of the sticky control band that remains fixed to the top of the viewport under the header once the user has scrolled past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control shares the first row of the control band with the view-mode-switcher and favorites-toggle-button. It is right-aligned within the content container, sitting flush to the right edge of the grid alignment (approximately 24–32px from the right edge of the viewport). The control itself takes up about 15–20% of the content width and appears as a compact dropdown or icon-plus-label element. It sits on the same horizontal baseline as the view-mode-switcher, with around 8–12px of vertical padding above and below inside the control band. A narrow gap of 8–16px separates it from the favorites-toggle-button to its left. The visual treatment is understated—a dark, slightly raised surface with light text—so it is readable but subordinate to the overall imagery. When the sticky behavior kicks in, the sort-control travels with this top row, maintaining its position anchored to the right side as users scroll the card grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the sticky top control band, remaining fixed under the global header as the user scrolls through the listing; otherwise it scrolls normally when the hero is still in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tickets-attractions-list forms the main body of the page and is arranged as a tight, three-column grid reminiscent of the mockup’s photo gallery. It begins directly below the control band, or below the favorites-banner if that banner is visible. The grid is centered within the page’s content container, with left and right margins of about 24–32px to the viewport edge. Each row contains three attraction-card components of equal width, each occupying roughly one-third of the content width (≈4 columns of an implicit 12-column grid), separated by narrow horizontal gutters of about 16px. Vertically, rows are spaced with 16–24px gaps, so the grid looks dense and image-driven but still readable. The background between cards remains pure black, creating strong framing for the imagery. As the user scrolls down, the grid continues in this three-up rhythm, creating a mosaic wall of attractions. There is minimal extra vertical padding around the list; it feels like a continuous gallery extending from just below the controls down toward the page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the full scrollable body area beneath the sticky control band; it scrolls normally while the band remains fixed, giving the impression of cards sliding under a static top toolbar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each attraction-card is a tile in the three-column grid that visually echoes the mockup’s gallery thumbnails. On desktop, each card is approximately one-third of the content width, minus narrow horizontal gutters (around 16px between cards). The card’s height is governed primarily by a full-bleed image with an aspect ratio of roughly 3:4 or 16:9, giving each tile a cinematic feel. The image fills the entire card width with no internal padding, and a subtle, soft radius (or squared but not heavily bordered) keeps it clean against the black background. At the bottom of the image, a dark translucent gradient overlays the lower 20–30% of the card, containing the attraction name, a short descriptor (ticket type or attraction category), and key meta info (price, time) in compact, stacked lines. This text block has 12–16px internal padding. There is no external frame or thick border; the separation comes from the black gutters around the cards. Vertically, there is about 16–24px space below each card before the next row. The cards collectively create a rhythmic, L-to-R scanning pattern in each row, drawing attention to the imagery first and text second. Each card is entirely clickable, reinforcing the impression of a gallery of experiences rather than a list of traditional content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the responsive three-column grid; cards wrap to new rows as needed. No fixed or sticky behavior—each card scrolls naturally with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-toggle-button is a small, high-contrast control living in the first row of the control band, between the view-mode-switcher and the sort-control. It is positioned to the right of the view-mode-switcher with a horizontal gap of about 16px, and to the left of the sort-control with an 8–16px gap, sitting on the same baseline. The button is compact—roughly the width of a small label plus a star or heart icon—and has about 8–12px vertical padding and 12–20px horizontal padding, matching the tight spacing rules. It does not span full width; instead, it occupies only the space it needs, contributing to a clustered, tool-like row. Visually, it can glow subtly or use a neon accent when active, making it noticeable without dominating the layout. Its location just under the hero and directly above the grid keeps the connection clear: toggling it immediately changes what appears in the cards below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the sticky control band, so it remains visible and interactive at the top of the viewport as users scroll through the list of attraction-cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-banner appears as a full-width notification strip within the content container, positioned immediately below the filters bar (tickets-filters-bar or attractions-filters-bar) and directly above the first row of attraction-cards. When displayed, it spans the same width as the grid—aligned to the same left and right bounds with 24–32px margins from the browser edge. Its height is modest, around 48–56px, with 12–16px vertical padding, so it feels like a tight, cinematic ribbon rather than a large panel. The banner features a darker, slightly tinted background (e.g., a subtle blood-red glow against black) with clear, centered or left-aligned text indicating the favorites state (such as “Showing only your favorite horrors”). A small close or 'clear' action can align to the right edge. There is a small vertical gap of about 16px between the bottom of the banner and the top row of cards, preserving a visual separation without breaking the continuous dark backdrop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When visible, it scrolls with the content, positioned directly below the sticky control band. It does not itself stick; as the user scrolls, the banner moves upward with the grid while the control band remains pinned above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-link lives in the global site header, which is positioned above the hero section. The header is a slim, dark horizontal bar that either overlays the top of the hero or sits directly above it, spanning the full browser width. Within this header, the cart link is anchored to the far right, approximately 24px from the right edge of the viewport, aligned horizontally with any logo or navigation on the left. The link itself is compact—a small icon with an optional item count badge—and uses a slightly brighter neon or light tone to stand out against the dark header. It does not intrude into the content area: the hero imagery begins just below the header, and the control band begins below the hero, so the cart link stays visually isolated as part of global chrome rather than the content grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the global header, which is typically sticky at the top of the viewport. As the user scrolls, the header (and thus the cart link) remains pinned above the hero and control band, providing constant access to the cart without affecting the layout of the main content.

name: Attraction Detail & Ticket Booking
title: Attraction Tickets & Booking
description: Detail page for a single attraction where users can view attraction information, select a date and timeslot, configure ticket options and add-ons, apply a promo code, see live pricing, and add the selection to their cart or proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load full attraction details and available timeslots for a given date using getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots(attractionId, date, time_filter, sort).
Display attraction metadata such as scare level, age rating, duration, venue/location, tags, and base price range from the attraction data.
Provide controls to select or change the event date, time filter, and sort order, and reload timeslots via getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots with updated parameters.
List all available timeslots for the selected date, indicating availability, pricing per ticket type, and VIP-only restrictions, and allow the user to select exactly one timeslot.
Show available ticket types (e.g., standard, vip) per attraction from ticket_options and allow the user to select a ticket type (or use the default).
Allow the user to configure ticket quantity or party size (depending on attraction rules defined at implementation time) for the chosen timeslot.
Display optional add-ons from add_on_options with selectable checkboxes, including handling of required add-ons that must always be selected.
Provide a promo code input that calls getAttractionPricingPreview to validate and apply discounts for the current configuration, and update a pricing summary with the returned breakdown.
Offer a live pricing summary (base subtotal, add-ons total, promo discount, line total before tax) based on getAttractionPricingPreview for the current configuration.
Allow users to add the configured tickets to the cart via addTicketsToCart and optionally stay on the page.
Allow users to add tickets and then immediately proceed to checkout by calling addTicketsToCart and navigating to checkout.html with from=attraction.
Provide a favorites toggle for the attraction, using addAttractionToFavorites and removeAttractionFromFavorites to update the single-user favorites collection.
Provide navigation links back to the Tickets view for the current date (tickets.html?view=tickets&date=...) or back to all attractions (tickets.html?view=attractions).
Allow users to open the cart page (cart.html) after adding tickets, for example via a 'View Cart' action or a cart icon.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Open attraction detail from tickets listing: 1) User clicks an attraction card on tickets.html which links to attraction.html with attractionId, date, time_filter, sort, ticket_type, and ref. 2) attraction.html reads URL parameters and calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots with attractionId, date, time_filter, sort. 3) Page displays attraction details, date/time controls pre-populated from URL, and the filtered/sorted timeslot list. 4) If ticket_type is present in the URL and exists in ticket_options, that ticket type is preselected in the configuration component.

Workflow 2: Change date, filter, or sort and choose a timeslot: 1) User adjusts the date control; the page updates the date query parameter in the URL and re-calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots with the new date while keeping attractionId, time_filter, and sort consistent. 2) User optionally changes time_filter or sort; the page updates those parameters in the URL and re-calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots. 3) Timeslot list updates with new data. 4) User selects one timeslot; the selected timeslotId is stored in page state and used as input for pricing preview and add-to-cart operations.

Workflow 3: Configure tickets and see pricing: 1) After selecting a timeslot, user chooses a ticket type if more than one is available (or keeps the default). 2) User sets quantity or party size in a numeric input. 3) User optionally selects add-ons via checkboxes; required add-ons, if any, are automatically selected and cannot be unselected. 4) On any change to timeslot, ticket_type, quantity/party_size, or add_on_ids, the page calls getAttractionPricingPreview with attractionId, timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity, party_size, add_on_ids, and the current promo_code (if any). 5) The pricing summary component displays the latest pricing_breakdown and indicates whether a promo was applied.

Workflow 4: Apply or change promo code: 1) User types a promo code into the promo input and clicks an Apply action. 2) Page validates that a timeslot and ticket quantity/party size have been selected; if not, it blocks the action and shows a validation message without calling the API. 3) If valid, page calls getAttractionPricingPreview with current configuration and promo_code. 4) If success is true, page updates the promo_code in state using promo_code_normalized and updates the pricing summary with the returned pricing_breakdown and promo_applied flag. 5) If success is false, page shows the message from the API and clears or retains the previous promo state according to implementation logic.

Workflow 5: Add configured tickets to cart and continue browsing: 1) User completes configuration, optionally applies a promo code, and reviews the pricing summary. 2) User clicks Add to cart. 3) Page validates that a timeslot is selected and that quantity/party_size meets business rules (e.g., minimum 1). 4) Page calls addTicketsToCart with attractionId, timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity, party_size, add_on_ids, promo_code (if applied), and action_context indicating an add-to-cart action. 5) If success is true, page updates any cart summary indicator (if present) using cart_summary, and may display the returned message. 6) User can then click a cart navigation element to go to cart.html or continue adjusting the configuration.

Workflow 6: Add tickets and proceed to checkout: 1) User configures tickets and clicks Proceed to checkout / Reserve Now. 2) Page performs the same validation as Add to cart. 3) Page calls addTicketsToCart with action_context indicating a checkout-intent action. 4) If success is true, page redirects to checkout.html?from=attraction so the checkout page knows the source context. 5) If the API call fails, page shows message and stays on the same page for correction.

Workflow 7: Manage favorites: 1) User toggles a favorites control (e.g., button) in the attraction header. 2) If the attraction is not currently in favorites (tracked in page state from prior responses or a separate initialization), page calls addAttractionToFavorites with attractionId. 3) If success is true, page updates the visual state of the toggle and may reflect favorites_count if displayed. 4) If the attraction is already in favorites and the user toggles it off, page calls removeAttractionFromFavorites with attractionId, and on success, updates the visual state and any favorites_count display.

Workflow 8: Navigate back to listings: 1) If the user wants to return to date-specific tickets view, they click Back to tickets for this date, which navigates to tickets.html with view=tickets and date equal to the current date state (which is synchronized with the date URL parameter on attraction.html). 2) If the user wants to return to the general attractions listing, they click Back to all attractions, which navigates to tickets.html?view=attractions. 3) The ref parameter from the incoming URL can be used to decide which back link is emphasized or shown by default, without affecting outgoing URLs beyond the defined view and date parameters.
interactions: On page load, attraction.html reads attractionId, date, time_filter, sort, ticket_type, and ref from the URL query string and immediately calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots with attractionId, date, time_filter, and sort to populate attraction details, ticket options, add-ons, and timeslot list.

When the user changes the date in the date selector, the component updates the date parameter in the URL (replacing any existing date) and re-calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots with the new date and current attractionId, time_filter, and sort values.

When the user selects a time filter (such as evening_after_6pm, start_after_8pm, or before_7pm), the component updates time_filter in the URL and re-calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots, updating the timeslot list according to the server-side filtering.

When the user selects a sort option (such as price_low_to_high or earliest_time_first), the component updates sort in the URL and re-calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots, updating the order of displayed timeslots.

When the user clicks on a specific timeslot in the list, the selected timeslotId is stored in page state; the UI marks that timeslot as selected and any subsequent calls to getAttractionPricingPreview or addTicketsToCart use this timeslotId.

When the user changes the ticket type (for example, toggling between standard and vip), the component updates selected ticket_type in state and also updates the ticket_type parameter in the page URL to allow deep-linking; it then triggers getAttractionPricingPreview with the new ticket_type and current configuration, if a timeslot is already selected and quantity/party_size is valid.

When the user increments or decrements ticket quantity or modifies party size, the component validates the numeric value (e.g., minimum 1, maximum not exceeding capacity_remaining if implemented on the client side) and, if valid, triggers getAttractionPricingPreview to refresh pricing based on the new amount.

When the user checks or unchecks add-on checkboxes, the component updates the list of selected add_on_ids in state; if a required add-on is present, the component enforces that it remains selected. After any change, if a timeslot is selected and quantity/party_size is valid, it calls getAttractionPricingPreview.

When the user types into the promo code field, no API calls are made until they explicitly submit (e.g., click Apply or press Enter). On submit, if configuration is valid, the component calls getAttractionPricingPreview with the entered promo_code and updates the pricing summary using the response; if promo_applied is false or success is false, the component displays the message from the API and may clear the promo input.

When the user clicks Add to cart, the booking component first performs client-side validation (timeslot selected, ticket_type chosen, quantity/party_size valid, required add-ons selected). If validation passes, it calls addTicketsToCart and, on success, optionally shows a confirmation state along with cart_summary data; the user can then navigate to cart.html via a cart link interaction.

When the user clicks Proceed to checkout / Reserve Now, similar validation and addTicketsToCart call occur; on a successful response, the page immediately navigates to checkout.html?from=attraction without waiting for further user input.

When the user toggles the favorites control, the component decides whether to call addAttractionToFavorites or removeAttractionFromFavorites depending on the current favorite state and updates the control’s appearance based on the success flag and favorites_count in the response.

When the user clicks Back to tickets for this date, the page constructs a URL tickets.html?view=tickets&date=current_date_state and navigates there. When the user clicks Back to all attractions, the page navigates to tickets.html?view=attractions.

When the user clicks any cart icon or View Cart action available on the page, the browser navigates to cart.html without additional parameters, relying on the cart created or updated by addTicketsToCart.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from and synchronized with URL query parameters and API responses. On initialization, attractionId is required and must be present in the URL; without it, the page cannot call getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots. Optional parameters date, time_filter, sort, and ticket_type, if provided, are passed directly into getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots and also populate the initial UI controls. The ref parameter is used only to determine contextual back-link behavior (for example, prioritizing a 'Back to favorites' label in the UI logic if implemented) but does not directly affect API calls. After the initial API call resolves, the page stores in state: attraction details, the current array of timeslots, ticket_options, add_on_options, and any derived flags such as whether VIP is supported. As the user changes date, time_filter, or sort, the page updates those values in the URL and makes a new getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots call; the attractionId parameter never changes while on this page. Selected timeslotId, selected ticket_type, quantity or party_size, selected add_on_ids, and the current promo_code are kept in transient page state and are not added as URL parameters beyond ticket_type, which is synchronized for deep-linking. Whenever a configuration is complete enough (timeslotId set and quantity/party_size valid), the page can call getAttractionPricingPreview; its latest successful response is stored as the current pricing state and rendered in the pricing summary. The normalized promo code from pricing preview (promo_code_normalized) replaces any previously-entered promo code in state. When the user triggers addTicketsToCart, the current configuration state (attractionId, timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity, party_size, add_on_ids, promo_code) and an action_context describing the user’s intent are sent to the API; on success, cart_summary is not persisted in URL but may be used to update any cart indicator in state. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; page state is fully determined by the current URL parameters plus the data returned from the assigned interfaces during the lifetime of the page session.

components.functionality: Displays the main attraction information, including name, short description, scare level, age rating, duration, venue name, location note, tags, base price information, kid-friendly indicator, and integrates the favorites toggle for this attraction.
event_handlers: onInitLoad(attractionId, date, time_filter, sort) -> calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots and initializes attraction state.
onFavoriteToggle(attractionId) -> calls addAttractionToFavorites or removeAttractionFromFavorites based on current favorite state and updates the UI according to the success result.

components.functionality: Provides functional links to go back to the tickets listing for the current date or back to the full attractions listing, using the current date filter from state and fixed view parameters.

Allows the user to select the event date, time_filter, and sort options for timeslots, updates the URL accordingly, and triggers reloading of attraction timeslots using getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots.
event_handlers: onDateChange(newDate) -> updates date query parameter in URL, calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots(attractionId, newDate, current_time_filter, current_sort), and refreshes timeslot data in state.
onTimeFilterChange(newFilter) -> updates time_filter in URL, calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots(attractionId, current_date, newFilter, current_sort), and refreshes timeslot data.
onSortChange(newSort) -> updates sort in URL, calls getAttractionDetailAndTimeslots(attractionId, current_date, current_time_filter, newSort), and refreshes timeslot ordering.

components.functionality: Displays the list or grid of available timeslots for the selected date, filtered and sorted according to current controls, and lets the user select one active timeslot to configure tickets for.
event_handlers: onTimeslotSelect(timeslotId) -> updates selected_timeslot_id in page state and triggers a pricing refresh via getAttractionPricingPreview if ticket quantity/party_size and ticket_type are already valid.
onTimeslotHover(timeslotId) -> optional, can highlight details; no API calls required.

components.functionality: Provides controls for selecting ticket type, ticket quantity or party size, and linking those selections to the currently chosen timeslot to form a complete ticket configuration.
event_handlers: onTicketTypeChange(ticket_type_code) -> updates selected ticket_type in state, updates ticket_type query parameter in URL, and if a timeslot and quantity/party_size are valid, calls getAttractionPricingPreview with current configuration.
onQuantityChange(quantity) -> validates quantity according to business rules; if valid and a timeslot is selected, calls getAttractionPricingPreview with updated quantity.
onPartySizeChange(party_size) -> validates party_size; if valid and a timeslot is selected, calls getAttractionPricingPreview with updated party_size.

components.functionality: Lists available add-on options for the attraction and allows the user to select or deselect them, enforcing required add-ons when specified, and integrating the selection into pricing and cart configuration.
event_handlers: onAddOnToggle(add_on_id, checked) -> updates selected add_on_ids in state; if a timeslot and quantity/party_size are valid, calls getAttractionPricingPreview with the new set of add_on_ids.
onRequiredAddOnEnforcement() -> ensures add-ons marked as is_required remain selected and cannot be unchecked, adjusting UI state without additional API calls.

components.functionality: Captures a promo code from the user, validates that the current configuration is complete enough to evaluate, and then calls getAttractionPricingPreview to check and apply the discount.
event_handlers: onPromoInputChange(promo_code_input) -> updates promo_code in transient state; does not call any API.

onPromoApplySubmit() -> if selected_timeslot_id and ticket quantity/party_size are valid, calls getAttractionPricingPreview(attractionId, timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity, party_size, add_on_ids, promo_code_input); updates pricing summary and promo state with the response.
onPromoClear() -> clears promo_code from state and re-calls getAttractionPricingPreview without a promo_code to show non-discounted pricing, if configuration is otherwise valid.

components.functionality: Displays the current pricing details for the selected configuration, including base_subtotal, add_ons_total, promo_discount, and line_total_before_tax, reflecting the latest response from getAttractionPricingPreview.
event_handlers: onPricingPreviewUpdate(pricing_preview_response) -> updates the visible pricing fields and indicates whether a promo is applied based on success, promo_applied, and pricing_breakdown.

onConfigurationChange() -> listens to changes in configuration state (timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity/party_size, add_on_ids, promo_code) and prompts the corresponding components to call getAttractionPricingPreview; this component itself does not call APIs directly.

components.functionality: Provides action buttons to add the current ticket configuration to the cart or to add and immediately proceed to checkout, handling validation and invoking addTicketsToCart.
