event_handlers: onAddToCartClick() -> validates configuration (selected_timeslot_id, ticket_type, quantity/party_size, required add-ons); if valid, calls addTicketsToCart(attractionId, timeslotId, ticket_type, quantity, party_size, add_on_ids, promo_code, action_context='add_to_cart'); on success, uses cart_summary to update any cart indicator and may display the message.
onProceedToCheckoutClick() -> performs the same validation and calls addTicketsToCart with action_context='checkout'; on success, navigates to checkout.html?from=attraction.
onViewCartClick() -> navigates directly to cart.html so the user can review their cart after a successful addTicketsToCart call.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic for marking or unmarking the current attraction as a favorite, showing its state independently of other header information if needed.
event_handlers: onMount(attractionId) -> may initialize favorite state by checking whether attractionId is in the current favorites collection (if provided by surrounding context).

onToggleFavorite(attractionId, isCurrentlyFavorite) -> if not favorite, calls addAttractionToFavorites(attractionId); if favorite, calls removeAttractionFromFavorites(attractionId); updates internal favorite state and any displayed favorites_count based on the response.

components.functionality: Provides a simple navigation control to access the cart page and, optionally, displays a cart item count using data returned from addTicketsToCart.
event_handlers: onCartSummaryUpdate(cart_summary) -> updates any displayed cart count or subtotal when addTicketsToCart returns new cart_summary data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, center-aligned stack: a dominant full-width attraction hero at the top, followed by an immediately visible, grid-like time-slot selection area that echoes the 3-column gallery from the mockup. Below that, ticket configuration, add-ons, promo code, pricing, and booking actions are stacked as distinct dark sections, each spanning the main content width with subtle separation. Navigation and cart are minimal, sitting as a thin bar above the hero, and favorites is integrated directly into the hero surface.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, center-aligned stack: a dominant full-width attraction hero at the top, followed by an immediately visible, grid-like time-slot selection area that echoes the 3-column gallery from the mockup. Below that, ticket configuration, add-ons, promo code, pricing, and booking actions are stacked as distinct dark sections, each spanning the main content width with subtle separation. Navigation and cart are minimal, sitting as a thin bar above the hero, and favorites is integrated directly into the hero surface.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The attraction hero occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans about 90–100% of the content width (with small side margins). The date controls and time-slot grid together take up the next 25–35% of the vertical space, visually acting as the 'gallery' layer. Ticket configuration, add-ons, and promo code are medium-height blocks, each separated vertically by 32–48px. Pricing summary and booking actions form a denser, high-importance block near the bottom, with tighter internal spacing. Margins to the browser edge are modest to maximize image and control real estate over empty space.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense: sections are clearly separated, but within each section controls and imagery are packed with relatively narrow gutters (8–16px) and compact vertical spacing (16–24px between related elements). Hero and time-slot grid emphasize visual immersion with near edge-to-edge coverage. Form sections have sufficient padding (16–24px) for legibility but avoid large, airy whitespace to keep the cinematic, intense feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye is pulled first to the bright, cinematic hero image in the top center, then moves downward to the time-slot grid which behaves like a gallery of selectable tiles. Within the grid, the eye scans left-to-right across each row of time slots. Below that, attention descends through ticket configuration and add-ons, finally landing on the high-contrast pricing summary and bold booking actions. The breadcrumb and cart link at the very top are visually quiet, supporting quick orientation without competing with the hero.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep black backdrop with a centered content band that stretches close to full width, giving the experience a cinematic, full-bleed feeling. At the very top, a thin, low-contrast navigation strip with breadcrumbs at the left and a cart icon at the right quietly frames the experience. Immediately beneath it, a large, neon-lit attraction hero image dominates the upper half of the viewport, spanning almost the entire width and setting the horror event mood. The attraction title and key info overlay the lower-left area of the hero, while a favorites heart floats in the top-right corner of the image, all glowing subtly against the dark. A narrow strip of black separates the hero from a dense booking section below, where the date selector stretches across the width, and underneath it a 3-column grid of time-slot tiles forms a uniform mosaic reminiscent of the mockup’s photo gallery. Further down, the ticket configuration, add-ons, and promo code blocks stack one after another as dark panels with thin gutters, each still spanning the same width so the overall silhouette reads as a clean vertical slab of content. At the bottom of this stack, a slightly more contrasted pricing summary and bold booking buttons form a clear end-cap, anchoring the user’s journey from cinematic discovery at the top to decisive action at the bottom, all within a tight, image-driven, horror-themed visual atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The attraction header is realized as a full-width hero image that dominates the top portion of the page, sitting directly beneath the slim navigation row. It spans approximately 90–100% of the available content width, leaving only narrow side margins of 24–32px against the browser edge, and occupies about 40–50% of the initial viewport height. The hero is centered within the page and stretches almost to the left and right edges to recreate the immersive, image-first feel from the mockup. The attraction title and key details (location, rating, duration) are overlaid in the lower-left quadrant of the hero, arranged in a compact vertical stack with 8–16px spacing, so that they sit above the darker part of the image for legibility without adding separate chrome. The lower edge of the hero transitions sharply back to pure black background with a modest vertical gap (24–32px) before the date-and-time section begins, similar to the small separation between hero and gallery in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width hero section that scrolls with the page; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is placed at the very top of the content, forming a slim horizontal strip that spans the same content width as the hero below but uses the pure black site background. It is left-aligned within this strip, starting roughly 24px from the left content boundary and sitting about 16–24px from the top edge of the viewport. The breadcrumb text is relatively small and low-contrast compared to the hero typography, ensuring it remains functional but visually subordinate. Vertically, it shares the same narrow band as the cart navigation on the right, creating a minimal top frame that does not compete with the hero but gives users a sense of location before their attention drops to the large attraction image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row at the top of the page; scrolls away normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-and-time controls form the top bar of the booking section directly beneath the hero, spanning the full content width so they read as a single, continuous strip above the time-slot grid. They are center-aligned within the same horizontal bounds as the hero, with roughly 32–48px of vertical gap from the hero’s bottom edge. Within this strip, date selectors (e.g., a row of date pills or a compact calendar toggle) are laid out horizontally from left to right, using moderate spacing (16–24px) between items to echo the tight but distinct gutters of the image grid below. The height of this bar is relatively modest—about 64–96px total—keeping it compact and functional while leaving visual dominance to the hero and the grid of time slots that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that spans the content width above the time-slot list; scrolls with the page with no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeslot list sits immediately below the date-and-time controls and is laid out as a uniform 3-column grid that strongly echoes the mockup’s photo gallery. Each time-slot tile occupies roughly one-third of the content width, with narrow horizontal gutters of 8–16px between columns and a similar vertical gap between rows. The grid typically displays two visible rows by default (6 slots), but can extend vertically as needed while maintaining the 3-column rhythm. Each tile is a dark, slightly raised card or pill-like block with its time centered, giving the impression of a gallery of selectable panels rather than plain buttons. The overall grid section spans the full content width and extends down the page based on the number of slots, with about 32–48px of vertical spacing separating its bottom edge from the start of the ticket configuration form below, preserving the stacked, gallery-beneath-hero pattern from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid section that expands vertically based on content; tiles reflow to fewer columns (e.g., 2 or 1) on smaller screens but maintain equal-width columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket configuration form appears as a full-width, dark panel directly beneath the timeslot grid, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–48px. It spans the same content width as the hero and grid, with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides so form fields are comfortably readable without feeling airy. Fields (ticket types, quantities, age groups) are arranged in a compact vertical stack or in short horizontal groupings, but always contained within this single panel rather than splitting into separate sidebars, preserving the linear stack behavior of the mockup. The form’s height adapts to its content, but visually it reads as one uninterrupted block of controls that continues the top-to-bottom flow from hero to gallery to configuration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level form section that scrolls with the page; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-ons selector sits directly beneath the ticket configuration form, with a similar vertical separation (around 32px) to mark it as its own section while maintaining the dense stack. It spans the full content width and uses the same internal padding (16–24px). Within this section, individual add-ons are presented as card-like tiles arranged either in a 2–3 column grid depending on viewport width, with narrow horizontal and vertical gutters of 16px to mirror the structure of the earlier timeslot grid. This gives the add-ons area a mini-gallery feel, aligning with the image-driven DNA even if cards are more text-heavy or icon-based. The section’s bottom edge sits 24–32px above the promo code form, keeping the flow continuous and visually cohesive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section with an internal responsive grid; expands vertically as more add-ons are shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo code form is a compact, horizontally oriented strip that appears below the add-ons selector, acting as a narrow band before the more substantial pricing and action area. It spans roughly 60–80% of the content width and is centered within the main content column, leaving small side margins of black on either side so it doesn’t visually overpower the adjacent sections. The input field and apply button are arranged in a single row with 8–16px spacing, and the entire strip has 12–16px of vertical padding. There is about 24px of vertical space between this form and the add-ons above, and another 24–32px before the pricing summary below, allowing it to feel like a brief interstitial control in the flow rather than a major section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control section that scrolls with the page; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing summary sits near the bottom of the page’s main stack, directly above the booking actions, and shares a combined section with them to create a strong call-to-action zone. This section spans the full content width and is slightly more contrasted than other panels (e.g., a subtly lighter dark background) to stand out on the black canvas. Within the upper part of this section, the pricing breakdown (line items, taxes, fees, total) is left-aligned and arranged in a tight vertical stack with 8–12px between rows, using about 60–70% of the width. The right side can remain open or used for subtle visual accents, but the emphasis stays on the textual breakdown. Vertical padding of 16–24px ensures clarity without diluting the dense, cinematic atmosphere. A small vertical gap (16–24px) separates the last line of pricing from the booking actions directly below within the same visual block, so they read as connected but distinct elements of one decision zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that anchors the lower part of the page; scrolls normally without sticky behavior, though it is visually emphasized as an endpoint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking actions (primary 'Add to Cart' / 'Book Now' buttons and any secondary actions) are positioned immediately beneath the pricing summary within the same combined lower section, forming the bottom-most focal area of the page. The primary button is right-aligned or centered within the content width, with secondary actions placed either to its left or directly below with 8–16px spacing. Buttons are large and horizontally expansive (covering roughly 30–50% of the content width) with generous internal padding (12px vertical, 20–24px horizontal) to match the bold, cinematic character. There is minimal vertical space (about 16px) between the pricing summary and the main button cluster so that they visually read as a single decision unit, and around 32–48px of bottom padding before the content fades back into the black page background, gracefully terminating the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action area at the bottom of the main content stack; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites toggle appears as an icon-only or icon-plus-label control overlaid directly on the top-right area of the attraction hero image, mirroring the minimal chrome feel of the mockup. It is anchored about 24px inward from the hero’s right edge and 24px down from the top edge of the image, floating above the visual content without its own separate background bar. The control is compact but visually distinct, using a brighter accent color (e.g., neon red or green) that glows subtly against the dark image. Its position ensures it is clearly associated with the attraction itself, not with the global navigation, reinforcing the cinematic, full-image approach while keeping the control unobtrusive in the visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlaid control positioned absolutely relative to the hero section; scrolls away with the hero image as the page moves.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart navigation sits in the same thin top strip as the breadcrumb navigation but is right-aligned, forming a subtle visual counterbalance to the left-aligned breadcrumbs. It is placed approximately 24px from the right content boundary and vertically centered in the small 48–64px tall top bar. The cart is likely represented by a small icon (optionally with an item count badge), using a slightly brighter accent color than the breadcrumb text but still much less saturated than the hero’s neon highlights, to avoid competing for attention. This keeps the global cart action always discoverable at the top of the page while preserving the low-chrome, image-dominant feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation element in the top bar; scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page.

name: Shopping Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Review and manage tickets, merchandise, and parking passes currently in the cart, adjust quantities and fulfillment options, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Fetch and display the current cart contents via getCartSummary, grouping items by item_type (e.g., tickets, merch, parking_pass) and showing quantities, prices, and item details.
Allow users to change item quantities (and party sizes where applicable) using updateCartItemQuantity, updating line totals and cart subtotal in real time.
Allow users to remove individual cart items using removeCartItem, updating the cart summary and handling the case where the cart becomes empty.
Display the current merchandise delivery method from getCartSummary.merch_delivery_method and allow users to change it using setCartMerchDeliveryMethod.

Provide item-specific navigation: edit ticket items by going to attraction.html with appropriate URL parameters, view/edit merch items via product.html, and change parking selections via map_parking.html.
Display an order cost summary using subtotal, taxes, fees, and estimated_total from getCartSummary, and provide a clear button to proceed to checkout.html with from=cart.
Use the optional highlightItemId URL parameter to scroll to and visually emphasize the corresponding cart item, and optionally surface contextual messaging based on the from URL parameter.
user_workflows: Review and adjust cart contents: (1) User navigates to cart.html (optionally with highlightItemId and from parameters). (2) Page controller calls getCartSummary and renders grouped lists of items. (3) User reviews ticket, merch, and parking items, including quantities, unit prices, and line totals. (4) If highlightItemId is present, the corresponding item row is scrolled into view and marked as highlighted.

Change item quantity or party size: (1) User locates a cart item row and interacts with its quantity (and optionally party size) control. (2) On change, the row component calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity/party_size values. (3) On success, the component updates the item’s displayed quantity, line_total, and triggers a refresh or local update of subtotal and related summary values. (4) On failure, the component shows an inline error message using the message returned.

Remove an item from the cart: (1) User clicks a remove action on a cart item row. (2) The row (or a dedicated control) calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId. (3) On success, the item is removed from the page, and the cart summary subtotal and item count are updated using the response or by re-calling getCartSummary. (4) If remaining_items_count is 0, the empty cart state component is shown instead of item lists. (5) On failure, an inline error message is displayed.

Change merchandise delivery method: (1) User views the delivery method selector component that reflects the current merch_delivery_method from getCartSummary. (2) User selects a different option (e.g., ship_to_address or pick_up_at_event_venue). (3) The selector calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod with the chosen delivery_method. (4) On success, the selector updates its selected state and may trigger a re-fetch of getCartSummary if delivery method affects fees or totals. (5) On failure, the selector reverts to the previous value and shows the returned message.

Edit ticket details from the cart: (1) User clicks an action like “Edit tickets” on a ticket-type cart item row. (2) The row component uses its bound ticket details (ticket_attraction_id, date) to navigate to attraction.html?attractionId=<ticket_attraction_id>&date=<date>. (3) On attraction.html, the user modifies timeslot, ticket type, or add-ons and may add updated tickets back to the cart, eventually returning to cart.html.

Edit merch or parking items from the cart: (1) For merch items, user clicks “View product” or similar on the merch item row, which navigates to product.html?productId=<merch_product_id>. (2) For parking passes, user clicks “Change parking”, navigating to map_parking.html?parkingAreaId=<parking_area_id>. (3) After changes on those pages, new or updated items can be added to the cart, and the user may return to cart.html with highlightItemId set to the updated or newly added item.

Proceed to checkout: (1) After reviewing items and totals, user clicks the “Proceed to checkout” button. (2) The button navigates to checkout.html?from=cart. (3) The checkout page then uses the existing Cart as the source for creating an Order during the checkout flow.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters (highlightItemId, from), call getCartSummary once data dependencies are ready, and then render the cart contents and totals.
On quantity input change or quantity stepper interaction within an item row, debounce and trigger updateCartItemQuantity with the new quantity (and party_size when applicable).
On click of an item’s remove control, call removeCartItem with the corresponding cartItemId, then update the UI based on the returned success flag and cart_subtotal/remaining_items_count.

On change of the merchandise delivery method selector (e.g., radio buttons or dropdown), call setCartMerchDeliveryMethod with the selected delivery_method and update the visual selection state when successful.
On click of ticket-specific actions, navigate using URL parameters to attraction.html?attractionId=<ticket_attraction_id>&date=<date> derived from the cart item’s details.
On click of merch-specific actions, navigate to product.html?productId=<merch_product_id> derived from the cart item’s details.
On click of parking-specific actions, navigate to map_parking.html?parkingAreaId=<parking_area_id> derived from the cart item’s details.
On click of the “Proceed to checkout” button, navigate to checkout.html?from=cart without modifying cart data on this page.

If highlightItemId is present and matches a cart_item_id from getCartSummary, programmatically scroll that row into view and set an internal isHighlighted flag for that row; clear or ignore the flag when the user navigates away or the item is removed.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial render, the page reads highlightItemId and from from the URL query string (e.g., cart.html?highlightItemId=<id>&from=<context>). It then calls getCartSummary to retrieve the current Cart and CartItem data, including merch_delivery_method and totals. The in-memory page state includes: (1) the full cart summary response; (2) derived groupings of items by item_type or item_type_label; (3) a highlightedItemId state derived from the URL; and (4) transient UI states such as loading flags, error messages, and the currently selected merch_delivery_method. The highlightedItemId state is used after getCartSummary returns to find and mark the corresponding item row as highlighted and scroll it into view; if no matching item exists, no special behavior occurs. If the user changes quantities (via updateCartItemQuantity) or removes items (via removeCartItem), the page either uses the returned updated values (line_total, cart_subtotal) to adjust state directly or triggers a fresh call to getCartSummary to ensure consistency, especially if taxes or fees depend on item composition. When the user changes the merchandise delivery method via setCartMerchDeliveryMethod, the page updates its local merch_delivery_method state and may optionally re-call getCartSummary if delivery method affects fees or totals. Navigation to other pages passes only the parameters specified: attraction.html?attractionId=<ticket_attraction_id>&date=<date>, product.html?productId=<merch_product_id>, map_parking.html?parkingAreaId=<parking_area_id>, and checkout.html?from=cart, ensuring that all cross-page context is contained in the URL rather than localStorage.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, fetching of cart data via getCartSummary, grouping of items by type, and distribution of data and callbacks to child components. Manages in-memory state for cart summary, highlightedItemId, loading/error states, and current merch_delivery_method.

Displays contextual information when the page is reached with a highlightItemId and/or from URL parameter, such as confirming that an item was added from a specific source context. Uses the highlighted cart item’s description to provide user feedback.

Receives the full list of items from getCartSummary and groups them by item_type or item_type_label (e.g., tickets, merch, parking_pass). Renders each group with an appropriate header and delegates individual item rendering to specialized item row components based on item_type.

Renders a single ticket-type cart item, including attraction name, date, time, ticket type, add-ons from details, quantity (and party size if applicable), unit price, and line_total. Provides controls to update quantity, optionally adjust party size, remove the item, and navigate to edit ticket details on attraction.html.

Renders a single merch-type cart item, displaying product_name from details (and any other descriptive data available via description), quantity, unit_price, and line_total. Provides controls to update quantity, remove the item, and navigate to the product details page via product.html for further editing or viewing.

Renders a single parking_pass-type cart item, including parking_area_name and arrival_datetime from details, along with quantity, unit_price, and line_total. Provides controls to remove the parking pass and a link to change parking by navigating to map_parking.html with the appropriate parkingAreaId.

Reusable control for adjusting CartItem quantity (and optionally party_size). Validates user input (e.g., ensuring quantity is at least 1), and calls updateCartItemQuantity when changes are committed. On success, emits an event for the parent controller to update totals; on failure, displays an inline error message.

Provides a remove button or link for a cart item. When activated, calls removeCartItem with the item’s cart_item_id and, based on the response, either removes the item from the UI and updates subtotal or displays an error.

Shows the current merch_delivery_method for the cart and allows the user to switch between available methods (ship_to_address, pick_up_at_event_venue). When the selection changes, it calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod and, on success, updates the selected method and notifies the controller so any dependent totals can be refreshed.

Displays the cart’s subtotal, taxes, fees, and estimated_total as returned by getCartSummary. Updates whenever the cart summary is re-fetched or when quantity/removal operations provide updated subtotal data.
Provides the primary action for moving from the cart to the checkout flow. On click, validates that the cart is not empty (based on current state) and navigates the user to checkout.html?from=cart.

Shown when getCartSummary returns no items or when removeCartItem results in remaining_items_count being zero. Suppresses item lists and totals, and may offer simple navigation links (outside the scope of assigned interfaces) back to browsing pages.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a vertical, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the hero + gallery pattern: a cinematic cart header band at the top (page controller overlaid on an info/hero banner), followed by a dense, 3-column grid of cart item tiles, with the order total/checkout area as a strong, full-width band anchored below the grid. The cart feels like a horror-themed promo page where the items themselves are the gallery tiles.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a vertical, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the hero + gallery pattern: a cinematic cart header band at the top (page controller overlaid on an info/hero banner), followed by a dense, 3-column grid of cart item tiles, with the order total/checkout area as a strong, full-width band anchored below the grid. The cart feels like a horror-themed promo page where the items themselves are the gallery tiles.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The combined page controller + highlight banner form a hero zone that consumes roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full content width with minimal side margins (16–24px). Below that, the cart items occupy a 3-column grid that uses most of the remaining width with narrow gutters (8–16px). The totals/checkout panel sits below the grid as a low, wide bar that spans most of the page width, visually echoing a secondary hero strip. Vertical gaps between hero, grid, and totals follow the 48–64px section spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense feel: images and dark cards nearly fill the canvas with only slim black gutters between them. Cards for items and totals have minimal padding (16–24px) for text and controls, while outer margins and gaps stay tight so that the cart feels like an immersive, cinematic board of tiles rather than a spacious form. The black background provides breathing room without relying on large empty areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top center hero band (title and banner imagery) down into a single, clearly defined grid of cart items. Within the grid, attention moves left-to-right across the three tiles (tickets, merch, parking) in the first row, then down to the totals bar and checkout button. Inside each tile, the user’s gaze flows from item title/visual at the top, down through delivery options (where relevant), and finally to quantity and remove controls at the bottom. The totals panel and checkout button form a strong visual anchor at the bottom of the viewport, closing the flow.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a dark, cinematic canvas with a minimal header and a thick, graphic band announcing the cart, almost like a movie poster for your ‘zombie haul.’ The header controls sit at the very top, lightly overlaying or hugging the upper edge of the hero-style highlight banner that stretches almost edge-to-edge, taking up about half the initial viewport. Beneath this, the layout snaps into a tight three-column grid: three equally sized, dark-tinted item cards for tickets, merch, and parking sit side by side, each like a horror-themed poster tile with its own content. The black background peeks through as narrow vertical and horizontal gutters, framing the cards without making the layout airy. Toward the bottom, a wide, low totals panel spans the same central width as the grid, condensing pricing details into a compact strip. The primary ‘Proceed to Checkout’ button glows at the right end of this band, becoming the final focal point after scanning the items. If the cart is empty, the item grid and totals strip vanish and are replaced by a single, centered, cinematic empty-state card in the same area, preserving the hero-over-grid visual rhythm. Overall, the page feels like a horror event microsite first and a cart second, with the grid of items and the totals strip behaving like gallery tiles beneath a marquee.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits at the very top of the page and spans the full browser width, acting like a thin, dark overlay band on top of the hero area. Its content is horizontally centered within a max-width region (roughly 80–90% of the page width) with 16–24px side padding from the viewport edges. Vertically, it has a compact height of about 64–72px, using 16–24px of internal padding. The controller’s elements (e.g., back navigation on the left, page title centered or slightly left of center) align to an implicit 12-column grid and remain visually lightweight so that the cinematic banner directly beneath dominates. There is no hard separation beneath it; instead, it subtly floats over or directly abuts the top edge of the highlight banner, maintaining a continuous dark surface from header into hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and part of the normal document flow; it anchors at the top of the page and scrolls away with the content, maintaining a minimal presence so as not to compete with the banner imagery.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight banner occupies the top hero zone immediately below (or partially behind) the page controller. It spans nearly the full content width, leaving slim black margins of roughly 16–24px on the left and right before reaching the browser edges, aligning with the same center width as the rest of the page. Vertically, it takes up about 40–50% of the initial viewport height, making it a dominant cinematic strip. The banner resembles a wide, image-driven panel with dark zombie-horror artwork and neon-lit text, with key copy centralized horizontally and vertically within the band. The bottom of the banner stops cleanly, with a modest 48–64px vertical gap before the item grid begins, letting the hero feel expansive but closely connected to the cart content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero-style section that scrolls normally; it anchors the top of the cart experience but does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items group list sits directly below the highlight banner, separated by a 48–64px vertical band of pure black background. It is centered on the page and constrained to the same max-width as the banner content, effectively forming a single, gallery-like block. Within this block, a 3-column grid is established across the full width: each column approximates 1/3 of the available width (about 4 grid columns in a 12-column system), with narrow horizontal gutters of 8–16px between them. The three cart-item-row components (tickets, merch, parking) occupy this first grid row, forming a clean horizontal stripe of equal-height, dark cards. The group list ensures that the cards align horizontally at the top and share consistent vertical spacing beneath, creating a cohesive “board” of items that sits roughly in the middle of the page on larger screens. On smaller viewports, the list gracefully collapses into a single column, stacking the item cards vertically with 16–24px gaps between them while preserving the immersive dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that defines a 3-column responsive grid; no sticky or floating behavior, but it controls how item rows wrap from a 3-column layout on desktop to a single-column stack on mobile.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket cart item row occupies the leftmost column of the 3-column grid within the cart-items-group-list. It appears as a vertically oriented dark card that fills its column’s width, with no internal outer margin and 16–24px of inner padding for its text and controls. The card’s top edge aligns horizontally with the top edges of the merch and parking cards, forming a straight row. Its height grows with content but should visually match or closely approximate the height of the other item cards so the row feels balanced. Inside the card, any ticket imagery or badge sits at the upper left, with the ticket name and event details spanning the top portion. Below that, price information sits in the midsection. Toward the bottom of the card, the quantity control anchors to the bottom-left region while the remove control anchors to the bottom-right, leaving a narrow strip of dark background between them. Overall, the ticket card reads as the first tile in a gallery row, visually weighted slightly more than the others if tickets are the main item type.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying a single grid cell; it resizes in height with its content and reflows to a full-width row when the layout collapses to a single column on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The merch cart item row sits in the center column of the 3-column grid, directly to the right of the ticket card and to the left of the parking card. It shares identical outer dimensions: full column width with 8–16px gutters separating it from its neighbors, and 16–24px internal padding. Its top edge aligns perfectly with the ticket and parking cards, forming a symmetric first row of three tiles. Inside this card, merch imagery or a product thumbnail is placed at the top, slightly left of center, with the merch title and key details aligned to the left beneath or beside the image. Directly below the main product details, the merch delivery selector spans most of the card’s width, forming a horizontal band before the controls. Beneath that, near the bottom of the card, the quantity control anchors to the lower-left and the remove control to the lower-right, echoing the ticket card’s control layout to maintain consistency. This card feels like the central tile in the gallery, visually balanced between the tickets and parking tiles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the middle grid cell; it maintains equal height with neighboring cards where possible and stacks vertically between them when the grid collapses on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parking cart item row occupies the rightmost column of the 3-column grid, mirroring the ticket card on the left. It fills its assigned column width, with the same 8–16px gutter to its left and identical 16–24px inner padding. Its top aligns with the ticket and merch cards, forming the third tile in the first row. Inside, a simple parking icon or small illustrative image sits near the top-left, followed by the parking pass name and date details. Price information sits just below the textual description, and at the bottom of the card the quantity control sits anchored to the lower-left while the remove control anchors to the lower-right, with a narrow band of background between them. The card is kept visually simpler than tickets or merch to reflect its supporting role, but its physical size is equal so the row reads as a uniform gallery strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the right grid cell; it aligns with the other item cards on desktop and becomes the third vertically stacked card in the single-column mobile layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity control appears inside each cart item row card near the bottom-left corner of the card’s content area. It is positioned in the lower horizontal band of the card, sitting about 8–12px above the card’s bottom padding and aligned with the left padding edge. Horizontally, it may occupy approximately 25–30% of the card’s width, leaving room on its right side for price or spacing before the remove control on the far right. It uses a compact footprint with minimal chrome to maintain the dense, cinematic look—dark buttons or plus/minus steppers with subtle neon accents. On narrower screens, the quantity control remains in the bottom-left area but may stretch slightly wider, stacking above the remove control if necessary while still clearly anchored to the lower portion of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within each card’s bottom control row; it participates in responsive wrapping within its parent card but does not move outside of that bottom band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove item control is positioned inside each cart item row card at the lower-right corner, mirroring the quantity control. It sits along the same baseline as the quantity control, about 8–12px above the card’s bottom padding, and is right-aligned to the card’s inner right edge. The control is visually compact—either a small text link or icon-with-label—so that it doesn’t overpower the card’s imagery, but it contrasts enough (e.g., subtle neon red) to be easily discoverable against the dark background. There is a small horizontal buffer of 8–16px between the remove control and any price text to its left. On smaller screens, the remove control may wrap onto a new line beneath the quantity control, but it remains anchored to the right side of the card’s lower section to preserve the left-to-right control reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the card’s bottom-right control area; it aligns to the right and wraps beneath the quantity control in very narrow layouts while staying in the lower segment of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The merch delivery selector is located inside the merch cart item row card, occupying a full-width band in the midsection of the card. It sits directly below the merch product title and primary details, and above the quantity/remove control row. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full inner width of the card (minus 16–24px side padding), creating a noticeable yet compact horizontal stripe of interface elements across the card. Vertically, it uses about 40–56px of height, behaving like a slim, pill-based toggle or dropdown with minimal chrome to keep the card image-centric. There is 8–16px of vertical space separating it from the details above and the controls below, so it feels integrated but distinct. On smaller screens, it maintains its position in the vertical stack, still residing above the quantity control, and stretches slightly if needed to remain legible and easy to tap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the merch card’s vertical layout; it expands or contracts with available width but stays anchored between the product details and the bottom controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The totals summary panel sits below the cart items grid as a wide, low band that visually echoes a secondary hero strip. It is separated from the bottom edges of the item cards by a 48–64px vertical gap of black background, reinforcing it as a distinct section. Horizontally, it aligns with the same central column as the grid: it spans across the full width of the three columns plus their gutters, leaving 16–24px of margin from the viewport edges. The panel itself is a dark, slightly raised card with 24px of internal padding. Within it, line items (subtotal, taxes, fees) are arranged in a compact vertical stack on the left and center, taking up roughly 60–70% of the panel’s width, while the right side is reserved for the prominent Proceed to Checkout button. The overall height is modest—just enough to fit its content with a bit of breathing room—so it feels like a strong, grounded footer band to the cart. On scroll, this panel remains an important visual anchor near the bottom of the viewport but does not visually overpower the hero or the items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static in the normal flow; optionally, on larger screens it can become slightly sticky within the lower viewport (e.g., sticking near the bottom once reached) to keep totals and the checkout button in view, but always within the same centered width and without covering the cart items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Proceed to Checkout button lives inside the cart-totals-summary panel and acts as the focal point on the right side of that strip. On desktop, it is right-aligned within the panel, sitting flush with the panel’s right inner padding and vertically centered in the totals band. It occupies approximately 25–30% of the panel’s width, with generous but not oversized padding (around 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal), and stands out through a bright, neon-like color against the dark background. A small horizontal gap of 16–24px separates it from the totals text block on the left, so the button feels distinct yet connected. On smaller screens, the button shifts to a full-width configuration within the panel: it drops beneath the totals text, spanning nearly the entire width with side padding matching the panel’s, but still remains vertically close so the relationship between totals and action is clear. It forms the visual end point of the cart’s vertical narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the totals panel; its position is anchored to the right side of the panel on wider screens and reflows into a full-width block beneath the summary text on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message replaces the entire cart-items-group-list and totals-summary area when the cart contains no items. In this mode, the layout transitions from a grid of three cards to a single, centered tile that mirrors the proportions of one large gallery item. The empty-state card is centered horizontally within the same max-width content area and sits in roughly the same vertical slot where the item grid would normally begin, leaving the hero banner above intact. Vertically, the card occupies about 30–40% of the viewport height, with ample 24px internal padding. Its content is centered both horizontally and vertically within the card: a cinematic illustration or icon at the top center, a bold headline under it, and a short description and potential CTA link below. The surrounding black background extends uniformly around the card, with 48–64px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero banner and the top of this card, and another 48–64px before any footer content. This preserves the hero-over-gallery feeling, but the ‘gallery’ is now a single, dramatic empty-state tile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered: it occupies the same general space that the item grid and totals panel would use, centered in the viewport. It is static and scrolls normally, with no sticky behavior.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Review all items in your cart, choose merchandise delivery, enter purchaser and payment details, and place your order.

core_features: Load the current checkout summary (cart items, applied promo codes, merch delivery method, subtotal, taxes, fees, total) using getCheckoutSummary when the page initializes.
Display a detailed list of all cart items (tickets, merch, parking passes) including description, quantity, unit_price, and line_total from the checkout summary.
Display the current merchandise delivery method from the checkout summary and allow the user to switch between available options by calling setCartMerchDeliveryMethod, updating totals if needed.
Collect purchaser contact details (name, email, phone) and required acknowledgments (accept_terms, accept_safety_waiver) as part of the checkout form.
Collect payment details, including payment_method and payment_token provided by an integrated payment solution, and pass them into placeOrder.
Create the final order and process payment by calling placeOrder with all collected form data, then handle success, pending, or failure states and show an appropriate confirmation or error message.
user_workflows:
Standard checkout from cart (from=cart):
1. User navigates to checkout.html?from=cart from cart.html.
2. On page load, the page reads the from URL parameter and stores that value in page state for contextual messaging.
3. The checkout-summary-loader component calls getCheckoutSummary.
4. When getCheckoutSummary returns, the items list, promo codes, merch_delivery_method, and totals are displayed.
5. User reviews line items in checkout-items-list.
6. User optionally adjusts the merch delivery method in merch-delivery-selector; this calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod and then re-triggers getCheckoutSummary to refresh totals.
7. User fills in purchaser-details-form (name, email, phone) and checks the required terms and safety waiver boxes.
8. User selects a payment method and completes payment details in payment-details-form; an external payment widget (if used) returns a payment_token and a payment_method identifier to the form.
9. User clicks the Place Order button in checkout-submit-controller.
10. The controller validates that required fields are filled, required acknowledgments are checked, and a payment_token exists; if validation fails, it shows inline errors and prevents submission.
11. If validation passes, the controller calls placeOrder with purchaser details, merch_delivery_method, shipping_address (if required by delivery method), billing_address, payment_method, payment_token, accept_terms, and accept_safety_waiver.
12. While placeOrder is in progress, the page shows a submitting state and disables form resubmission.
13. On successful response (success=true), the page stores order_id and order_summary in page state and transitions to a confirmation view on the same page (or redirects to a dedicated confirmation page using order_id in the URL).
14. If placeOrder returns success=false or payment_status indicates a failure, the page shows an error message and allows the user to retry after adjusting details.

Direct checkout after booking an attraction (from=attraction):
1. User completes a booking flow on attraction.html and is navigated directly to checkout.html?from=attraction with the tickets already in the cart.
2. On page load, the page reads from=attraction and adjusts contextual text (e.g., indicating they are finishing an attraction booking) without changing functionality.
3. checkout-summary-loader calls getCheckoutSummary and renders the items, which will include the newly booked attraction tickets.
4. User verifies attraction name(s), timeslot(s), ticket type(s), and quantities in checkout-items-list.
5. User optionally adds merch to cart via prior steps (not on this page); the checkout summary will already reflect any added merch.
6. User proceeds with purchaser-details-form, merch-delivery-selector (if merch exists), payment-details-form, and checkout-submit-controller exactly as in the standard checkout workflow.
7. On successful placeOrder, the confirmation view ensures that the user can see their attraction tickets as part of the order summary.

Changing merchandise delivery method during checkout:
1. User arrives on checkout.html and getCheckoutSummary returns a merch_delivery_method and one or more merch items.
2. merch-delivery-selector shows the currently selected merch_delivery_method and any associated instructions.
3. User selects a different delivery method (e.g., choosing an option different from the one returned in the summary).
4. The onChange handler calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod with the newly selected delivery_method value.
5. While setCartMerchDeliveryMethod is in progress, the selector is temporarily disabled or shows a loading indicator.
6. On success=true, the selector updates to reflect the confirmed merch_delivery_method from the response.
7. The component then triggers getCheckoutSummary again to update subtotal, taxes, fees, and total if they depend on the delivery method.
8. If setCartMerchDeliveryMethod returns success=false, the selector reverts to the last known method and displays the message from the response.

Handling validation and payment errors during order placement:
1. User fills in purchaser-details-form and payment-details-form and clicks Place Order.
2. checkout-submit-controller runs client-side validation: required fields (name, email, email format, payment_token presence, required checkboxes) must be satisfied.
3. If validation fails, the controller sets error flags in page state; the relevant fields display inline error messages and focus is moved to the first invalid field.
4. If validation passes, placeOrder is called. The page enters a placing-order state.
5. If placeOrder returns success=true and payment_status='paid', the page moves to a success/confirmation state.
6. If placeOrder returns success=true but payment_status is not 'paid' (e.g., 'pending'), the confirmation state shows that payment is pending and may instruct the user to check their email for updates.
7. If placeOrder returns success=false or payment_status='failed', the page shows the message provided and remains on the checkout form, allowing the user to correct issues (e.g., re-enter payment, choose a different payment method) and submit again.
8. The user can reattempt placing the order, which re-runs validation and calls placeOrder again.
interactions: Page load: onMount, read the from URL parameter and store it in page state; then trigger getCheckoutSummary. While the summary is loading, show a loading state and disable the submit button.

Item review: users can scroll and view each cart item in checkout-items-list. No editing of quantities occurs on this page; if they need to adjust items, they follow a standard navigation link back to cart.html (navigation, not an API call).

Merch delivery selection: in merch-delivery-selector, user selects a delivery option (e.g., via radio buttons). The onChange event calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod with the selected delivery_method and, upon success, refreshes the checkout summary via getCheckoutSummary.

Purchaser details input: in purchaser-details-form, user types into name, email, and phone fields. onChange events update local form state. Blur events can trigger basic validation (e.g., email format).

Address input (if required): when merch_delivery_method requires shipping, shipping address fields are displayed. onChange events update a local representation of the address, which is combined into the shipping_address string that is passed to placeOrder. Billing address fields are either shown separately or an option allows copying the shipping address into billing_address.
Acknowledgment checkboxes: in purchaser-details-form, user must check accept_terms and accept_safety_waiver checkboxes. onChange updates local flags used in validation before calling placeOrder.

Payment method selection: in payment-details-form, user selects a payment_method (e.g., provided by a payment widget). onChange updates the selected payment_method in form state. The payment widget, on successful completion, emits a payment_token captured by an onPaymentAuthorized handler, which stores the token in form state.

Order submission: in checkout-submit-controller, clicking the Place Order button triggers onSubmitOrder. This handler aggregates data from purchaser-details-form, merch-delivery-selector, and payment-details-form, validates it, and if valid, calls placeOrder. The button is disabled while placeOrder is in progress.

Error handling: if getCheckoutSummary or setCartMerchDeliveryMethod encounters an error, an error state is set and an inline error message is displayed. Users can retry by reloading the page (re-triggering getCheckoutSummary) or by reselecting the delivery method.

Confirmation view: after a successful placeOrder, the confirmation state renders order_summary items and totals from the response. Users can follow a navigation link to another page (e.g., a dedicated order confirmation or tickets page) using the returned order_id as a URL parameter, but this navigation itself does not call any additional interfaces from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on the `from` URL parameter and interface responses to manage state. On initialization, the page reads `from` from the query string (allowed values include 'cart' and 'attraction'). This value is stored in in-memory page state and used only to adjust contextual messaging, such as indicating whether the user is finishing a cart checkout or an attraction booking; it does not change data loading logic or interface calls. After reading `from`, the page triggers `getCheckoutSummary`. The result of `getCheckoutSummary` populates in-memory state for `cart_id`, `items`, `promo_codes`, `merch_delivery_method`, `subtotal`, `taxes`, `fees`, and `total`. This state is shared with components that display items and totals, and with components that need the current `merch_delivery_method`. When the user changes the merchandise delivery method, the newly selected value is stored in temporary UI state and immediately sent to `setCartMerchDeliveryMethod`. On a successful response, the confirmed `merch_delivery_method` from the response replaces any temporary value and `getCheckoutSummary` is called again; on failure, the method in UI is reverted to the last successful value and an error state is shown. Form fields for purchaser details, addresses, acknowledgments, and payment details are held in client-side state only and are not persisted in URL parameters or localStorage. Before calling `placeOrder`, the controller composes the parameters from this state (including `merch_delivery_method` and any combined address strings). After `placeOrder`, an in-memory `checkoutResult` state holds `success`, `order_id`, `payment_status`, `fulfillment_status`, `message`, and `order_summary`. If `success` is true, the page transitions to a confirmation state and optionally uses `order_id` to build navigation URLs to other pages (e.g., confirmation.html?order_id=...). All such navigation uses href-based URLs with query parameters, not additional API calls. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; all state is ephemeral and re-derived from URL parameters and interfaces on each page load.

components.functionality: On page mount, reads the `from` URL parameter into page state, then calls getCheckoutSummary to fetch the current checkout summary. It stores the returned cart_id, items, merch_delivery_method, promo_codes, subtotal, taxes, fees, and total in shared page state for other components to consume. It manages loading and error flags, which control whether the checkout form is enabled and whether error messages are displayed.

Displays the list of items from the checkout summary. For each item, it shows the item_type, description, quantity, unit_price, and line_total exactly as returned by getCheckoutSummary. It also shows any applied promo_codes at the summary level. This component is read-only: users cannot change quantities here; instead, a standard navigation link back to the cart is provided if the user needs to modify items.

Allows the user to view and modify the merchandise delivery method for the current cart. It initializes its selected value from the merch_delivery_method returned by getCheckoutSummary. When the user selects a different option, it calls setCartMerchDeliveryMethod with the chosen delivery_method. On success, it updates the selected method in page state and triggers a summary refresh via getCheckoutSummary so totals can be recalculated. On failure, it reverts to the previous method and shows the error message from the setCartMerchDeliveryMethod response. It also controls whether shipping address inputs are shown (for ship_to_address) or hidden (for pick_up_at_event_venue) in the purchaser-details-form.

Collects purchaser information and required acknowledgments. It provides input fields for purchaser_name, purchaser_email, and purchaser_phone, and maintains their values in local form state. Based on the merch_delivery_method from shared state, it conditionally displays shipping address fields and an optional billing address section. The shipping_address and billing_address inputs are captured as field groups and then composed into string values that match the placeOrder parameters. The form also shows checkboxes for accept_terms and accept_safety_waiver; these must be checked before submission. The component exposes its current values and field-level validation results to the checkout-submit-controller.

Manages payment selection and captures the payment_token provided by an integrated payment provider. It allows the user to select a payment_method option and triggers the external provider (e.g., a card entry widget or digital wallet). When the payment provider reports a successful authorization or tokenization, the component stores the resulting payment_token and associated payment_method string in local state. It exposes these values and any validation status (e.g., whether a valid token is present) to the checkout-submit-controller.

Displays up-to-date monetary totals for the checkout, including subtotal, taxes, fees, and total, using the values from getCheckoutSummary while in the pre-order state and the values from the placeOrder response's order_summary after a successful order. It listens for summary refresh events (e.g., after a delivery method change) and for order placement results to update its display accordingly.

Coordinates form submission and order placement. When the user clicks the Place Order button, it gathers values from purchaser-details-form (purchaser_name, purchaser_email, purchaser_phone, shipping_address, billing_address, accept_terms, accept_safety_waiver), merch-delivery-selector (merch_delivery_method), and payment-details-form (payment_method, payment_token). It runs combined validation across these inputs. If validation fails, it sets error states that individual components use to display inline error messages. If validation passes, it calls placeOrder with all required parameters. While placeOrder is in progress, it sets a placing-order flag to disable inputs and the submit button. After placeOrder resolves, it updates global checkoutResult state and triggers either a transition to a confirmation view (on success) or error messaging (on failure). It can also optionally trigger a navigation to a dedicated confirmation page using the returned order_id as a URL parameter.

Displays a short contextual message based on the `from` URL parameter and current checkout state. If from='cart', it indicates the user is checking out items from their cart; if from='attraction', it indicates the user is completing an attraction booking. If `from` is missing or unrecognized, it falls back to a generic checkout message. It updates its content only on initial load based on the URL parameter and the presence of items returned by getCheckoutSummary.

Renders after a successful placeOrder call. It uses the checkoutResult state to display a confirmation message, referencing payment_status, fulfillment_status, order_id, and order_summary (items and totals). It replaces or hides the main checkout form components when active. It can present a navigation link pointing to a dedicated order or tickets page, using order_id in the query string, but does not invoke any additional interfaces from this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom arrangement: a dominant, full‑width contextual hero at the top (the checkout context message) followed by a dense, three‑column grid that functions as the main checkout workspace. Under the hero, interactive components are visually grouped into two horizontal ‘bands’ of three cards, echoing the mockup’s 3×2 image gallery: the first band focuses on reviewing items and starting the checkout (cart items, delivery, payment), and the second band focuses on completing details and confirming (purchaser details, order total, submit). Loading and confirmation states sit above or replace this structure rather than introducing new layout patterns.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom arrangement: a dominant, full‑width contextual hero at the top (the checkout context message) followed by a dense, three‑column grid that functions as the main checkout workspace. Under the hero, interactive components are visually grouped into two horizontal ‘bands’ of three cards, echoing the mockup’s 3×2 image gallery: the first band focuses on reviewing items and starting the checkout (cart items, delivery, payment), and the second band focuses on completing details and confirming (purchaser details, order total, submit). Loading and confirmation states sit above or replace this structure rather than introducing new layout patterns.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The checkout context hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full content width (12 columns), with small lateral margins (about 5–8% page width total) to prioritize immersive imagery. Below it, the three‑column grid fills the remaining height with narrow black gutters (8–16px) between columns and rows. Each column in the grid approximates 4 of 12 columns (~30–32% width), aligned to the implicit 12‑column system. Vertical spacing between the hero and the grid is modest (around 48px), and spacing between the two horizontal bands of cards within the grid is about 32px. Within cards that contain forms or controls, padding is 16–24px; image‑dominant or purely structural panels lean toward edge‑to‑edge content within their card frames.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately dense feel: the dark background fills the browser, and the main area is dominated by large, dark cards with minimal whitespace around them, similar to a gallery of image tiles. Narrow gutters of pure black separate components, acting as frames rather than generous whitespace. Forms are compact but readable, with concise vertical spacing between fields (8–16px), so the page feels rich and immersive without being cluttered. The emphasis remains on an intense, cinematic checkout experience with minimal visual chrome and clear, boxed sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye is first captured by the high‑contrast hero at the top center (checkout context message with dramatic event imagery). From there, attention moves straight down into the first horizontal band of the three‑column grid. The user scans left‑to‑right: starting with the cart items list, then the delivery selector, then the payment form. Scrolling or scanning slightly further down reveals the second band, again read left‑to‑right: purchaser details, then the order total summary, then the final submit controls. Loader and confirmation views appear centered and full‑width, intercepting this flow while active but keeping the top‑to‑bottom, then left‑to‑right scanning pattern intact.

layout.overall_description: The checkout page reads like a cinematic, horror‑themed landing experience that has been adapted into a functional, high‑density checkout grid. At the very top, a full‑width, dark hero panel stretches nearly edge to edge, with a neon‑lit event title and a brief checkout context message floating over a gritty, zombie‑themed background. This hero claims the upper half of the first viewport, setting mood more than behaving like a traditional UI header. Beneath it, after a slim band of solid black, the layout snaps into a structured three‑column grid reminiscent of a 3×2 image gallery. Each column houses one or more stacked checkout cards framed by narrow black gutters. The top row presents three key panels side by side: the cart items list at left, delivery options in the center, and payment details at right. Slightly below, a second row of three panels aligns visually with the first row: the purchaser details extend under the middle of the page, the order total summary sits beneath and slightly overlapping the payment band on the right, and the submit controls anchor themselves at the lower right edge, tightly coupled to the total. Throughout, the black background bleeds between all components, with subtle neon highlights and edge treatments echoing the horror event branding. When data loads or confirmation appears, a full‑viewport, darkened overlay or a replacement result panel occupies the same core canvas, maintaining the same immersive, centered feeling while simplifying the layout to a single focal block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full‑viewport overlay that temporarily sits above the hero and the three‑column grid. It covers the entire visible page from the very top edge of the browser to the bottom, extending full width with a semi‑opaque black veil over the existing content so the underlying cinematic imagery is barely visible. At the exact center of the viewport, a compact loading indicator (spinner or pulsing icon) and a short status line are placed, both horizontally and vertically centered, occupying roughly 15–20% of the viewport width. The loader does not alter the underlying layout; instead, it simply floats above it, keeping the user’s focus locked in the center while the rest of the page remains dimmed and static beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full‑viewport overlay that covers all other components while active; content centered in the middle of the screen and unaffected by page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The items list card occupies the left third of the main checkout grid directly beneath the hero. It is aligned to the left content margin and spans approximately 4 of 12 grid columns (about 30–32% of the content width), with a narrow black gutter (8–16px) separating it from the adjacent middle column. Vertically, its top edge sits about 48px below the bottom of the hero panel, forming the left tile of the top gallery row. The card has minimal outer margin and a dark, slightly elevated surface that feels like a framed still from the event, with 16–24px padding inside for text and controls. The items list stretches downward to roughly align with the bottom edges of the cards in the neighboring columns; if there are many items, the interior of this card becomes scrollable while the card height generally matches or slightly exceeds the combined height of the cards in the middle and right columns’ first band. This makes the left column feel like a tall, continuous pillar of content anchoring the grid. The list’s content is arranged top‑to‑bottom inside the card, with compact vertical spacing so that multiple items can fit without breaking the dense gallery feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three‑column grid; the card remains in place as the user scrolls the page, while its interior list area can scroll independently if content exceeds the visible height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The merch delivery selector card sits in the middle column, forming the central tile of the top gallery row directly beside the items list. It aligns horizontally so that its top edge is level with the top of the cart items card and the payment card on the right, creating a clean horizontal band of three aligned cards. It spans about 4 of 12 grid columns, with equal‑sized black gutters on both left and right separating it from the neighboring columns. The card’s height is moderate—roughly one‑third to one‑half the height of the items list card—so it feels like a compact yet substantial panel for choosing delivery options. Inside, 16–24px padding is used, and controls are stacked vertically with 8–16px gaps between radio options or dropdowns, maintaining a tight but legible layout. Below this card, a vertical gap of about 24–32px of black background separates it from the purchaser details form card, visually echoing the gap between gallery rows in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the middle column; scrolls with the page as part of the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchaser details form sits directly beneath the merch delivery selector in the middle column, forming the central tile of the second gallery row. Its left and right edges line up exactly with the delivery card above, maintaining a strict vertical column rhythm. The space between the delivery selector and this form is a narrow horizontal band of black (roughly 24–32px), mirroring the row separation from the mockup. The card spans the same 4 of 12 columns in width but is taller, occupying roughly the lower half of the visible grid height so that its bottom edge aligns closely with the base of the order total and submit cluster on the right. Inside, the form fields are stacked top‑to‑bottom with tight but consistent spacing (8–16px between fields), making the card feel dense yet organized. The header of the card (e.g., a title like “Purchaser Details”) sits near the top with only a small inset (16–24px padding) so that the panel still feels image‑like and framed, fitting the gallery aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked card in the middle column; scrolls with the page and expands vertically based on content, contributing to the second row in the three‑column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment details form occupies the right column’s upper position, forming the right‑most tile of the top gallery row. It sits to the right of the delivery selector card, with a narrow black gutter (8–16px) between them, and its top edge is aligned horizontally with the tops of both the items list and delivery selector cards. The card spans approximately 4 of 12 grid columns, matching the width of the other two columns to reinforce the clean three‑column structure. Vertically, this card is one of the taller panels in the top row, occupying about half to two‑thirds of the visible grid height to accommodate multiple input fields such as card number, expiry, and security data. Internally, it uses 16–24px padding and a stacked vertical form layout, with field groups separated by 8–16px of space. The bottom edge of this card is positioned slightly above the top of the order total summary card below, leaving a gap of about 24–32px of pure black that visually echoes the gallery row separation and keeps the right column’s banding consistent with the middle column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right column; scrolls with the page. On larger viewports, its height is tuned so that, combined with the order total and submit cluster below, it visually matches the vertical weight of the other columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order total summary card is positioned in the right column directly beneath the payment details form, forming the right‑most tile of the second gallery row. It aligns precisely with the right edge of the payment form and shares the same column width (about 4 of 12 grid columns). The gap above it is a narrow horizontal band of black (around 24–32px), matching the vertical separation between the top and bottom rows in the neighboring columns. This card occupies a compact vertical slice—roughly one‑quarter to one‑third of the column height—so it feels like a dense, high‑impact panel. Inside, totals and line items are stacked with tight spacing (4–8px between lines), and the overall card padding remains at 16–24px. The order total figure is visually emphasized and placed near the bottom of the card so that the submit controls, sitting just beneath in their own card or attached strip, feel naturally connected. Viewed as a whole, the payment card above and the total summary plus submit controls below read like a two‑frame vertical sequence in the right column of the gallery grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, but on larger screens it can behave as a sticky block within its column—remaining visible as the user scrolls longer forms in the middle column—while respecting top and bottom offsets so it does not overlap the hero or footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit controller is tightly coupled to the order total summary at the bottom of the right column, forming the lowest card in the grid’s right‑hand side. It either appears as a separate, short card directly beneath the order total summary with a very small gap (about 16px) of black between them, or as a visually distinct bottom band attached to the total summary card, but in either case its left and right edges align exactly with the total summary card above. This panel occupies a relatively shallow height—roughly the height of one or two buttons plus 16–24px padding—so that it feels like a narrow, high‑contrast control strip anchoring the lower right corner of the grid. The primary action button (e.g., “Place Order”) dominates the width of the card, centered or left‑aligned within the column, with 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding for the button itself, maintaining a compact but tappable size. Its vertical position visually anchors the entire checkout experience; when you look at the grid from top to bottom, this controller is the final, right‑aligned focal point, mirroring how the last thumbnail in a gallery row pulls the eye at the end of a scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card or attached strip at the bottom of the right column; scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it may become full‑width and docked closer to the bottom of the viewport for easier access, but it maintains its role as the final call‑to‑action anchor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout context message is presented as a full‑width hero band at the very top of the page, before any checkout controls. It spans the entire content area from the left to the right content margins (approximately 12 of 12 columns, with only small outer margins), and its height takes up roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport. This panel features a dark, cinematic background image related to the zombie event, with a neon or high‑contrast title and brief explanatory text centered horizontally and vertically within the hero. There is no visible card boundary; instead, the hero is framed by the page edges and a clean black transition beneath it. At the bottom of the hero, the imagery softly fades into solid black, and after about 48px of pure black background, the three‑column grid of checkout content begins. This hero functions as the equivalent of the mockup’s main image banner, setting mood and context without crowding the interface below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full‑width top section that stays at the top of the document; it scrolls off the screen as the user moves down into the checkout grid and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful checkout, the confirmation view replaces the three‑column grid area with a simpler, cinematic result panel. The hero at the top remains, preserving the initial mood, but the area that previously contained the grid (from roughly 48px below the hero downward) is now occupied by a single, centered confirmation card or band. This confirmation card spans a large portion of the width—about 8–10 of 12 grid columns, centered—leaving slightly larger black margins on the left and right than the grid did, so it feels like a spotlighted final frame. The card’s height covers a substantial portion of the viewport (roughly 50–70% of the height beneath the hero), with the primary success message and order summary centered vertically within it.
