The dense grid of cards is gone; instead, the eye is drawn to a single, bold block of content that mirrors the hero’s cinematic tone. Any key details (order number, brief item summary, follow‑up links) are stacked top‑to‑bottom in this card with 16–24px padding and controlled vertical spacing (8–16px) between lines, making the layout feel deliberate and calm after the complexity of the checkout. The result is analogous to showing a single feature image after navigating a gallery: one clear focal piece that confirms completion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the main checkout grid section beneath the hero. Static in the flow; the user can scroll if the confirmation content extends beyond the viewport, but the overall structure remains a single, prominent center card rather than reintroducing columns.

name: Shop / Merch Listing
title: Shop
description: Browse and filter zombie-themed merchandise by category, price, rating, tags, and search, then open product detail pages from product cards.

core_features: Load merch categories and expose them as selectable navigation to filter the listing by category.
Load available filter options (price range, rating thresholds, tags) and use them to configure interactive filters.
Render a search form that narrows the merch listing based on a text query.
Render a product listing grid using the current category, filters, search query, and sort order by calling the merch listing interface.
Allow users to adjust price, rating, and tag filters, and update both the URL parameters and product results accordingly.
Allow users to change sort order (e.g., by price or rating) and update URL parameters and product results.
Allow users to click a product card to open the product detail page with the current filter/search context passed as URL parameters.
Allow users to access the cart page via the header cart icon without altering the current shop filter state.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on the shop with default or preselected category
1. User navigates to shop.html (with or without query parameters).
2. The page controller reads URL parameters (category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort).
3. The page calls getMerchCategories to retrieve all categories.
4. The active category is determined: if a category param is present, it is used; otherwise a default category code is chosen according to business rules.
5. The page calls getMerchFilterOptions to obtain available ranges/options for price, rating, and tags.
6. The page normalizes current filter state (ensuring values are within the allowed ranges/options) and updates the URL if needed.
7. The page calls listMerchProducts with the current category_code and other filter parameters.
8. The product grid displays the resulting merchandise as product cards, each showing key information (image, name, price, rating, availability status).
9. The user can scroll and review items, then click any card to navigate to the corresponding product detail page.

Workflow: Filtering by price and rating within a category
1. User is on shop.html with an active category and initial results already loaded.
2. User opens the filter sidebar and adjusts the maximum price control.
3. The filter sidebar emits a filter-change event with a new max_price value.
4. The page controller updates the URL parameter max_price and the internal current filter state.
5. The page controller calls listMerchProducts again with the updated max_price and other current parameters.
6. The product grid re-renders to show only products whose price is within the new maximum.
7. User then selects a minimum rating option (e.g., a specific rating threshold) in the filter sidebar.
8. The filter sidebar emits a filter-change event with a new min_rating value.
9. The page controller updates the URL (min_rating parameter) and internal state, then calls listMerchProducts with the new min_rating.
10. The product grid re-renders to show only products meeting the rating threshold, allowing the user to identify suitable items for a bundle of merch to purchase later.

Workflow: Searching within the merch shop
1. User focuses the search input in the search form and enters a search term.
2. User submits the search form.
3. The search-form component prevents full page reload, then emits a search-submit event with the search_query value.
4. The page controller updates the URL search_query parameter, preserves other current filters (category, max_price, min_rating, tags, sort), and pushes a new URL state.
5. The page controller calls listMerchProducts with the updated search_query and other filter parameters.
6. The product grid re-renders to show only products whose data matches the search query.
7. The user can refine by adjusting filters, which continue to combine with the search query when calling listMerchProducts.

Workflow: Selecting tags to refine merch
1. User opens the filter sidebar and sees available tag options populated from getMerchFilterOptions.
2. User selects or deselects one or more tags.
3. The filter sidebar builds a comma-separated tags string from the selected options and emits a filter-change event containing this string.
4. The page controller updates the URL tags parameter and the internal current filter state.
5. The page controller calls listMerchProducts with the updated tags parameter in addition to category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, and sort.
6. The product grid re-renders to show only merch items associated with the selected tags.

Workflow: Changing sort order for merch results
1. User opens the sort control (e.g., a dropdown) and selects a sort option.
2. The sort-control component emits a sort-change event with the selected sort value.
3. The page controller updates the URL sort parameter and the internal current sort state, leaving the other URL parameters unchanged.
4. The page controller calls listMerchProducts with the new sort parameter and existing category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, and tags.
5. The product grid re-renders to show the same set of products ordered according to the chosen sort rule.

Workflow: Navigating to product details with filter context
1. From the product grid, the user clicks a product card.
2. The product-card component captures the click and reads the current filter context (category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort) from the page controller.
3. The browser navigates to product.html, passing the clicked product’s product_id as productId and including from=shop plus the current filters as URL parameters.
4. On product.html, the product detail page can use these parameters (if implemented) to render a back-link to the shop that restores the previous filtered view, supporting bundle creation by quickly returning to the same filtered merch list.

Workflow: Using deep links and browser navigation
1. A user receives or bookmarks a URL like shop.html with specific query parameters representing a filtered view.
2. When they open this URL, the page controller parses all parameters, initializes current filter state, and calls getMerchCategories, getMerchFilterOptions, and listMerchProducts accordingly.
3. The UI (category navigation, filters, search, sort control) all reflect the values from the URL.
4. As the user changes filters and search, each change updates the URL using the History API.
5. The user clicks the browser back button to revert to a previous filter state.
6. The page controller listens to URL changes (popstate), re-reads parameters, and re-calls listMerchProducts so that the product grid and filter controls are always synchronized with the URL.

Workflow: Accessing the cart from the shop
1. While viewing products on shop.html, the user clicks the cart icon in the header.
2. The header component navigates to cart.html without altering the current shop URL or state.
3. If the user later returns to shop.html (e.g., via browser back or a navigation link), the previous filters and search can be restored via the URL parameters if the user’s navigation preserved them.
interactions: Clicking a category in the category navigation updates the active category, updates the category URL parameter, resets or retains other filters according to business rules, and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Submitting the search form updates the search_query URL parameter, preserves other filters, and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Changing the maximum price filter updates the max_price URL parameter and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Changing the minimum rating filter updates the min_rating URL parameter and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Selecting or deselecting tag filters updates the tags URL parameter (as a comma-separated list) and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Changing the sort selection updates the sort URL parameter and triggers a new call to listMerchProducts.
Clicking a product card navigates to product.html with productId and current filter/search context as URL parameters.
Clicking the cart icon in the header navigates to cart.html.
Using the browser back/forward buttons changes the URL; the page controller responds by syncing the internal filter state and re-calling listMerchProducts so the UI matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page controller reads URL parameters: category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, and sort. It calls getMerchCategories to retrieve all categories and determines the active category: if category is present in the URL and matches a known category code, that category is used; otherwise, a default category code is selected according to business rules (for example, a featured category such as one representing apparel). getMerchFilterOptions is called to obtain price_min, price_max, rating_options, and tag_options. The controller validates any existing URL filter parameters against these options and may normalize them (for example, clamping max_price to within price_min/price_max, or ensuring min_rating is one of the allowed rating_options). When normalized values differ from those in the URL, the controller updates the URL (e.g., via replaceState) to keep the URL authoritative. The controller maintains an internal current_filters object mirroring the URL parameters: {category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort}. This object is passed to child components (category navigation, search form, filter sidebar, sort control, product grid). Whenever a user interaction changes filters, search, or sort, the corresponding component emits an event with the new value(s). The controller updates current_filters and writes the changes back to the URL query string (e.g., via pushState) so the URL remains the single persistent state source. After every change to current_filters or category, the controller calls listMerchProducts with parameters derived directly from current_filters: category_code = current_filters.category, max_price = current_filters.max_price, min_rating = current_filters.min_rating, search_query = current_filters.search_query, tags = current_filters.tags, sort = current_filters.sort. The products returned from listMerchProducts are bound to the product-grid component. The controller also listens to browser navigation events (such as popstate). When the URL changes due to back/forward, it re-reads the URL parameters, rebuilds current_filters, re-syncs the child components' props, and calls listMerchProducts again to ensure the listing always corresponds to the URL state.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter parsing, filter state management, and communication between all shop components. It is responsible for calling getMerchCategories, getMerchFilterOptions, and listMerchProducts using the current URL-derived state, and for updating the URL when any filter or search parameter changes.

Displays the list of merch categories retrieved from getMerchCategories as selectable category tabs or buttons. Highlights the active category and notifies the page controller when the user selects a different category.
Provides a text input for searching within merch. Initializes the input value from the search_query URL parameter and emits an event when the user submits the form.

Renders interactive filters based on getMerchFilterOptions and current filter state: a control for maximum price bounded by the price_min/price_max values, a set of selectable minimum rating options derived from rating_options, and a set of selectable tags derived from tag_options. It reads initial values from URL-driven current_filters and emits consolidated filter-change events when any filter is updated.

Allows the user to choose how merch products are ordered (for example, by price or rating). Initializes its selected value from the sort URL parameter and emits sort-change events when the user selects a different sort option.

Displays the list of merch products returned from listMerchProducts as a grid or list of product-card components. It receives the array of products and renders each with name, image_url, price, rating, rating_count, tags, and status. It passes click events on product cards back to the page controller so navigation can include the current filter context.

Represents a single merch product item within the grid. It renders key fields such as name, price, rating, and availability status, and displays the product image if available. When clicked, it triggers navigation to product.html, passing the product’s product_id as productId along with from=shop and the current category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, and sort values from the page controller.
Represents the cart icon in the page header. When activated, it navigates the user to cart.html without changing the current shop filters or search parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page mirrors the mockup’s simple vertical stack: a dominant, cinematic hero at the top controlled by the shop-page-controller, followed by a dense, 3-column product grid that visually echoes the photo gallery. All shop controls (category tabs, search, sort, filter trigger) are grouped in a thin control band immediately below the hero, so the page still reads as: HERO → CONTROL STRIP → GRID, with no permanent sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page mirrors the mockup’s simple vertical stack: a dominant, cinematic hero at the top controlled by the shop-page-controller, followed by a dense, 3-column product grid that visually echoes the photo gallery. All shop controls (category tabs, search, sort, filter trigger) are grouped in a thin control band immediately below the hero, so the page still reads as: HERO → CONTROL STRIP → GRID, with no permanent sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full content width (within a modest max-width and small side margins). Below it, a compact control band (category tabs + search/sort/filter) uses about 10–15% of the initial viewport height. The remaining vertical space is devoted almost entirely to the 3-column product grid. Gutters around the main content are small (about 24px from browser edge and 8–16px between cards), letting imagery dominate against a continuous black backdrop.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense: imagery fills most of the canvas with thin black gutters in between. Controls sit close to each other in a tight band to minimize interruption of the cinematic feel. Internal padding for text areas on cards follows the 16–24px range, while pure image areas have effectively zero padding, relying on gutters for separation. Section gaps (hero to controls, controls to grid) are kept modest (~32–48px) to maintain immersion rather than spacious minimalism.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the bright, neon-tinted hero center. From there it drops directly down to the control band: first scanning the category tabs horizontally, then moving slightly down and left-to-right across search, filter, and sort controls. Once a filter or search is considered, attention continues down into the product grid, scanning each row left-to-right across three equally weighted product cards before moving down to the next row. Overall, the flow is top-to-bottom then left-to-right, with the 3-column rhythm reinforcing exploration of individual items.

layout.overall_description: The shop page opens on a dark, cinematic hero that fills the top half of the screen, a wide, neon-lit scene of zombies and merch that bleeds almost edge to edge with only slim black margins. A subtle, translucent header bar floats over the very top, with the cart link glowing at the right edge. As you scroll your gaze downward, the hero fades into pure black, and a tight control band emerges: centered category tabs form a horizontal row of pill-shaped filters, just above a single line where the search field stretches from the left and meets the sort and filter controls at the right. Below this band, the main content erupts into a dense 3-column grid of product cards, each card a framed, high-contrast image with a shallow black caption bar for title, price, and rating. Narrow vertical and horizontal gutters of black hold the grid together like a film contact sheet. The entire page feels like an image-driven horror portfolio that just happens to be shoppable: hero at the top, control band as a thin seam, and an immersive wall of merchandise thumbnails below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-page-controller defines the overall canvas as a dark, full-height scrolling page. It centers all content within a max-width of roughly 1200–1440px, with small left and right margins of about 24px so imagery nearly touches the viewport edges on large screens. At the very top it reserves space for a translucent header bar, then immediately below spans a full-width hero section that occupies about 40–50% of the initial viewport height. The hero stretches across all 12 implicit columns, with no visible card borders, creating a single cinematic panel. Directly beneath the hero, the controller creates a slim, full-width control band for the category-navigation and the row containing the merch-search-form, sort-control, and filter-sidebar trigger zone. Below that, it allocates the remainder of the vertical space to the product-grid, which fills the width of the content area and scrolls as a continuous 3-column layout. Section boundaries (hero → controls → grid) are separated by modest 32–48px vertical gaps of pure black background to match the immersive but slightly segmented feeling of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main scroll container for the page content. The hero scrolls away normally; the control band can optionally become sticky at the top of the content area after the hero scrolls off-screen, but by default everything behaves as standard vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-navigation sits immediately below the hero, centered horizontally within the content width. It appears as a single horizontal row of pill-shaped category tabs, visually echoing the rhythm of a 3-column grid but compressed into one band. The tabs span roughly 60–70% of the available width, leaving soft black space on either side so they feel like a deliberate strip rather than edge-to-edge controls. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of black space between the bottom of the hero and the top of the tabs, and about 16–24px between the tabs and the control row below (search/sort/filter). Tabs are evenly spaced with small 8–12px gaps, creating a compact but readable navigation strip that does not compete with the hero or the grid. On smaller screens, the row can become horizontally scrollable, but it remains directly under the hero so the flow stays: hero image → category choices → product results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally scrolls with the page. In an enhanced behavior, the entire control band (category-navigation + search/sort/filter row) can become sticky as a block once the hero scrolls out of view, anchoring it at the top edge of the viewport while the product-grid scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The merch-search-form is placed in the main control row directly under the category-navigation. It is left-aligned within the content area and occupies roughly 55–65% of the width on desktop, extending across the left two-thirds of the 12-column grid. The search field itself is a wide, low-profile bar with a dark, slightly translucent background, matching the gritty aesthetic while remaining legible. To its right, separated by a 16–24px horizontal gap, sit the sort-control and the filter trigger (which belongs to the filter-sidebar). This combined row sits about 16–24px below the category tabs, forming a tight band of controls before the product-grid begins. Vertically, the search field height is around 48px, with internal padding aligned to the 8–16px scale. The result is that the search form feels like a single, dominant utility control in the top-left of the shopping area, but still visually subordinate to the hero above and balanced by the smaller sort/filter controls on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in-line as part of the control band. If the band is made sticky, the search form travels with it and stays docked at the top of the viewport while the grid content scrolls beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar is not a permanent, left-rail sidebar; instead, it behaves as an off-canvas panel to preserve the mockup’s full-width, grid-centric layout. Its presence in the default layout is indicated by a compact 'Filter' control located in the same row as the merch-search-form and sort-control, typically to the right of or just left of the sort dropdown, all aligned along the right third of the control band. When activated, the filter-sidebar slides in from the left edge of the viewport and overlays the product-grid, occupying roughly 280–320px in width (about 3–4 of the 12 columns) and extending from just under the header/control band down toward the bottom of the viewport. A semi-transparent black scrim covers the rest of the page to reinforce focus. The interior of the panel uses 16–24px padding, with filter groups stacked vertically and separated by 16–24px gaps. When closed, the grid returns to full-width, uninterrupted 3-column layout, ensuring the default view still reads like the original 3-column gallery from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden by default and revealed as a sliding, overlay panel anchored to the left side of the viewport. It remains fixed in place while open, independent of page scroll (the underlying grid can be locked or dimmed). Closing it returns the user to the normal scrolling grid view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control sits in the same horizontal band as the merch-search-form, aligned to the far right of the content area. It occupies a compact width of about 160–220px, roughly a single column plus gutter in the 12-column system, and is vertically centered relative to the search field. To its immediate left, separated by about 12–16px, sits the 'Filter' trigger tied to the filter-sidebar. This creates a tight cluster of utility controls on the upper-right of the grid area that visually balances the dominant search field on the left. The sort-control appears as a minimal dropdown or segmented control with a dark, matte surface and subtle neon accent on active states, ensuring it is readable yet understated. A small 16–24px vertical gap separates this control band from the first row of product cards, so the sort selector feels associated with the grid, not with the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the control band. If the band is configured to be sticky, the sort-control remains fixed at the top-right within that band as the user scrolls through products.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the full width of the content area under the control band and is the visual equivalent of the mockup’s 3-column photo gallery. It is organized into three equal-width columns on desktop, each column representing approximately 4 of the 12 underlying grid columns. Thin horizontal and vertical gutters (8–16px) of pure black separate the product cards, creating a dense but orderly mosaic. The first row of products sits about 24–32px below the control band, and subsequent rows stack without additional section separation, producing a continuous wall of imagery as the user scrolls down. The grid extends as far down the page as necessary, maintaining consistent 3-column alignment. On narrower viewports, it responsively snaps to 2 columns and then 1 column, but always maintains the visual rule of equal card widths and tight gutters to preserve the immersive, gallery-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing grid that scrolls vertically. It yields temporarily under the overlay of the filter-sidebar when filters are opened, but otherwise occupies the main scrolling area of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card is a vertically oriented tile within the 3-column product-grid. The card’s outer bounds align exactly to its column, with no internal padding around the main image so the image touches the card edges and maximizes impact. The image area consumes roughly the top 70–80% of the card height, maintaining a consistent aspect ratio (around 3:4 or 4:5) to create a disciplined, poster-like rhythm across the grid. Beneath the image, a shallow, full-width caption bar sits on a pure black or very dark panel with 16–20px of internal padding. This bar contains the product name, price, and optionally a small rating icon row, stacked or aligned in a compact manner. Vertical spacing between this caption bar and the next card below is about 24px, consistent across the grid. Interaction states (hover/focus) can slightly brighten the image or reveal a subtle overlay CTA, but structurally the card remains a simple, image-first block that reads like a still from a horror film, echoing the mockup’s photo-centric tiles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky tiles that participate in the responsive grid. Their size and position adjust only as the grid reflows for different screen widths; otherwise, they remain static blocks with no floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-link lives in the topmost header bar that overlays the hero. It is positioned at the far right edge of the header, horizontally aligned with the brand mark or logo on the left. The header itself spans the full browser width, with minimal height (about 64px) and a subtle dark gradient or translucent black background so the hero imagery remains visible beneath. The cart link is presented as a concise icon (with or without a numeric badge), surrounded by 12–16px of horizontal padding, and sits about 16px from the right viewport edge. It stands out via a bright, neon-accented outline or icon color, while still keeping visual weight low so the hero remains dominant. As you scroll, the header (and thus the cart link) remains consistently visible above all content, reinforcing its functional importance without adding much interface chrome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport as part of a persistent header bar that overlays the hero and the rest of the page content. It remains accessible at all times during scrolling.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays a single merchandise product with full details, lets the user configure options and quantity, add the item to the cart, and return to the filtered merch listing.

core_features: Load detailed information for a single merch product using getMerchProductDetail based on the productId URL parameter.
Display product core data: name, main image, description, price, tags, availability status, rating, and rating_count.
Show a compact rating snippet using rating and rating_count from the product detail.
Render selectable product options (e.g., sizes and colors) using available_sizes and available_colors from the product detail.
Allow the user to set a numeric quantity with validation (minimum of 1) before adding the product to the cart.
Use addMerchToCart to add the configured product to the shopping cart, including quantity and selected_options.
Display feedback after addMerchToCart (success or failure) and update an inline cart summary using the returned cart_summary.
Provide navigation to cart.html via a cart icon or a 'View Cart' action after adding an item.
Provide a 'Back to results' link that reconstructs the merch listing state using incoming URL parameters (from, category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort).
user_workflows: View product details from merch listing: (1) User clicks a product on shop.html, which navigates to product.html with productId and the current filter/sort URL parameters. (2) On load, the page reads productId and calls getMerchProductDetail. (3) When the response arrives, the page shows the product image, name, description, price, rating snippet, and available options. (4) A 'Back to results' link is configured to send the user back to shop.html with the original filter and sort parameters.

Configure options and quantity, then add to cart: (1) After the product detail is visible, the user selects a size (if available_sizes is provided) and a color (if available_colors is provided). (2) The user adjusts the quantity using the quantity selector, which enforces a minimum of 1. (3) The user clicks the 'Add to cart' button. (4) The page validates that required options and quantity are set, then calls addMerchToCart with productId, quantity, and selected_options. (5) On success, the page shows a success message, updates the cart summary with cart_summary.total_items and cart_summary.subtotal, and offers a 'View Cart' action that navigates to cart.html.

Handle add-to-cart errors: (1) If addMerchToCart returns success = false or an error, the page shows an error message (using the message field when available). (2) The user can adjust options or quantity and attempt to add to cart again without reloading the page.

View product when opened from cart or other pages: (1) When product.html is opened with only productId (for example, from cart.html or index.html), the loader still calls getMerchProductDetail and displays the product. (2) Because no from=shop context is provided, the 'Back to results' link is either hidden or replaced by a generic navigation option (such as a simple link to shop.html without filters).

Handle unavailable or out-of-stock products: (1) After getMerchProductDetail returns, the page reads status. (2) If status indicates a non-purchasable state (e.g., not equal to the active value), the add-to-cart controls are disabled and an availability message is shown. (3) The user can still see details and navigate back to results or elsewhere, but cannot submit addMerchToCart.
interactions: On page load, the product-detail loader reads productId and any navigation context parameters from the URL, then calls getMerchProductDetail(productId).
If getMerchProductDetail resolves successfully, components bound to product data render name, image_url, description, price, rating, rating_count, tags, available_sizes, available_colors, and status.
If getMerchProductDetail fails (e.g., no product found or API error), a status message component displays an error state and hides the add-to-cart form.
When the user changes the size option, the product-options form updates its internal selected_options state for size and triggers validation for the add-to-cart button.
When the user changes the color option, the product-options form updates its internal selected_options state for color and triggers validation for the add-to-cart button.
When the user changes quantity, the quantity selector enforces numeric-only input, clamps the value to at least 1, and passes the valid quantity to the add-to-cart controller.

When the user clicks 'Add to cart', the add-to-cart controller gathers productId, quantity, and selected_options, validates them, then calls addMerchToCart. During the request, it can show a loading state on the button.
On addMerchToCart success, the page shows a success notification, updates the cart summary indicator with the returned cart_summary, and enables a 'View Cart' action.
On addMerchToCart failure, the page shows an error notification and keeps current selections so the user can adjust and retry.
When the user clicks the cart icon or 'View Cart', the page navigates to cart.html without modifying the existing URL filters for the merch listing.
When the user clicks 'Back to results', the page navigates to shop.html with category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, and sort taken from the original URL parameters if they were present.
If the user interacts with rating or tags (e.g., hovers or focuses), no additional API calls are made; the components only display the values returned by getMerchProductDetail.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is encoded in the URL query parameters. On initialization, the page reads productId to determine which product to load and calls getMerchProductDetail(productId). If productId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state and does not attempt to call addMerchToCart. Additional URL parameters (from, category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort) are treated as navigation context rather than filter controls on this page: they are read once on load and stored in the page's in-memory state as return_filters. These values are not modified by user actions on this page. The 'Back to results' link uses these stored values to construct a URL to shop.html, setting category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, and sort query parameters only when corresponding values existed in the incoming URL. If the from parameter does not indicate that the user originated from shop.html, the page either omits the 'Back to results' link or links to shop.html without additional parameters. Internal interaction state such as selected size, selected color, quantity, loading flags, and error/success messages is maintained purely in memory within the page components and is not persisted to the URL or to localStorage. Calling addMerchToCart does not alter the URL; instead, its result is reflected immediately in the cart summary indicator and status messages. Navigation to cart.html is a full page navigation that does not append special state parameters from this page. All state needed to reconstruct the merch listing is kept solely via URL parameters passed back to shop.html as specified, ensuring that the listing can restore its filter and sort state based on those parameters.

components.functionality: On page load, reads productId and navigation context parameters from the URL, calls getMerchProductDetail to retrieve full product details, and distributes the result and return_filters to child components. Manages loading and error states for the entire page.
Displays core product information from the loaded MerchProduct: name, main image, description, price, availability status, and tags. Does not perform any additional data fetching.

Shows a compact summary of product feedback using rating and rating_count from the product detail, such as a visual rating indicator and total number of ratings. It is read-only and does not load additional review data.

Renders selectable product options based on available_sizes and available_colors from the product detail. Manages internal selected_options state and ensures that required options are chosen before allowing an add-to-cart submission.
Provides an input control for the user to specify the desired quantity. Enforces numeric input and a minimum quantity of 1, and communicates valid quantity changes to the add-to-cart controller.

Coordinates the add-to-cart process. On submission, collects productId, current quantity, and selected_options from the options and quantity components, validates them, and calls addMerchToCart. Handles loading, success, and error states, and exposes success feedback and a 'View Cart' action when an item is added.

Displays a minimal summary of the current cart after addMerchToCart calls, using the cart_summary returned from the interface (e.g., total_items and subtotal). Updates its display whenever add-to-cart succeeds and provides a click target to navigate to cart.html.

Constructs and renders a 'Back to results' link when the from parameter indicates the user came from shop.html. Uses stored return_filters (category, max_price, min_rating, search_query, tags, sort) to build the href to shop.html so the original merch listing can restore its filter and sort state. If no valid context is provided, the link can be hidden or target shop.html without filters.

Reads the status field from the loaded MerchProduct to determine whether the product can be purchased. Communicates this state to the add-to-cart controller so that controls are disabled and an appropriate availability message is shown when the product is not active.

Shows global status messages for the page, including loading messages, errors when product details cannot be loaded, and success/error notifications from addMerchToCart. Consumes messages from the loader and add-to-cart controller and renders them in a non-blocking way.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a cinematic, image-led hero at the top of the page for the product itself (product-main-info as a full-width hero), followed by a dense three-column grid of interaction panels that mirror the mockup’s gallery layout. All functional elements (options, quantity, add-to-cart, availability) are grouped into card-like tiles in this grid directly below the hero, stacked vertically in a simple top-to-bottom flow. Status/notification and navigation elements float at the edges of the hero and grid rather than forming separate, text-heavy sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a cinematic, image-led hero at the top of the page for the product itself (product-main-info as a full-width hero), followed by a dense three-column grid of interaction panels that mirror the mockup’s gallery layout. All functional elements (options, quantity, add-to-cart, availability) are grouped into card-like tiles in this grid directly below the hero, stacked vertically in a simple top-to-bottom flow. Status/notification and navigation elements float at the edges of the hero and grid rather than forming separate, text-heavy sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height to a full-width hero that spans the entire content width. Below the hero, dedicate the full content width to a three-column grid: each column is approximately one-third of the content width with tight horizontal gutters (~16px) and modest vertical separation (~24–32px) from the hero. The page uses small side margins (24–32px) to keep imagery and cards visually dominant, with vertical gaps between major blocks around 32–48px, matching the immersive, image-first feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense layout: the hero nearly touches the content margins, and the three-column grid fills most of the remaining viewport with narrow black gutters between cards. Within each card, padding is compact (16–24px) and elements stack tightly. There is little empty space; the black background functions as framing rather than traditional whitespace, echoing the mockup’s portfolio-like density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye first to the central hero image and product title, then immediately down to the middle column of the three-column grid where the quantity selector and add-to-cart controller live. From there, the eye moves left and right across the grid (options on the left, availability and cart summary/back link on the right), echoing the mockup’s top-to-bottom then left-to-right scan. Supporting elements like rating, cart summary, and back navigation are visually subordinate and hugging edges so they don’t disrupt the main hero → purchase flow.

layout.overall_description: The page opens onto a dark, cinematic canvas: a full-width hero dominates the top half of the viewport, filled with a gritty neon-lit product photo framed by pure black margins. Over the lower-left of this hero, the product name, a short tagline, a compact rating snippet, and a subtle availability hint sit inside a translucent, charcoal overlay, giving the feeling of a movie poster rather than a typical product card. At the top corners of this hero layer, a faint ‘Back to Results’ link hovers on the left while a compact, pill-shaped cart summary indicator glows on the right, both kept small so they don’t compete with the central imagery. Scrolling (or glancing) just below the hero, a narrow status bar appears when needed, then immediately transitions into a three-column control grid that fills most of the width: on the left, a dark-paneled options form; in the center, a stacked quantity selector and oversized add-to-cart button; on the right, a prominent availability tile and supporting secondary actions. The cards are tightly packed with narrow horizontal gutters of black, creating a rhythm reminiscent of the mockup’s thumbnail gallery: dense, immersive, and image-centric, with a strong vertical pull from the hero and a left-to-right sweep across the interactive grid below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The init loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily sits on top of the hero and the three-column grid. It covers the entire visible area from the very top edge of the browser to the bottom, matching the dark background but slightly dimming it with a semi-opaque black scrim so any underlying content is barely visible. A single, small, horror-themed spinner or loading glyph is centered both vertically and horizontally, with no additional framing box, to keep the cinematic background uninterrupted while data loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay: positioned fixed to the viewport, centered, covering 100% width and height, and removed entirely once data loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product main info forms the hero section at the very top of the page content stack. It spans the full content width (essentially the full viewport width minus small side margins of about 24–32px), stretches horizontally across all implicit 12 grid columns, and occupies roughly 40–50% of the vertical viewport height on desktop. The hero’s background is a full-bleed, edge-to-edge product image with a slight dark gradient overlay from top to bottom to keep text legible while preserving a cinematic, neon-lit atmosphere. Along the bottom-left quadrant, a text block is anchored: the product name in large, bright type, a compact subtitle or short descriptive line below it, and a reserved slot where the rating snippet component sits directly under or beside the title. This text block is housed in a translucent charcoal panel with 16–24px internal padding, but the panel itself has no visible border, relying on contrast against the image to define it. The bottom-right portion of the hero remains mostly visual, letting the product imagery dominate. Minimal breathing room (about 24–32px) separates the bottom edge of the hero from the next section, reinforcing the stacked, hero-then-grid layout from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero section: sits at the top of the page’s content column and scrolls away with the page; it is not sticky, but its large scale makes it the initial focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating snippet is visually integrated into the hero’s lower-left text overlay, closely associated with the product title. It is positioned directly beneath the title (or to its immediate right on wider screens), left-aligned with the title’s text block inside the translucent overlay. The snippet occupies roughly one-quarter to one-third of the hero’s width, appearing as a compact horizontal strip showing star icons and a small review count. Vertical spacing between the title and the rating snippet is tight—about 8–12px—to keep the block unified. There is no separate card; instead, the snippet floats within the hero overlay, ensuring it is visible without distracting from the central image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded, static element within the hero’s text cluster; no independent scrolling or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product options form is the first tile in the control grid that sits below the hero. After a vertical gap of about 32px beneath the hero, the form occupies the left third of the content area, approximately 4 of 12 grid columns wide, forming a dark, rectangular card that aligns with the top edges of the other two columns in the row. Its edges sit about 24–32px in from the page sides, with a narrow 16px gutter separating it from the middle column. The card has 16–24px internal padding and minimal visual framing: a near-black background with a subtle, thin border or outer glow to differentiate it from the page background. Within the card, option controls (such as size, color, style) are stacked vertically with tight 8–16px gaps, maintaining the dense, utilitarian feel. The card height is driven by its content, typically making it a medium-tall tile that visually balances with the central quantity/add-to-cart card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid item: participates in the three-column layout below the hero. On narrower screens, it collapses to span nearly 100% width and stacks above the other control cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector is grouped with the add-to-cart controller in the central column of the three-column grid, forming the upper portion of a unified purchase-action card. This central card aligns horizontally with the options form on the left and the availability card on the right, occupying roughly one-third of the width (4 of 12 grid columns). Inside this card, the quantity selector is placed at the top, stretching almost the full card width with 16–24px padding from the card’s edges. It is left-aligned with the add-to-cart button below and sized so that the selector’s controls (minus/plus and input) sit on a single line. The vertical space between the bottom of the quantity selector and the top of the add-to-cart button is minimal (~8–12px), reinforcing a sense of direct dependency: choose quantity, then immediately add to cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element contained within the central grid card; it does not move independently of the add-to-cart controller but is a distinct component area within that shared card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart controller occupies the lower portion of the central grid card directly beneath the quantity selector. The card itself is positioned in the middle of the page width, about 16px from the left and right neighboring cards, reinforcing its importance as the primary interaction tile. The main add-to-cart button spans nearly the full internal width of the card, with 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, and is centered visually within the card’s width. There is a small vertical gap (8–12px) between the quantity selector and this button so the two feel connected but distinct. The button is the brightest element in the control grid—glowing or highlighted in a neon accent color—to draw the eye as the focal point after the hero. Any supporting text (e.g., “Secure checkout” or error hints) sits directly below the button, in tight vertical spacing, keeping the card compact and dense. The card’s overall height is similar or slightly taller than the options card, maintaining a balanced grid row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static central grid item, visually anchored in the middle column. On smaller screens, it stacks below the options form but remains visually dominant within its full-width card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary indicator appears at the top-right of the content area, visually hovering over the hero’s upper-right region but constrained within the page’s content width. It is a compact, pill-shaped or small rectangular badge about 25–30% of the hero’s width, aligned horizontally with the top of the hero image and inset roughly 24–32px from the right edge. The indicator is subtle but readable: a dark background with a thin neon border and small, high-contrast text showing cart item count and total amount. It sits above the main visual content without its own heavy chrome, so it feels like a HUD element overlaying the cinematic scene rather than a standalone header bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport: as the user scrolls past the hero, the indicator repositions to stay pinned near the top-right corner of the viewport (within the content column), ensuring persistent cart visibility without creating a full sticky header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results link is placed at the top-left of the content area, mirroring the cart summary indicator’s position on the right. It sits just above or slightly overlapping the upper-left corner of the hero, inset about 24–32px from the left edge. Visually, it’s a small, understated text link with an arrow icon (e.g., “← Back to merch”), rendered in a muted, light color so it is readable against the black background but clearly secondary to the hero. It does not have a heavy button treatment; instead, it resembles a subtle navigation crumb that frames the hero and gives the user an escape path back to the listing. A small vertical gap (8–12px) separates it from the hero’s top edge if not overlapping, so it feels anchored but not detached.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static: remains at the top of the page’s content stack and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page; it does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability status indicator is presented as the primary tile in the rightmost column of the three-column grid just below the hero. After the 32px gap under the hero, it aligns vertically with the tops of the options form (left) and the add-to-cart card (center) and spans the right third of the content area (4 of 12 columns), separated from the central card by a narrow 16px gutter. The indicator appears as a dark card with a high-contrast status pill (e.g., ‘In Stock’, ‘Low Stock’, ‘Sold Out’) at the top-left inside the card, followed by any short supporting text such as estimated shipping or pickup notes, all tightly stacked with 8–12px vertical spacing. The card dimensions echo the other grid tiles, with 16–24px internal padding and similar vertical height where possible so that the three cards form a visually consistent row reminiscent of the mockup’s uniform image tiles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid item: participates in the three-column layout below the hero. On smaller screens, it collapses into a full-width card that stacks beneath the add-to-cart card, maintaining its close spatial relationship to purchasing actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page status messages component appears as a full-width notification banner directly below the hero and above the three-column grid. After the hero, a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px separates it from the image, and the banner spans the entire content width across all 12 grid columns. Its height is compact—roughly 48–64px depending on text—keeping the page dense. The background is a slightly lighter or tinted dark tone compared to the page background (e.g., dark red for errors, dark green for success) with clear, high-contrast text centered vertically within the strip. When visible, it visually bridges the hero and the control grid without feeling like a separate section; when hidden, the grid shifts up, preserving the clean hero → grid rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, context-sensitive banner: appears and disappears above the control grid without affecting other components’ relative positions beyond pushing the grid down when present.

name: Event Calendar & Workshops Listing
title: Event Schedule & Workshops
description: Calendar-driven listing of scheduled events, shows, and workshops with filters for date, time, price, and tags, plus view modes for full schedule vs. workshops & shows. Users can inspect events for selected criteria and see which ones are already in My Night Plan.

core_features: Determine and persist the current schedule view mode (all_events or workshops) using the view URL parameter and the getPageViewPreference/savePageViewPreference interfaces.
Display available filter options (view modes, tag filters, price range) by calling getEventFilterOptions and binding the results to the filter controls.
Allow users to select a single date or a date range and map these selections to the date/start_date/end_date URL parameters used to call listEvents.
Allow users to filter events by daily time window using start_time and end_time URL parameters and pass those to listEvents.
Allow users to filter events by price using max_price and an optional free_only toggle, reflecting these as URL parameters and listEvents arguments.
Allow users to filter events by tags, mapping selected tags to the tags URL parameter (comma-separated) and to the tags argument of listEvents.
Fetch and display a list of events that match the current filters and view mode by calling listEvents and rendering the returned events collection.
Indicate on each event card whether the event is already part of My Night Plan using the is_in_night_plan field from listEvents.
Allow users to click an event/workshop card to navigate to event.html with the appropriate eventId and from=schedule parameters.
Provide sharable schedule URLs by encoding all active filters and view mode into the query string so another user can load the same filtered schedule state.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the schedule from navigation
1. User clicks a header or footer link that navigates to schedule.html, optionally with a view parameter and/or filter-related URL parameters.
2. On page initialization, the page reads URL parameters (view, date, start_date, end_date, start_time, end_time, max_price, free_only, tags).
3. If view is missing, the page calls getPageViewPreference with page_code='schedule_page' to retrieve a previously saved view_mode, and uses that value if returned.
4. The page calls getEventFilterOptions to retrieve available view_modes, tag_filters, and price bounds, and binds these to the filter controls.
5. The page normalizes date parameters: if date is present and start_date/end_date are not, it sets an internal start_date and end_date equal to that single date for use in listEvents; if start_date and end_date exist, it uses them and ignores date.
6. The page constructs the arguments for listEvents from the normalized date range, current time window (start_time, end_time), price selection (max_price, free_only), tag selection (tags), and the resolved view (mapped to view_mode).
7. The page calls listEvents with these arguments.
8. When listEvents returns, the page renders the event list using the returned events array, including capacity_remaining, is_free, is_in_night_plan, tags, and other fields as provided.

Workflow 2: Changing view mode between full schedule and workshops
1. User clicks a control (e.g., a toggle or segmented control) to switch between 'All Events' and 'Workshops & Shows'.
2. The control sets the view URL parameter to 'all_events' or 'workshops' and updates the browser URL query string without removing other active filter parameters.
3. The page calls savePageViewPreference with page_code='schedule_page' and view_mode equal to the new view value so that this choice can be used as the default in future visits.
4. The page re-invokes listEvents with the same date/time/price/tag parameters, but with view_mode set to the new view value.
5. The event list re-renders to show only the events matching the new view mode (filtering logic handled by the backend via listEvents).

Workflow 3: Filtering by date range, time window, price, and tags
1. User adjusts the date selection (single date or range) via the date-picker controls.
2. The date-picker component updates the date/start_date/end_date URL parameters, ensuring that either a single date or a range is represented and that the query string reflects the new selection.
3. User optionally adjusts the time window (start_time and end_time) using time-range controls; these values are written into the URL parameters.
4. User optionally adjusts price filters: changing max_price updates the max_price URL parameter; toggling free_only updates the free_only URL parameter, and the UI may also align max_price with the semantics of a free-only filter when free_only is enabled.
5. User optionally selects or deselects tag filters (for example, family-friendly, workshops, or other tags provided by getEventFilterOptions); the set of selected tags is converted to a comma-separated string and stored in the tags URL parameter.
6. After any filter change, the page reconstructs the argument set for listEvents from the updated URL values and calls listEvents.
7. When listEvents returns, the event list updates to show only events matching the new filters. The URL now encodes the complete filter state for sharing or bookmarking.

Workflow 4: Inspecting event details and My Night Plan indicators
1. After events are loaded via listEvents, each event is displayed as a clickable card that exposes key fields (title, time, venue_name, price/is_free, tags, capacity_remaining, and the is_in_night_plan indicator).
2. The card visually reflects the is_in_night_plan boolean (for example, by rendering an indicator state) but does not modify My Night Plan on this page since no NightPlan interfaces are available here.
3. User clicks an event card they are interested in.
4. The page performs a navigation to event.html with URL parameters eventId set to the event's event_id and from set to 'schedule'.
5. The user views detailed information on event.html; when they navigate back to schedule.html (via browser back or navigation links), the previous filter state is restored from the URL.

Workflow 5: Navigating to My Night Plan from the schedule
1. User clicks a 'My Night Plan' link in the global header or other navigation element while on schedule.html.
2. The navigation takes the user to night_plan.html without additional parameters or modifying the current schedule URL state.
3. If the user later returns to schedule.html via browser back, the previous schedule filters and view mode remain represented in the URL, and the page reconstitutes the state from those parameters and re-calls listEvents.
interactions: On page load (onInit), parse URL query parameters (view, date, start_date, end_date, start_time, end_time, max_price, free_only, tags). If view is absent, call getPageViewPreference with page_code='schedule_page' to obtain a default view_mode; use that as the effective view unless overridden by a URL value.
On page load (onInit), call getEventFilterOptions to populate available view modes, tag filters, and price bounds, and initialize filter controls accordingly.

When the user changes the view mode control (onChange), update the view URL parameter, call savePageViewPreference with page_code='schedule_page' and the new view_mode, then call listEvents with the updated view_mode and current filter parameters.

When the user selects a single date from the date picker (onChange), set or update the date URL parameter, clear or synchronize start_date and end_date as needed, update the URL, and re-call listEvents using a date range where start_date and end_date match the selected date.

When the user selects a date range from the date picker (onChange), update start_date and end_date URL parameters, remove or ignore the date parameter for the listEvents call, and re-call listEvents using the new range.

When the user changes the start_time or end_time fields (onChange), update the corresponding URL parameters, validate that the start is not after the end (onValidate), then re-call listEvents with the updated time window.
When the user changes the max_price input (onChange), update the max_price URL parameter, optionally adjust the free_only control state if it conflicts, and re-call listEvents.

When the user toggles the free_only control (onChange), update the free_only URL parameter, optionally adjust max_price if treating free-only as a special case, and re-call listEvents with free_only reflecting the toggle state.

When the user selects or deselects tag filters (onChange), recompute the set of selected tags, convert them into a comma-separated string, update the tags URL parameter, and re-call listEvents with that tags string.

When listEvents resolves (onSuccess), update the event list component’s data model with the returned events array and re-render the event cards, including the is_in_night_plan indicator where provided.
When listEvents fails (onError), show an appropriate error state in the event list component (e.g., a functional message) without relying on hardcoded event content.
When the user clicks an event card (onClick), navigate to event.html with eventId equal to the card’s event_id and from='schedule' in the query string.
When the user clicks the 'My Night Plan' navigation link (onClick), navigate to night_plan.html, leaving the current schedule URL intact for back navigation.
When the browser back or forward buttons are used (onPopState), re-parse the URL parameters, recompute the filter state, and re-call listEvents so that the UI reflects the URL-driven state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state of the schedule page is stored and reconstructed through URL parameters and the PageViewPreference interface. URL parameters always represent the source of truth for filter and view state at any given time. The 'view' parameter determines whether the page shows the full schedule or only workshops & shows. On initial load, if 'view' is not provided in the URL, the page calls getPageViewPreference(page_code='schedule_page'). If a view_mode is returned, that value is used as the effective view mode; if not, a default view mode defined by business rules is used. When the user changes the view mode via the UI, the 'view' parameter in the URL is updated and savePageViewPreference is called so that subsequent visits can default to this mode.

Date-related state uses 'date', 'start_date', and 'end_date'. If 'date' is present with no 'start_date'/'end_date', it is internally translated into a range where listEvents receives start_date=date and end_date=date. If 'start_date' and 'end_date' are present, they override 'date' for listEvents calls, and 'date' can be ignored or cleared. The UI ensures that all visible date selections are consistent with these URL parameters.

Time window state is controlled by 'start_time' and 'end_time' URL parameters. If both are present, they are passed unmodified to listEvents. If one is missing, the UI may use business-defined defaults for display but only sends the values present (or normalized) to listEvents. Any change to these controls updates the URL and triggers a new listEvents call.

Price filter state is stored in 'max_price' and 'free_only'. The UI writes user selections to these parameters directly. When free_only is enabled, the interface may also adjust max_price according to the free-only semantics (for example, aligning max_price with a free filter) while still passing both max_price and free_only to listEvents, leaving final interpretation to the backend.

Tag filters are represented by a 'tags' URL parameter as a comma-separated list. The UI maintains a set of selected tags; any modification leads to recomputing this string, updating the 'tags' parameter, and calling listEvents with the same string in its 'tags' argument.

Whenever any of the relevant URL parameters changes (including via direct navigation, user input, or history navigation), the page re-derives the full filter state from the URL and calls listEvents(start_date, end_date, start_time, end_time, max_price, free_only, tags, view_mode), where view_mode is resolved from the 'view' parameter (or preference default). No state is persisted in localStorage or other client-side storage; all persistence beyond the current URL is handled through the PageViewPreference interface for view mode only.

components.functionality: Central page controller responsible for reading URL parameters, resolving view mode preferences, fetching filter configuration, normalizing date/time/price/tag state, and orchestrating calls to listEvents and downstream components.
Allows user to switch between full schedule view and workshops & shows view. Updates the 'view' URL parameter and persists the selection via PageViewPreference.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize selected view mode from URL or PageViewPreference and inform schedule-page-init.

onChange: When user selects a different view mode, update URL 'view', call savePageViewPreference(page_code='schedule_page', view_mode), and trigger schedule-page-init.onViewModeChange to re-call listEvents.

components.functionality: Enables selection of either a single date or a date range for the schedule, and maps this selection to the date/start_date/end_date URL parameters used for listEvents.
event_handlers: onInit: Read date/start_date/end_date from URL and render the corresponding selection in the UI.

onChangeSingleDate: When user selects a single date, set URL 'date' to that value, adjust or clear 'start_date' and 'end_date' as required, push new URL state, and notify schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.
onChangeDateRange: When user selects a date range, set URL 'start_date' and 'end_date', clear or ignore 'date', push new URL state, and notify schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.

components.functionality: Controls daily time window for events through start_time and end_time, allowing users to restrict events to a specific portion of the day.
event_handlers: onInit: Read start_time and end_time from URL and display current values in the time controls.
onChangeStartTime: Update the start_time URL parameter when user changes the start time, validate against end_time if present, push updated URL, and trigger schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.
onChangeEndTime: Update the end_time URL parameter when user changes the end time, validate against start_time if present, push updated URL, and trigger schedule-page-init.onFilterChange
onValidate: Ensure the time window is logically consistent before notifying schedule-page-init; if invalid, revert or adjust values based on business rules.

components.functionality: Manages price-based filtering, including max_price input and interaction with the free_only toggle, using bounds from getEventFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize max_price input from URL or from getEventFilterOptions bounds if URL is not set.

onChangeMaxPrice: When user adjusts max_price, update the max_price URL parameter, ensure consistency with free_only if required by business logic, push the updated URL, and trigger schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.

components.functionality: Provides a dedicated toggle to limit events to free options only, synchronizing with the free_only URL parameter and coordinating with max_price behavior.
event_handlers: onInit: Read free_only from URL to set the initial toggle state.

onChange: Toggle the free_only state, update the free_only URL parameter (and optionally adjust max_price URL as necessary to represent free-only behavior), push the updated URL, and trigger schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.

components.functionality: Displays available tag filters and allows multi-selection of tags, mapping selections to the comma-separated 'tags' URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Use getEventFilterOptions.tag_filters to render available tags and parse URL 'tags' into the initial selected set.

onChangeTagSelection: When user selects or deselects a tag, recompute the selected tag list, convert it into a comma-separated string, update URL 'tags', push the new URL, and trigger schedule-page-init.onFilterChange.

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the current filter state and the number of events returned, without hardcoding any event data.
event_handlers: onEventsLoaded: Update the displayed count using the length of the events array from listEvents.
onFilterStateChange: Reflect current filter state (derived from URL parameters) in the summary text.

components.functionality: Renders the list or grid of events returned from listEvents, delegating to event-card for each entry.
event_handlers: onInit: Render initial empty or loading state while listEvents is in progress.
onEventsLoaded: Receive events array from schedule-page-init, clear previous content, and render one event-card per event.
onEventsLoadError: Display a functional error or empty state when listEvents fails or returns no events.

components.functionality: Represents a single event or workshop, showing essential data and supporting navigation to the event detail page, as well as My Night Plan indication.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to event.html with query parameters eventId=listEvents.events[].event_id and from='schedule'.
onHover: Optionally show additional functional details based solely on the event data provided (no hardcoded content).

components.functionality: Handles navigation into this page via header links such as 'Schedule' and 'Workshops & Shows', ensuring the appropriate view parameter is present in the URL.
event_handlers: onClickScheduleLink: Navigate to schedule.html with view set to 'all_events' (preserving or resetting other filters as determined by global navigation rules).
onClickWorkshopsLink: Navigate to schedule.html with view set to 'workshops' (preserving or resetting other filters as determined by global navigation rules).

components.functionality: Global navigation element that routes the user from the schedule page to the My Night Plan page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to night_plan.html without altering the current schedule.html URL so that back navigation recovers the same filter state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a dominant, cinematic hero at the top (for the schedule context and navigation) followed by a dense, image-driven grid of event cards. All filters (view mode, date, time, price, free, tags) sit in a compact horizontal filter band immediately below the hero, acting like a thin control strip above the event grid. The event list itself is realized as a 3-column grid of visual event cards, echoing the gallery pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a dominant, cinematic hero at the top (for the schedule context and navigation) followed by a dense, image-driven grid of event cards. All filters (view mode, date, time, price, free, tags) sit in a compact horizontal filter band immediately below the hero, acting like a thin control strip above the event grid. The event list itself is realized as a 3-column grid of visual event cards, echoing the gallery pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full content width (about 90–96% of the browser width, aligned to a 12-column grid). Below it, the filter band and results summary form a low-height strip (about 10–15% of the initial viewport), leaving the remaining vertical space for a 3-column event grid that can scroll. Black background fills edges and gutters, with narrow horizontal gutters (8–16px) between event cards and modest margins (16–24px) to the browser edge to maximize image real estate.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use a moderately dense layout: imagery dominates with minimal whitespace, mimicking an image portfolio. Event cards sit close together with narrow black gutters; filters are compact and almost flush to the event grid. Padding inside interactive controls respects the 8–24px scale, but pure imagery (hero and event cards) has virtually no internal padding—only external margins/gutters frame them. Sections are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps, enough to be legible while preserving the immersive, dark, continuous feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first caught by the bright, neon-tinged hero at the top center (title and main schedule context). From there, attention moves downward to the filter band, which forms a clear horizontal checkpoint before the content grid. Users then scan the event grid left-to-right across each row, then down to subsequent rows, mirroring the mockup’s gallery flow. Navigation and the My Night Plan link are anchored in the hero’s top edge and become a subtle sticky strip as users scroll, preserving orientation without adding heavy chrome.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a full-width, dark, cinematic hero panel that fills roughly the upper half of the viewport. A gritty, neon-lit zombie-event photo stretches edge-to-edge across the central content area, with a subtle 16–24px margin from the browser edges so the imagery feels dominant but still framed by black. Overlaid at the top of this hero is a thin navigation bar: on the left, the schedule header navigation; on the right, a glowing My Night Plan link. Centered in the hero is the event calendar title and a view mode toggle, floating above the imagery. As you scroll down, the hero yields to a low, dark filter band that spans the same width, holding date, time, price, free-only, and tag filters in tight horizontal clusters. Immediately beneath this, a concise results summary line anchors the top of a dense, 3-column grid of cinematic event cards. Each row shows three equally sized, image-led cards with narrow black gutters between them, creating a rhythm similar to an image gallery. The background stays uniformly black from top to bottom, so the page feels like one continuous, immersive canvas where bright imagery and minimal UI glow against the darkness, guiding the user from the hero, through the filters, and into the grid of events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component has no direct visual representation. Conceptually, it anchors the entire schedule page at the top of the document flow, just above the hero section, initializing filters, view mode, and event data before any visual elements render. Its ‘position’ is purely structural; it does not occupy visible space or alter the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; behaves as an invisible page-level controller with no spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule header navigation is overlaid at the very top of the hero image, spanning almost the full width of the central content area (about 90–96% of the browser width, centered). It forms a slim horizontal bar approximately 56–64px tall. The left side (occupying roughly the left 4–5 columns of the 12-column grid) holds the page or site logo/title and basic navigation. The center area is mostly negative (transparent) space to let the hero image show through, maintaining the cinematic feel. The right side (about 3–4 columns) houses the My Night Plan link and any auxiliary navigation. The bar itself is subtly separated from the hero by a faint translucent black overlay, but visually it feels integrated into the hero rather than a distinct chrome layer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially overlays the top of the hero. On scroll, it compresses slightly and becomes a sticky bar pinned to the top of the viewport, maintaining its height around 48–56px and preserving quick access to navigation and the My Night Plan link without introducing heavy visual weight.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Night Plan link sits inside the schedule header navigation, aligned to the far right edge of the navigation bar. It appears as a compact but visually striking pill-shaped button or link, about 32px tall with 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, occupying roughly 1 column in width with some breathing room to its left. It is vertically centered within the navigation bar’s height. On large screens, it visually anchors the top-right of the hero, glowing subtly against the dark backdrop, making it easy to spot without overpowering the hero imagery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows the behavior of the schedule header navigation: it moves with the sticky navigation bar and remains fixed to the top-right when the user scrolls past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view mode toggle is placed within the lower half of the hero section, horizontally centered on the page. It sits directly below the main hero title or calendar label, with about 16–24px vertical spacing separating it from the title text. The toggle’s width is modest—spanning roughly 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid—forming a compact segmented control (e.g., ‘Full Schedule’ vs. ‘Workshops & Shows’). It does not extend the full width of the hero; instead, it floats as a discrete horizontal element anchored to center, creating a focal point between the hero imagery and the upcoming filter band below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls away together with the hero content. Once the hero is out of view, the selected view mode state remains active, but the control itself is no longer visible (it is not sticky), keeping the persistent chrome minimal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date range picker is the first and most left-aligned control in the filter band directly below the hero. The filter band spans the same width as the event grid (about 90–96% of the page width, centered) and sits about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero image. Within this band, the date picker occupies roughly the leftmost 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid, forming a horizontally oriented control. It has about 12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal internal padding, with 8–12px of external horizontal gutter separating it from the next control. It is aligned to the top edge of the band so that all filter controls form a tight, single-row or two-row block with minimal vertical gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside a unified filter strip that becomes sticky just below the sticky navigation on scroll. As the user scrolls the event grid, the date range picker remains fixed at the top of the viewport within this band, enabling continuous refinement of the schedule without disrupting the gallery-like event list.
