component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time window filter is positioned immediately to the right of the date range picker within the same filter band. It occupies about 2–3 columns of the 12-column grid, forming a compact dropdown or pill-based selector. There is a narrow horizontal gutter of about 8–12px between this control and the date range picker, aligning their top edges so they visually read as a cluster. Like the other filters, it rests on the dark background with subtle glow or border cues, but the overall look remains understated so it doesn’t compete with the event imagery below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the sticky behavior of the filter band. When the user scrolls, it stays locked just below the sticky navigation bar, maintaining its relative position to the date range picker.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price filter controls are placed to the right of the time window filter in the filter band, occupying approximately 2–3 columns of the 12-column grid. They are rendered as a compact control group—perhaps a slider thumb or a small set of discrete price step buttons—arranged horizontally. The group has modest internal padding (8–12px) and sits flush with the top of the band so that its top edge aligns with the date and time filters. A narrow 8–12px gutter separates it from the adjacent controls, keeping the overall band tight and dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides within the unified sticky filter strip, remaining fixed below the navigation as the user scrolls through the event grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The free-only toggle is a small, visually distinct switch or pill placed immediately adjacent to the price filter controls, slightly offset to the right. It occupies roughly 1–1.5 columns of width, with just enough horizontal padding (about 12px vertical, 16px horizontal) to be tappable while staying compact. It aligns vertically with the center of the price controls, effectively reading as an inline qualifier. A small gap (about 8px) to its left separates it from the price controls, but it still feels part of the same ‘cost’ cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the sticky filter band; remains fixed to the top of the viewport, maintaining its relative position next to the price controls as content scrolls beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tag filter controls sit toward the right side of the filter band, occupying the remaining horizontal space (roughly the rightmost 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid). They are rendered as a horizontal row or two of compact, pill-shaped tags with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding each. Tags wrap to a second line if necessary, but the entire tag area is visually bounded to the height of the filter band (which may expand slightly to accommodate one additional row). The tag cluster is right-aligned within the band so that date/time/price/free form a left cluster and tags form a right cluster, giving the strip a balanced, gallery-header feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Also part of the sticky filter band. When the user scrolls, the tags remain visible along the top, aligned to the right, allowing quick thematic filtering while browsing the event grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event results summary is a single-line text element positioned immediately below the filter band and aligned to the left edge of the event grid. It sits about 16–24px below the bottom-most filter control (or within the lower edge of the filter band if the band is taller), acting as a quiet caption for the grid. It spans roughly the left 6–8 columns of the 12-column grid but is visually low-profile: small, dimly lit text that does not compete with the imagery. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the results summary and the first row of event cards, clearly separating meta information from the grid while keeping everything tightly stacked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the event grid and is not sticky; it moves out of view as the user scrolls down through more events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event list occupies the main content area below the event results summary, filling the remaining vertical space on the page and extending indefinitely downward as a scrollable region. It is laid out as a 3-column grid aligned to the same content width as the hero and filter band (about 90–96% of the browser width, centered on the page). On large screens, each column corresponds to roughly 4 of the 12 grid columns, with horizontal gutters of about 12–16px between cards and a minimal margin of 16–24px from the outer edges of the content area to the browser edge. Vertically, cards are stacked in rows with 16–24px spacing between rows. This creates a uniform mosaic of event cards—three per row—mirroring the mockup’s gallery structure. The background between cards is pure black, acting as a framing void that makes each card’s neon imagery pop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls under the sticky navigation and filter band. On narrower screens, the grid can responsively shift to 2 columns or 1 column, but it always maintains equal-width cards and consistent gutters, preserving the gallery-like rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event card is a tall, image-led tile that fits into one column of the 3-column grid. The card’s width adapts to its column (about one-third of the content width on large screens), and its height is proportioned so that it feels slightly taller than it is wide—for example, a 3:4 or 4:5 aspect ratio—to enhance the cinematic poster feel. The card has no internal padding around the image; instead, the event imagery bleeds to the card’s edges, with only a subtle 8–12px radius or hard edge differentiating it from the black background. A dark gradient overlay at the bottom 20–30% of the card provides space for overlaid text: event name, time, and a small tag or price marker. This text is left-aligned within the bottom area, with about 12–16px padding from the card edges. Cards are separated from adjacent cards by narrow black gutters (about 12–16px horizontally and 16–24px vertically), preserving the dense, gallery-like arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid; cards do not stick or float. They may slightly enlarge or brighten on hover, but spatially they remain locked to their grid positions, maintaining the uniform mosaic.

name: Event / Workshop Detail
title: Event / Workshop Detail
description: Displays full details for a single scheduled event or workshop, and lets users add it to My Night Plan or register for eligible free workshops.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a single event or workshop based on the eventId URL parameter using getEventDetail.
Show key event metadata including description, date, start and end times, duration, venue name, tags, family-friendly status, and capacity/availability.
Indicate whether the event is already in My Night Plan and allow users to add it to their plan via addEventToNightPlan.
Allow removal of the event from My Night Plan via removeEventFromNightPlan when the event was added during the current page session and the night_plan_item_id is available.
Display a conditional free workshop registration section for events where is_free is true and event_type is 'workshop', including a registration form bound to registerForWorkshop.
Collect registration data (full_name, email, phone, party_size, newsletter_opt_in) and submit it to registerForWorkshop, showing inline success or error feedback.

Provide navigation back to the calendar (schedule.html?view=all_events) when the page is accessed from the schedule (from=schedule) and a shortcut to night_plan.html after adding an event to the plan.
user_workflows:
View event details from the schedule:
1) User selects an event in schedule.html, which navigates to event.html with URL parameters eventId and from=schedule.
2) On page load, the event-detail-loader component parses eventId and calls getEventDetail(eventId).
3) When getEventDetail returns successfully, the event-header, event-meta-info, and event-availability-indicator components render the title, description, timing, venue, tags, and capacity information.
4) User reviews what the event is about, when and where it happens, how long it lasts, and whether it is family-friendly or has limited capacity.

Add an event to My Night Plan:
1) With event details loaded, the night-plan-toggle component reads is_in_night_plan from the getEventDetail response.
2) If is_in_night_plan is false, the component shows an actionable 'Add to My Night Plan' control.
3) User clicks the control; night-plan-toggle calls addEventToNightPlan(eventId) and enters a loading state.
4) On success, the component stores the returned night_plan_item_id in local page state, updates its internal isInNightPlan state to true, and updates the UI to reflect that the event is now in the plan.
5) The component reveals a 'View My Night Plan' navigation control that, when clicked, navigates the user to night_plan.html.

Remove an event from My Night Plan (when added in the current session):
1) After a successful addEventToNightPlan() call on this page, the night-plan-toggle component has a nightPlanItemId in local state.
2) The component can show a 'Remove from My Night Plan' action alongside the added state.
3) When the user triggers this action, night-plan-toggle calls removeEventFromNightPlan(nightPlanItemId) and enters a loading state for that action.
4) On success, the component clears nightPlanItemId from local state, sets isInNightPlan back to false, and returns the control to its original 'Add to My Night Plan' state.
5) If getEventDetail reports is_in_night_plan as true but no local nightPlanItemId is available (e.g., event was added elsewhere), the component instead shows a non-removable 'Already in My Night Plan' indicator and a link to open night_plan.html for management there, without calling removeEventFromNightPlan.

Register for a free workshop:
1) After getEventDetail resolves, the workshop-registration-section checks if the event’s is_free is true, event_type is 'workshop', and (optionally) capacity_remaining is greater than zero.
2) If conditions are met, the section displays the workshop-registration-form with inputs for full_name, email, phone, party_size, and an unchecked-by-default newsletter_opt_in checkbox.
3) User fills in the form and submits it; the form validates that required fields are non-empty and party_size is a positive number.
4) On successful client-side validation, the form calls registerForWorkshop with eventId, full_name, email, phone, party_size, and newsletter_opt_in, then disables inputs and shows a submission-in-progress state.
5) On successful API response, the registration-feedback-panel displays the returned message and/or a generic confirmation, associates the response.registration_id with the local success state, and the form is either cleared or collapsed.
6) Optionally, after success, the event-detail-loader may trigger a fresh getEventDetail(eventId) call to update displayed capacity_remaining to reflect the new registration.

Navigate back to the calendar:
1) When the user arrived from schedule.html (indicated by from=schedule in the URL), the event-navigation-links component displays a 'Back to calendar' link.
2) Clicking this link navigates to schedule.html with the URL parameter view=all_events.
3) The user lands back on the main event calendar while the previously viewed event state is handled by schedule.html according to its own logic.
interactions: Page load: On initial load or when the eventId URL parameter changes, event-detail-loader reads eventId and calls getEventDetail(eventId). It manages loading and error states that drive visibility of other components.

Back to calendar: If the from URL parameter equals 'schedule', event-navigation-links renders a 'Back to calendar' link. Clicking it changes the location to schedule.html?view=all_events without additional API calls.

Add to My Night Plan button click: When is_in_night_plan is false and the user clicks 'Add to My Night Plan', night-plan-toggle calls addEventToNightPlan(eventId). On success, it updates local isInNightPlan and stores night_plan_item_id; on failure, it displays an error message and keeps the button enabled for retry.

Remove from My Night Plan button click (session-added only): If the event was added during the current page session and nightPlanItemId is present in local state, clicking 'Remove from My Night Plan' calls removeEventFromNightPlan(nightPlanItemId). On success, the component reverts to the 'Add to My Night Plan' state; on failure, it surfaces an error while keeping the event marked as in the plan until reattempted.

View My Night Plan navigation: Once an event is in the plan (either from the current session or reported by is_in_night_plan), the user can click a 'View My Night Plan' control that navigates directly to night_plan.html.

Workshop form field input: As the user types into full_name, email, phone, or party_size, workshop-registration-form updates its internal form state and clears any field-level validation errors when a field becomes valid.

Newsletter checkbox toggle: When the user checks or unchecks the newsletter_opt_in checkbox, workshop-registration-form updates its local newsletter_opt_in boolean, which is later passed to registerForWorkshop.

Workshop registration submit: On submit, workshop-registration-form performs validation. If validation passes, it calls registerForWorkshop with eventId and the form data. While awaiting a response, the form is disabled and a loading indicator is shown; on success or failure, registration-feedback-panel displays corresponding messaging.

Capacity-dependent behavior: event-availability-indicator reads capacity_total and capacity_remaining from the event data. If capacity_remaining indicates no remaining spots, it can disable the workshop-registration-form submission and/or display a non-interactive full/closed state without making additional API calls.

Error handling: If getEventDetail fails, event-detail-loader sets an error state that event-header and event-meta-info interpret to hide the main content and show an error message output instead of event details. If registerForWorkshop or night plan APIs fail, the corresponding components show inline error messages and reset controls to allow retry.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the eventId URL parameter. On every page load, or whenever eventId in the query string changes, the page calls getEventDetail(eventId) to obtain the event data. The optional from parameter influences navigation behavior: when from is 'schedule', the event-navigation-links component renders a 'Back to calendar' link that navigates to schedule.html?view=all_events; otherwise that link is omitted. The event data returned from getEventDetail populates local reactive state properties such as title, description, event_type, start_datetime, end_datetime, duration_minutes, venue_name, price, is_free, tags, capacity_total, capacity_remaining, is_family_friendly, and is_in_night_plan. The is_in_night_plan flag initializes the night-plan-toggle's isInNightPlan UI state. When the user successfully calls addEventToNightPlan(eventId), the response.night_plan_item_id is stored in a local variable (nightPlanItemId) for this page instance only; this ID is required to call removeEventFromNightPlan. If is_in_night_plan is true from the server but no local nightPlanItemId is present (e.g., because the event was added in a previous session or from another page), the component treats the event as already in the plan but does not expose removal functionality from this page, instead offering navigation to night_plan.html. For workshop registration, local state includes form field values, validation errors, a submission-in-progress flag, and a registrationResult object (with success, message, and registration_id) populated from registerForWorkshop responses. On a successful registration, components may optionally trigger a secondary getEventDetail(eventId) call to refresh capacity_remaining and reflect reduced availability. No persistent client storage such as localStorage is used; all persistent state is represented via server-driven responses and URL parameters (eventId and from), while component state exists only for the lifetime of the page view.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by parsing URL parameters (eventId and from), calling getEventDetail(eventId), and managing loading and error states. Exposes the loaded event data and context (from) to child components.

Displays the event title and key high-level information such as whether it is a workshop or other event type, and whether it is free or paid. Renders only when event data from event-detail-loader is available and not in error.

Shows detailed metadata for the event: description, formatted start and end datetime, optional duration_minutes, venue_name, tags, and indicators such as is_family_friendly. It updates whenever the underlying event data changes.

Visualizes availability information based on capacity_total and capacity_remaining, and can display messages such as available spots, limited capacity, or full. It also exposes a derived flag used by other components (e.g., workshop-registration-section) to enable or disable actions when capacity is exhausted.

Provides controls to add the current event to My Night Plan and, when possible, remove it. Initializes its isInNightPlan state from Event.is_in_night_plan. On add, it calls addEventToNightPlan(eventId) and stores the returned night_plan_item_id. On remove, it calls removeEventFromNightPlan(nightPlanItemId) when that ID is available. If the event is already in the plan but the item ID is unknown, it displays an informational state and a link to night_plan.html instead of directly calling removeEventFromNightPlan.

Determines whether to display the workshop registration form based on event data. It renders the workshop-registration-form and registration-feedback-panel only if the event is a workshop (event_type equals 'workshop') and is_free is true. It may also factor in capacity_remaining from Event and derived availability flags from event-availability-indicator to hide or disable registration when no capacity remains.

Collects registration details for an eligible free workshop: full_name, email, phone, party_size, and optional newsletter_opt_in. Performs client-side validation on submit and, if valid, calls registerForWorkshop with eventId and the form values. Manages its own loading state and passes the API response or error state to registration-feedback-panel.

Displays outcome messages for workshop registrations. When registerForWorkshop returns, it reads the success flag, message, and registration_id from the response and shows success or error messaging inline beneath the form. It can also signal to workshop-registration-form whether to reset or hide the form after a successful registration.

Renders contextual navigation controls based on URL parameters and event state. When the from parameter equals 'schedule', it shows a 'Back to calendar' link that navigates to schedule.html?view=all_events. It also exposes a hook for night-plan-toggle to render a 'View My Night Plan' link that navigates to night_plan.html after an event is added to the plan.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strong, full-width cinematic hero for the event header at the very top, occupying roughly the top half of the initial viewport. Below the hero, mirror the mockup’s 3-column gallery by transforming the event details into a dense, three-column information grid: left column for descriptive/meta info, center column for live availability and registration, right column for planning and navigation. All components flow in a single vertical stack: hero first, then the 3-column grid of detail content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strong, full-width cinematic hero for the event header at the very top, occupying roughly the top half of the initial viewport. Below the hero, mirror the mockup’s 3-column gallery by transforming the event details into a dense, three-column information grid: left column for descriptive/meta info, center column for live availability and registration, right column for planning and navigation. All components flow in a single vertical stack: hero first, then the 3-column grid of detail content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The event hero spans 100% of the content width and about 40–50% of the viewport height. Below it, a main content container sits centered and spans about 90–96% of the viewport width, divided into three equal columns (each ~1/3 of the width minus gutters). Vertical spacing between hero and grid is modest (about 32–40px), preserving the dense, immersive feel. Horizontal gutters between columns are narrow (8–16px), with small outer margins to the browser edge. Within each card-like block, padding is 16–24px where text or controls appear.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense layout: the hero and the detail grid use most of the available horizontal space with only thin black gutters as framing. Individual components sit in tight, self-contained blocks with minimal empty space between them. Cards are clearly delineated against the black background but there is no generous white space; instead, the black backdrop and neon-like highlights create visual separation while keeping content packed and immersive.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins with the event hero image and title at the top center, drawing attention immediately. The eye then naturally drops into the three-column grid: first scanning the left column (event description and meta context), then the center column (availability and registration, as the main interactive focus), and finally the right column (My Night Plan toggle and navigation links). Within each column, elements stack vertically with small gaps, encouraging a top-to-bottom read in each column and a left-to-right scan across the grid, echoing the mockup’s hero-then-gallery rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a dark, cinematic hero that fills the full width of the screen and about half the viewport height, showcasing a gritty zombie-event image with neon-lit title typography centered over it. The rest of the page sits on a deep black background, with a narrow strip of darkness separating the hero from the content below. Under this, the layout resolves into a tight, three-column grid that feels like a gallery of information blocks: on the left, a tall card of event meta details; in the center, a stack of availability status and registration controls; on the right, planning actions and navigation. Each column is roughly equal in width, separated by slim black gutters, so the entire mid-page reads like two or three rows of dark, glowing tiles. The content is dense but controlled, with each block clearly framed against the dark canvas. The user’s eye moves from the striking hero down into the center column’s live status and registration form, then sweeps across to supporting information and navigation, all within a visually immersive, horror-themed atmosphere that prioritizes imagery and mood over heavy UI chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily sits above all other content, including the hero. It uses the same deep black base as the page background, slightly dimming whatever is behind it. At the center of the screen (both vertically and horizontally), a compact loading indicator and short status text are positioned, roughly occupying a 240–300px wide area. This central loading module aligns visually with where the event title would appear in the hero, reinforcing the primary focal point. When the data finishes loading, this overlay fades out completely, revealing the underlying hero at the top and the three-column detail grid below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the entire viewport; centered content; appears only during data loading and is removed once data is available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-header functions as the hero section and is placed at the very top of the page, spanning the full available content width from left to right with minimal horizontal margins to the browser edge (about 2–4% on each side, if any). It occupies approximately 40–50% of the initial viewport height, creating a large cinematic banner. The background of this section is dominated by a high-impact zombie-event image, with any event title and key info overlaid in the central to lower-central area. The title is horizontally centered, with secondary text (date, location, maybe a short tagline) aligned closely beneath it, forming a tight text cluster that commands the viewer’s first attention. The bottom edge of the hero image transitions directly into the dark page background, with a modest vertical gap (about 32–40px) of black space before the three-column grid begins. The hero visually anchors the experience and aligns with the implicit 12-column grid by spanning all 12 columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width top section that scrolls away normally with the page; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-meta-info component occupies the left column of the three-column grid immediately below the hero. It sits within the main content container, which is centered and spans about 90–96% of the viewport width. This left column is approximately one-third of that width, minus a narrow gutter on its right side (8–16px). The meta-info card starts near the top of the grid area, directly aligned horizontally under the left edge of the hero’s content. It extends downward as needed, typically forming a tall, rectangular card with a solid black or slightly elevated background. Inside, content (description, age restrictions, time details, location specifics, etc.) is padded by about 16–24px on all sides. There is a small vertical gap (about 16–24px) below its internal heading, with subsequent meta rows stacked tightly. This card acts as a stable left anchor for the grid, visually similar in height to the central column’s content, giving the impression of a left-side image tile in the original gallery mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the main 3-column grid; participates in responsive stacking (on narrow screens it moves below the hero and above center/right column content in a vertical flow).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-availability-indicator is placed in the center column at the top of the three-column grid, aligned under the middle section of the hero. The center column is about one-third of the content width, with narrow gutters on both sides. The availability indicator is a shallow, wide band that spans the full width of this center column, sitting flush to its top edge. It occupies roughly 56–80px in height, resembling a status strip that would be the first thing seen after the hero when the eye moves downward. This band may use a subtle neon edge or glow to stand out, but spatially it remains compact so that the registration elements can stack directly underneath with a small gap (about 8–16px). Together, the indicator and the registration section form the central focal block of the grid, echoing the central gallery tile in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the top of the center column; it pushes subsequent components (like registration) downward in the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The night-plan-toggle is positioned in the right column of the three-column grid, near the top of that column, horizontally aligned with the upper edge of the event-meta-info and only slightly lower than the availability indicator in the center column. The right column is about one-third of the content width, separated from the center column by a narrow gutter (8–16px) and ending with a small outer margin to the browser edge. The toggle appears as a compact, high-impact action group—likely a primary button or pill paired with a secondary control—spanning roughly 70–90% of the column’s width and centered horizontally within the column. It sits in its own small card or strip with around 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal internal padding. A small vertical gap (about 16–24px) below it leaves room for the registration-feedback-panel or the event-navigation-links to stack beneath, creating a columnar rhythm similar to the stacked images in the right lane of the gallery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block aligned to the top of the right column; no sticky behavior; on narrow screens it appears below the registration content as part of a single-column vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-registration-section lives directly beneath the event-availability-indicator in the center column, forming the upper portion of the central interactive block. When applicable (for eligible free workshops), it appears as a card that spans the full width of the center column, starting about 8–16px below the availability band. This section provides contextual text or a summary of the workshop and introduces the form below. It has 16–24px of internal padding and a height that is driven by its content, usually shorter than the form itself. Visually, it reads as the top half of a larger central tile, with the form attached below, reinforcing the idea that the center column is the main action hub. If the workshop is not applicable, this section either collapses entirely (allowing the form to be omitted) or is replaced by a minimal message, maintaining the same vertical position so the column structure remains coherent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the center column that conditionally appears or collapses; when hidden, subsequent components in the center column move upward to fill the space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-registration-form is anchored in the center column immediately below the workshop-registration-section, with a small vertical gap (about 16–24px) separating the two. It spans the full width of the center column and typically occupies the largest vertical space within that column, resembling a tall card analogous to a prominent central image in the original gallery. The form fields are arranged in a tight vertical stack with clear but compact spacing (8–16px between fields), keeping the overall card dense but legible. The form’s outer container has 16–24px padding on all sides and a slightly elevated dark background, clearly framed against the underlying black page. Primary submit actions are positioned at the bottom right of the form’s internal layout. This form card visually balances the left column’s event-meta-info in height, so that the three-column grid reads like a row of substantial tiles beneath the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the center column; scrolls with the page; on narrow screens, it becomes a full-width block in a single-column stack, following the workshop-registration-section (if present).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-feedback-panel is positioned in the right column, directly beneath the night-plan-toggle, with a small vertical gap (about 16px) between them. It spans most of the right column’s width (around 80–100% of the column width) and is relatively shallow in height compared to the form, typically resembling a notification bar or compact card. When active (e.g., after submitting the registration form), it becomes a highly noticeable strip of feedback—success, error, or informational—using color and glow to stand out against the black background while remaining physically constrained within the right column. Vertically, it aligns approximately with the mid-portion of the registration form in the center column, echoing the appearance of a second row of smaller tiles beneath the primary row in the gallery mockup. When inactive, this panel either collapses entirely (allowing the event-navigation-links to move up) or reduces to a minimal subtle placeholder.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the right column that appears, disappears, or changes height based on feedback state; its appearance pushes content below it downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-navigation-links sit at the bottom of the right column, beneath the registration-feedback-panel with a moderate vertical gap (about 24px) when the panel is visible, or directly beneath the night-plan-toggle when the feedback panel is absent. They form a compact list or cluster of text links (e.g., back to schedule, next event, related attractions), stacked vertically in 1–3 lines, using about 16–24px of internal padding if presented as a card. This block does not extend as tall as the form or meta-info card; instead, it occupies the lower right area of the grid and visually reads as the final tile in the right column. Its lower edge typically aligns close to or slightly above the bottom of the left and center column content, creating a visually cohesive bottom line to the three-column grid. On larger screens, a bit of extra black space (about 32px) appears below this component before reaching the bottom of the viewport or further page content, echoing the subtle negative space between gallery rows in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the bottom of the right column; on narrower screens, it becomes a full-width link cluster near the bottom of the vertically stacked layout, following other interactive components.

name: My Night Plan
title: My Night Plan
description: This page shows the user’s planned events for the evening in time order, highlights time conflicts, and lets the user adjust or clean up the plan.

core_features: Fetch and display the current My Night Plan using getNightPlan, showing all planned events with title, date/time, venue, duration, and overlap status.

Sort and group listed events chronologically by start_datetime (and by end_datetime as a secondary key if needed), while still supporting custom ordering for events that share the same start time via reorderNightPlan.
Highlight events that have scheduling conflicts using the has_time_overlap flag returned by getNightPlan, and optionally filter the view to show only overlapping items.
Allow users to remove individual events from My Night Plan by calling removeEventFromNightPlan and updating the displayed list accordingly.
Enable reordering of events that start at the same time (for example via drag-and-drop handles or “move up/down” controls) and persist the updated order using reorderNightPlan.
Provide navigation from each plan item to its event detail page via event.html?eventId={event_id}.
Offer a simple summary/utility bar showing the total number of events and the number of overlapping events, along with controls to filter overlaps and collapse past events.
Provide a printable view by preparing a clean list layout and triggering the browser’s print dialog from the page (no extra interfaces).
Provide a shareable link by exposing/copying the current page URL (including view-related query parameters) so that the same view state can be reopened.
user_workflows: View current plan and overlaps: 1) User navigates to night_plan.html (optionally with query parameters such as showOverlapsOnly or collapsePastEvents). 2) On load, the page reads URL parameters into internal view state. 3) The page calls getNightPlan with no parameters. 4) When the response arrives, the page stores night_plan_id and items, sorts them chronologically, and groups them by date. 5) The list is rendered, applying any filters implied by URL state (for example, hiding non-overlapping events if showOverlapsOnly=true). 6) Each item with has_time_overlap=true is visually marked as conflicting. If focusEventId is present in the URL, the matching item is highlighted and optionally scrolled into view.

Remove an event from My Night Plan: 1) In the list, the user activates a control associated with an event (for example, a remove button on a row). 2) The page identifies the corresponding night_plan_item_id. 3) The page optionally shows a confirmation step and, on confirmation, calls removeEventFromNightPlan with nightPlanItemId. 4) On success, the removed item is excluded from the local list and, if needed, focusEventId or other URL parameters referencing that event are cleared or updated. 5) The page recalculates and re-renders overlap markers and counts, and the summary bar updates to reflect the new totals.

Reorder events that share the same start time: 1) The user initiates a reorder interaction on an item (for example, dragging a handle or clicking a move-up/move-down control). 2) The page constrains reordering so that items cannot move across different start_datetime groups, ensuring the overall list remains chronological while the order within equal start times can change. 3) When the reordering gesture is completed, the page computes the new global ordered list of night_plan_item_id values and calls reorderNightPlan with orderedItemIds. 4) On success, the page updates the in-memory ordering to match the new order and re-renders the list. 5) If overlapping events are present in the same time block, their relative order now reflects the user’s chosen priority.

Navigate to event details from My Night Plan: 1) The user clicks the event title within a list row. 2) The page navigates to event.html with the query parameter eventId set to the item’s event_id. 3) The event.html page can then present full details and additional actions (outside the scope of this page). 4) When the user navigates back to night_plan.html, the page again applies getNightPlan and URL-based state so the view is consistent.

Print or share the plan: 1) The user clicks the print control in the toolbar. 2) The page ensures that the list is fully expanded or in a print-friendly state, then triggers the browser’s print dialog so the user can produce a hard copy or PDF of the current plan. 3) For sharing, the user clicks a share/link control. 4) The page constructs a URL based on the current location (including view-related query parameters such as showOverlapsOnly, collapsePastEvents, or focusEventId) and exposes it to the user (for example by copying it to the clipboard or presenting it in a selectable text field). 5) Anyone opening that URL while authenticated to the same account/session will see the same Night Plan and view configuration, fetched via getNightPlan and interpreted via the query parameters.
interactions: Initial data load: On page load or manual refresh, a controller component invokes getNightPlan. The response is used to populate page state with night_plan_id and the items array, which drives all subsequent rendering.

Time-based grouping and sorting: After getNightPlan returns, the page sorts items by start_datetime and then by end_datetime. Items with identical start_datetime are kept as a subgroup where custom ordering (persisted via reorderNightPlan) is respected for display. This sorting happens in memory and does not require additional interfaces.

Overlap filtering toggle: A control (such as a checkbox or toggle) bound to URL parameter showOverlapsOnly lets the user switch between viewing all events and viewing only those with has_time_overlap=true. Changing this control updates internal state and also updates the URL query string via navigation APIs, without reloading the page. The list re-renders according to the new filter.

Collapse past events toggle: A control bound to URL parameter collapsePastEvents lets the user hide or collapse items whose end_datetime is earlier than the current time. When toggled, the page recalculates which items are considered past, collapses or hides them in the view, and updates the URL parameter to persist this preference for the current link.

Focus on a specific event: If a user opens the page with focusEventId in the query string, the page highlights the corresponding item and may auto-scroll to it. A user can also set focus by clicking an action on an item; this updates the focusEventId parameter in the URL and the highlight state in the list.

Remove event action: Each list item exposes a remove action. When activated, the page determines the associated night_plan_item_id and calls removeEventFromNightPlan. On a successful response, the item is removed from the in-memory list, overlaps are recalculated, and the view is updated. On failure, an error message is displayed without altering the list.

Reorder events interaction: Within each equal-start-time group, each item exposes a drag handle or move control. When the user completes a reorder, the page composes orderedItemIds as an array of all night_plan_item_id values in their new global order and calls reorderNightPlan. The page then updates local order optimistically and confirms or rolls back based on the success flag in the response.
Navigate to event details: Clicking an event title triggers a navigation to event.html with eventId set to the item’s event_id. No additional API call is made from this page for that navigation.

Print interaction: Clicking the print control prepares a view state suitable for printing (for example, ensuring all date groups are expanded) and then invokes the browser’s print dialog. No additional backend calls or interfaces are used for printing.

Share link interaction: Clicking the share control reads the current window.location (including query parameters), optionally normalizes query order, and then exposes this URL to the user. This action updates no backend state but allows the current Night Plan view configuration to be re-opened via the URL alone.

Empty state navigation: If getNightPlan returns an items array that is empty, the page shows an empty state component. This component offers navigation to other pages (such as the schedule or events listing) using standard links (for example, schedule.html) without invoking additional interfaces from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters to control view-related state, while always obtaining underlying data via getNightPlan. Key parameters include: showOverlapsOnly (optional, values such as 'true' or 'false'), collapsePastEvents (optional, values such as 'true' or 'false'), and focusEventId (optional, equal to an Event.id from one of the NightPlan items). On initial load, the page reads these parameters and initializes internal view state accordingly before or immediately after invoking getNightPlan. The data model (night_plan_id and items) itself is not driven by URL; getNightPlan always returns the current user’s NightPlan, and any plan-specific identity (night_plan_id) is handled internally by the backend. When the user toggles showOverlapsOnly, the page updates internal state and uses history.pushState or history.replaceState to synchronize the showOverlapsOnly parameter in the URL without reloading the page. The displayed list is filtered in-memory to include only items with has_time_overlap=true when the flag is active. Similarly, collapsePastEvents controls whether items whose end_datetime is earlier than the current time are hidden or collapsed; toggling this updates both in-memory state and the URL parameter. The focusEventId parameter is used to apply a visual focus and optional scrolling to a specific item; it can be set either from an incoming URL or by user action, and is reflected in the URL so that the same focused view can be restored on reload or share. Reordering and removal do not introduce new query parameters; these actions update backend data via reorderNightPlan and removeEventFromNightPlan and are reflected in the view through updated in-memory data and recalculated overlaps. URL state never stores raw NightPlanItem arrays or timestamps, only lightweight flags and identifiers, ensuring the page always rehydrates from getNightPlan and then applies view transformations based on the current query parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the My Night Plan page, parses URL parameters for view configuration, and fetches the current NightPlan data using getNightPlan. Stores night_plan_id and items in page-level state and triggers downstream components to render.

Reads and writes query parameters that control the view (such as showOverlapsOnly, collapsePastEvents, focusEventId). Provides a structured interface for other components to react to URL changes and to update the URL without full page reloads.

Displays summary information about the Night Plan (total number of items, number of overlapping items) and provides controls for filtering overlaps, collapsing past events, printing, and sharing the current view.

Calculates and displays the count of events with time overlaps and optionally a brief explanatory note about what constitutes an overlap. Used to give users a quick sense of where conflicts exist in their Night Plan.

Renders the list of NightPlan items grouped by date and sorted by start_datetime (then end_datetime). Applies in-memory filters based on showOverlapsOnly and collapsePastEvents, and highlights the item corresponding to focusEventId from URL state. Exposes reorder interactions within same-start-time groups and delegates item-level actions to subcomponents.

Represents a single NightPlan item row showing event title, time range, venue_name, duration_minutes, and an overlap indicator when has_time_overlap is true. Provides controls to navigate to the event detail page and to remove the event from the plan. If the item matches focusEventId, it applies a focused style.

Coordinates reordering of items within groups that share the same start_datetime. Receives proposed new ordering (for example, after a drag-and-drop operation) from the list component, assembles a global orderedItemIds array of night_plan_item_id values, calls reorderNightPlan, and updates local order based on the response.

Handles removal of a NightPlan item. Accepts a night_plan_item_id from an item row, optionally performs a confirmation step, calls removeEventFromNightPlan, and removes the item from local state on success. Also triggers recalculation of overlaps and can clear focusEventId if it referenced the removed event.

Displays a dedicated view when getNightPlan returns an empty items array. Provides contextual text explaining that no events are in the Night Plan and offers navigation links to pages where events can be added (such as the main schedule page) using standard URL navigation.

Implements the logic for printing and sharing the Night Plan. For printing, prepares the current list view to be fully visible and invokes the browser’s print dialog. For sharing, reads the current URL (including query parameters) and exposes it to the user, for example by copying it to the clipboard or making it selectable.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly vertical, hero-first arrangement. At the very top, a large, cinematic hero section establishes the 'My Night Plan' atmosphere and anchors the page title. Directly beneath it, a thin layer of functional UI appears in a stacked sequence: first a conflict/overlap summary banner, then a compact toolbar for organizing the plan. Below this control band, the main content is presented as a dense, 3-column grid of night-plan items, visually echoing the mockup's photo gallery. There are no sidebars; everything flows straight down the page: hero → summary → controls → plan grid (or an empty-state panel when there are no items). Non-visual controllers and managers operate at the page level, without occupying visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly vertical, hero-first arrangement. At the very top, a large, cinematic hero section establishes the 'My Night Plan' atmosphere and anchors the page title. Directly beneath it, a thin layer of functional UI appears in a stacked sequence: first a conflict/overlap summary banner, then a compact toolbar for organizing the plan. Below this control band, the main content is presented as a dense, 3-column grid of night-plan items, visually echoing the mockup's photo gallery. There are no sidebars; everything flows straight down the page: hero → summary → controls → plan grid (or an empty-state panel when there are no items). Non-visual controllers and managers operate at the page level, without occupying visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full content width, with minimal left/right margins to prioritize image immersion. The overlap summary and toolbar together occupy about 15–20% of the initial viewport height as a stacked strip below the hero. The remaining space is dominated by the night-plan list, which fills the page with a tight 3-column grid of image-backed cards. Vertical spacing between hero, summary, toolbar, and grid is modest—generally in the 24–48px range—so the page feels like one continuous, dark canvas with discrete bands of content rather than widely separated sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense, mirroring the mockup. Large imagery and cards occupy most of the horizontal space, with narrow black gutters (8–16px) between columns and from the page edge. Text and controls sit directly on top of or immediately adjacent to imagery, with minimal whitespace. The density is controlled by consistent gutters and clear card boundaries rather than large blank areas. When the list is populated, most of the viewport below the toolbar is filled with cards in a regular grid; when empty, a single cinematic empty-state panel occupies that space so the page never feels sparse.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the neon-style 'My Night Plan' title centered in the hero image. From there, the eye moves downward to the high-contrast overlap summary banner, which uses accent colors to highlight conflicts. The toolbar just below invites the next action (filtering, sorting, cleaning up the plan). Once past the control band, attention shifts into a left-to-right scan of the top row of night-plan cards, then down row by row through the grid. Subtle highlights (e.g., glowing borders for overlapping events, hover overlays for actions) guide the user to problem areas while maintaining the overall top-to-bottom, then left-to-right scanning pattern of the mockup’s image gallery.

layout.overall_description: The page is a dark, cinematic canvas: a full-width hero image fills the top half of the screen, drenched in neon horror lighting, with the 'My Night Plan' title glowing at the center. The hero stretches almost edge-to-edge horizontally, framed only by very slim black margins, and establishes the event’s atmosphere before any controls appear. As you scroll slightly, the hero gives way to a tight stack of functional UI floating on black—first a centered overlap summary banner, then a full-width toolbar. Both are shallow, horizontally oriented bands that barely interrupt the immersion. Beneath them, the screen fills with a 3-column grid of tall, image-backed event cards, each occupying roughly one-third of the width with narrow black gutters between them. Time conflicts glow subtly in red around problematic cards, while drag and remove affordances appear as minimal controls at the edges of each card so the imagery remains dominant. When there are no planned events, a single, wide empty-state panel sits where the grid would be, maintaining the cinematic feel with a large illustration and call-to-action. The entire layout feels like a landing page turned into a planner: vertical, image-driven, dense but legible, with the user’s night unfolding visually from top hero to the grid of experiences below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, page-level initializer. Conceptually, it wraps the entire viewport, ensuring that the hero, summary, toolbar, and grid render only after data has been loaded. When loading, it temporarily overlays the main canvas with a dark, semi-opaque layer that covers the full viewport (100% width and height). At the center of where the hero will appear, a small, cinematic loading indicator (e.g., a pulsing neon circle or flickering text) appears, occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport width, leaving the rest of the background almost entirely black to preserve the dark, immersive tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-screen overlay while active; otherwise non-visual and does not occupy space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is entirely non-visual and has no direct spatial footprint. It operates in the background, synchronizing the current night plan (selected items, ordering, and filter state) with the URL. Conceptually, it spans the whole page context, but it does not alter the arrangement or sizing of visible components in any way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, page-scoped logic with no layout impact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The night-plan toolbar is a low-profile horizontal strip positioned directly beneath the overlap summary and above the night-plan grid. It spans nearly the full content width—approximately 90–94% of the browser width—centered, leaving slim black margins (24–32px) on the left and right. Vertically, it is compact, about 64–80px tall, matching roughly one and a half times the base spacing scale, so it feels like a band rather than a standalone card. The toolbar background is a slightly lighter black or translucent dark overlay, visually distinct from the pure black page background but still subdued compared to the image-heavy sections. Controls (filters, sorting, clear plan, etc.) are arranged left-to-right within this band, with 16–24px padding inside the bar; important actions are grouped toward the left to align with the start of the main content grid below. The bottom edge of the toolbar aligns visually with the top edge of the first row of cards, separated by a modest 24–32px vertical gap to maintain the page’s continuous, stacked rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a normal block element; no fixed or sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overlap summary appears as a prominent but shallow banner directly below the hero and above the toolbar, acting like the first 'thumbnail row' after the hero in the original mockup. It is horizontally centered, spanning about 70–80% of the content width, with equal black margins on both sides so it reads as a single, featured strip. Its height is modest—roughly 56–72px—making it feel like a concise alert rather than a full section. The background uses a dark red or desaturated crimson overlay on black, with subtle glow or border to highlight its importance while still fitting the horror theme. Text inside is centered vertically and aligned slightly left of center horizontally, with 16–24px internal padding. The vertical gap between the bottom of the hero and the top of the overlap summary is about 32–48px, small enough that the summary still feels visually connected to the hero. When there are no conflicts, the banner can shrink slightly in height or mute its color but remains in place to keep the vertical rhythm consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal block element in the vertical flow, expanding or contracting slightly based on content but maintaining its horizontal centering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The night-plan list occupies the entire main content area below the toolbar, visually functioning as the mockup’s 3-column photo gallery. It stretches from just under the toolbar downwards, with a top margin of 32px that clearly separates it from the toolbar while keeping them visually connected. Horizontally, it fits within the core content width—roughly 90–94% of the viewport—centered, with narrow black gutters (12–16px) between columns and from the page edges. The list renders its children as a strict 3-column grid on desktop: each item spans one column (about 1/3 of the content width), and all cards in a row share the same height for a clean mosaic effect. A typical card height might be 240–280px to preserve a cinematic, poster-like feel. Vertically, rows are separated by 16–24px of black space. On smaller screens, the list responsively collapses to 2 columns, then 1 column, but the sense of dense, image-driven tiles remains. The list continues down the page as long as there are items, so scrolled views show repeated rows of these cards stacked tightly, maintaining the gallery-like rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid container that flows vertically; no fixed behavior, but internally manages wrapping into 3 → 2 → 1 columns based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each night-plan item row is visualized as a cinematic card in the grid, occupying one cell of the 3-column layout. The card spans approximately 1/3 of the content width (4 columns in a 12-column grid), with 8–16px gutters separating it from neighboring cards. It has no external padding so the imagery can reach almost to the card edges, but there is a slight inner border or shadow to distinguish the card from the black background. The card height stays consistent across all items in a row—for example, about 260px—to maintain a uniform mosaic. The card background is a dark, high-contrast image representative of the event (zombie maze, haunted ride, etc.), dimmed slightly to ensure text legibility. At the top-left, a time pill or label sits over the image, using 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, with a neon accent color. Near the bottom of the card, a short title and location/time details are overlaid in a gradient fade—text is left-aligned, with 16px padding from the left and bottom edges. Cards with time conflicts feature a subtle but clear visual treatment: a glowing red border or underlight around the card perimeter, and possibly a small conflict icon near the time pill. On hover or focus, the card darkens slightly, and action controls (reorder handle and remove icon, provided by their respective controllers) fade in adjacent to the edges, preserving the clean, image-driven look at rest.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grid item that maintains fixed aspect ratio/height per row; responds to hover/focus with slight visual elevation or glow but remains within its grid cell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reorder controller is integrated visually into each night-plan item card as an unobtrusive drag handle. Within each card, the handle appears near the lower-right or lower-left edge (choose a consistent side, e.g., right), aligned about 16px from the card’s edge and 16px above the bottom text overlay. It occupies a narrow vertical strip (approximately 24–32px wide and 40–48px tall), using a subtle icon pattern (e.g., vertical dots or small bars) in a muted gray. At rest, its opacity is low so it barely interrupts the cinematic image. On hover over the card or during reordering, the handle brightens and gets a faint neon outline, clearly indicating draggability without changing the card’s size. Dragging a card causes it to float above the grid with a slightly enlarged shadow, while the remaining cards slide to indicate the potential drop position. All of this behavior is constrained within the main list area; the controller does not extend beyond the card’s grid cell.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlaid within each card as part of the card’s interactive layer; participates in drag-and-drop within the list’s grid, temporarily lifting the card above the normal flow while dragging.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove controller appears as a small, circular icon button layered at the top-right corner of each night-plan item card. It slightly overlaps the card’s image area, positioned about 12–16px in from the top and right edges. The button is roughly 28–32px in diameter, with a dark, semi-transparent background and a high-contrast icon (e.g., an ‘X’ or trash symbol) in a neon accent color. When the card is idle, the button’s opacity is moderate to low; on hover or when the card is focused, it becomes fully opaque and may gain a subtle red glow to emphasize its destructive action. The button does not increase the card’s dimensions; instead, it sits as a floating element above the image content, ensuring that the overall grid remains tight and aligned while still making item removal easy and visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlaid, absolute-positioned within each card boundary; scrolls with the card and does not affect the card’s layout or grid alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no items in the night plan, the empty state replaces the usual grid within the night-plan list area. It is a large, cinematic panel centered horizontally below the toolbar, spanning about 60–70% of the content width and occupying roughly 35–45% of the viewport height, so it visually echoes the hero but on a smaller scale. The panel’s background is a dark illustration or photograph aligned with the zombie event theme, dimmed with a black overlay. At the center of the panel, a concise message (e.g., 'Your night is wide open') and a primary call-to-action button appear, aligned vertically and horizontally, with generous internal padding (24–32px around text and controls) to keep it readable. The panel is separated from the toolbar above by about 48px of vertical space, and from the bottom of the viewport by flexible space that grows or shrinks with screen height, maintaining visual balance. Because it occupies the space where the grid would normally go, the page still feels full and intentional rather than empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Single, centered block that appears only when the list is empty; otherwise hidden so the grid can occupy the area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print-and-share controller is presented as a compact cluster of icon buttons located in the upper-right region of the hero section, overlaid directly on the hero image. It sits approximately 24–32px in from the top and right edges of the content area, aligned horizontally as 2–3 small icons (print, share) with 8–12px spacing between them. Each icon is housed in a circular or pill-shaped button about 32–40px in size, with translucent dark backgrounds and bright neon icons to stand out against the imagery. On hover, these buttons intensify their glow slightly but remain understated compared to the central title. Once the user scrolls past the hero, this controller can optionally adopt a subtle sticky behavior: it shrinks slightly and pins near the top-right of the viewport, just above or aligned with the overlap summary, so print/share actions remain accessible without disrupting the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially overlaid on the hero image; may become a small, sticky cluster near the top-right of the viewport after the hero scrolls out of view, maintaining consistent right-side alignment with minimal intrusion.

name: Favorites / Saved Attractions
title: Saved Attractions
description: This page lets users view and manage their saved attractions, apply simple filters (via URL parameters), open attraction detail pages to book tickets, and remove items from their favorites list.

core_features: Load the user’s saved attractions by calling getFavoritesList, optionally passing the tag_filter URL parameter to restrict the results.
Display the returned favorites as a list of attraction entries, showing summary data such as attraction type, scare level, age rating, kid-friendly flag, tags, base price from, and venue name.
Provide in-page filtering controls that update the tag_filter parameter in the URL and trigger a fresh call to getFavoritesList using the new filter.
Allow users to remove an attraction from their favorites by calling removeAttractionFromFavorites with the attractionId, then updating the displayed list and favorites count based on the response.
Enable direct navigation from any saved attraction entry to its detail page (attraction.html) by passing attractionId and ref=favorites as URL parameters.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for the favorites list based on the results of getFavoritesList and removeAttractionFromFavorites.
user_workflows:
Viewing all saved attractions:
1. User navigates to favorites.html without specifying URL parameters.
2. The page controller reads the current URL, detects that tag_filter is not set, and calls getFavoritesList with no tag_filter value.
3. While the data is loading, a loading indicator is shown.
4. On successful response, the favorites list component receives the favorites array and renders each attraction entry.
5. The summary bar displays the total number of favorites based on the length of the favorites array.

Opening a saved attraction to book tickets:
1. From the favorites list, the user clicks on a saved attraction entry (or its primary action area).
2. The click handler reads the attraction_id of the selected favorite.
3. The browser navigates to attraction.html with URL parameters attractionId={attraction_id} and ref=favorites.
4. The destination page can use the ref parameter to adjust its own behavior or tracking (outside the scope of this page).

Filtering favorites using a tag_filter from another page:
1. On tickets.html or index.html, the user activates a control that links to favorites.html with a specific tag_filter in the URL (for example, favorites.html?tag_filter=someTag).
2. The user arrives on favorites.html; the page controller parses tag_filter from the URL and passes it to getFavoritesList(tag_filter).
3. The filter bar initializes its UI to reflect the active tag_filter value from the URL.
4. The favorites list shows only attractions that match the tag_filter according to the response from getFavoritesList.
5. If the user clears or changes the filter from the filter bar, the URL is updated (tag_filter changed or removed), and getFavoritesList is called again with the new value.

Changing filters directly on the Favorites page:
1. The user interacts with the filter bar (for example, selecting a tag-based filter from available options derived from the current favorites dataset).
2. The filter bar’s change handler updates the browser URL to set or change tag_filter to the selected value (or remove the parameter for no filter) without reloading the entire site shell where possible.
3. The page controller listens for URL changes, re-reads the tag_filter value, and calls getFavoritesList(tag_filter) again.
4. The favorites list and summary bar update based on the new dataset.
5. The back/forward browser buttons navigate between previous tag_filter states because the filter is encoded in the URL.

Removing an attraction from favorites:
1. In the favorites list, the user clicks a remove action for a specific attraction.
2. The remove action component reads that attraction’s attraction_id and calls removeAttractionFromFavorites(attractionId).
3. While the request is in progress, the remove action may show a loading state or disable the button for that item.
4. On success, the response includes success=true and an updated favorites_count.
5. The page controller removes that attraction from the in-memory favorites array and updates the summary bar using the favorites_count from the response.
6. If the list becomes empty, the empty-state component is shown instead of the list.
7. If the request fails, an error message is displayed and the item remains in the list.

Handling an empty favorites list:
1. The user navigates to favorites.html and getFavoritesList returns an empty favorites array.
2. The page controller detects that favorites.length is zero.
3. Instead of the favorites list, the empty-state component is rendered, indicating there are no saved attractions.
4. The empty-state component can expose navigation links (for example, to index.html or tickets.html) so the user can discover attractions to add to favorites (the actual adding is handled on other pages).
interactions: Page load / initialization: on page load, the controller reads tag_filter from the URL and triggers getFavoritesList(tag_filter) to populate the initial state.

Filter selection: when the user selects or changes a filter in the filter bar, an onFilterChange handler updates the tag_filter query parameter in the URL and triggers a new call to getFavoritesList with the updated tag_filter.
Clear filter: when the user uses a control to clear the active filter, the filter bar removes tag_filter from the URL and the controller calls getFavoritesList with no tag_filter parameter.

Open attraction detail: clicking on a favorites card (or a dedicated "view" area) triggers onCardClick, which builds a URL to attraction.html including attractionId and ref=favorites and then navigates the browser to that URL.

Remove from favorites: clicking the remove control on a card triggers onClickRemove, which calls removeAttractionFromFavorites with the card’s attraction_id, then updates the UI based on the API response.

Error handling: if getFavoritesList or removeAttractionFromFavorites returns an error or cannot be completed, the controller sets an error state that a generic error component or message area can display; users can then retry the failed action.

Browser navigation: using the browser’s back and forward buttons changes the URL (including tag_filter); the controller’s onURLChange logic re-runs getFavoritesList with the current tag_filter so the visible favorites list always reflects the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary explicit state for this page is carried via URL parameters, specifically the tag_filter query parameter. When favorites.html loads or when the URL changes, the page controller parses window.location.search to determine the tag_filter value (if present). This value is passed directly into getFavoritesList(tag_filter). If tag_filter is absent, null, or an empty string, getFavoritesList is called without a filter, meaning all favorites should be returned. The filter bar reads the same URL parameter to determine which filter option is currently active.

User actions that change filters do not modify any localStorage or cookies; instead, they update the browser URL by adding, changing, or removing the tag_filter parameter (for example, favorites.html?tag_filter=someTag). After the URL is updated, the controller responds to the URL change event, re-extracts tag_filter, and re-calls getFavoritesList to refresh the displayed favorites.

The in-memory page state maintained by the controller includes: (1) a loading flag for getFavoritesList requests, (2) an error object or flag if a request fails, (3) the current favorites array returned by getFavoritesList, and (4) a transient removingId or similar flag when removeAttractionFromFavorites is in progress for a specific attraction. These in-memory states are reset or updated whenever tag_filter changes.

When removeAttractionFromFavorites(attractionId) returns successfully, the controller updates the favorites array by removing the attraction with that id and optionally reconciles the length with favorites_count returned from the API. If the list becomes empty, the empty-state component is shown instead of the list. These updates are kept in memory only for the current page view; if the user refreshes or navigates away and returns, the state is re-derived from the APIs and URL parameters, not from any localStorage.

components.functionality: Coordinates the entire Saved Attractions page lifecycle: parses URL parameters, calls getFavoritesList with the current tag_filter, holds the favorites dataset and loading/error states in memory, passes data to child components, and handles updates after removeAttractionFromFavorites calls.

Provides controls for narrowing the favorites list by a tag-based filter. It initializes from the current URL tag_filter value and, when the user changes the filter, updates the URL accordingly so the controller can re-fetch data using getFavoritesList(tag_filter). The available filter options can be derived from tag values present in the current favorites dataset or from a predefined set of tag codes understood by the backend.

Displays a summary of the current favorites set, such as the total number of saved attractions currently visible under the active tag_filter, and a textual representation of the active filter. It updates whenever the favorites dataset changes (new getFavoritesList response or removeAttractionFromFavorites success) or when the tag_filter parameter changes in the URL.

Renders the collection of saved attractions returned by getFavoritesList as a structured list. For each item, it delegates to the favorite-card component to display attraction details and actions. It handles empty vs non-empty logic in conjunction with the empty-state component and triggers navigation to attraction.html when a card is activated.

Represents a single saved attraction entry. It shows the attraction’s name, attraction_type, scare_level, age_rating, is_kid_friendly value, tags, base_price_from, and venue_name as provided by getFavoritesList. It exposes actions to open the attraction detail page and to remove the attraction from favorites.

Encapsulates the logic for removing a specific attraction from the favorites collection. When triggered, it calls removeAttractionFromFavorites with the bound attractionId, manages a per-item loading/disabled state while waiting for the response, and notifies the page controller so the favorites list and summary can be updated.

Displays when getFavoritesList returns an empty favorites array. It informs the user that no attractions are currently saved and can provide navigation links to other pages (such as index.html or tickets.html) where users can discover attractions to add to their favorites list.

Shows a loading or busy state when the favorites list is being retrieved or when a removal operation is in progress. It appears while getFavoritesList is awaiting a response and can also indicate per-item loading when removeAttractionFromFavorites is called for a specific attraction.

Displays error messages when getFavoritesList or removeAttractionFromFavorites encounters an error. It shows context-appropriate feedback (for example, a list-level error if favorites fail to load, or an item-level error if a remove action fails) and may offer an option to retry.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict vertical stack mirroring the mockup: a dominant, full-width hero at the top (owned by the page controller), with the summary bar and filter bar integrated into the lower band of this hero, followed by a three-column, image-driven favorites gallery that fills the rest of the page. There are no alternating text/content sections; everything after the hero is an immersive grid of favorite attraction cards.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict vertical stack mirroring the mockup: a dominant, full-width hero at the top (owned by the page controller), with the summary bar and filter bar integrated into the lower band of this hero, followed by a three-column, image-driven favorites gallery that fills the rest of the page. There are no alternating text/content sections; everything after the hero is an immersive grid of favorite attraction cards.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans nearly the full browser width, with only a small margin to the edges. Immediately below, the favorites grid takes over the remaining viewport height and extends downward as needed. Gutters between cards are narrow (about 8–16px), and the vertical gap between the bottom of the hero and the top row of cards is modest (around 32–48px), reading as a shallow band of pure black. Controls (summary and filters) occupy a low-profile strip within the bottom quarter of the hero, so they don’t need a separate section height.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense and image-centric: the hero and favorite cards use almost all available width, with slim black gutters defining structure. Text elements (summary, filters, card labels) are minimal, overlaid on imagery or sitting directly on the black background. There is just enough spacing (24–32px between rows, 8–16px between columns) to avoid clutter while keeping the screen feeling full and immersive.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is top-to-bottom, then left-to-right. The eye is first pulled into the bright, cinematic hero at the top center (page title and thematic imagery). From there, attention drops to the bottom of the hero where the summary and filters sit as a subtle control layer. As the user scrolls, focus shifts into the favorites grid, scanning each row of three cards from left to right. Within each card, the user’s eye moves from the image to the title overlay, then to the small remove icon in the upper-right corner. Sticky behavior for the filter strip on scroll reinforces a top-anchor, then grid-exploration flow without adding heavy chrome.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a dark, cinematic wall of imagery. At the very top, a wide, high-contrast hero spans nearly edge to edge, consuming roughly half the viewport height and showcasing a gritty zombie-event visual with an overlaid favorites title. Along the lower third of this hero, a thin, semi-transparent black band contains the favorites summary on the left and filter controls on the right, both rendered in minimal, high-contrast type and pills that feel like part of the image, not separate UI. Beneath this, after a shallow strip of black negative space, the favorites grid begins: rows of three equally sized, poster-like attraction cards covering most of the width, with tight black gutters between them. The cards form a uniform mosaic that can extend down the page as the user scrolls. Interaction states (loading, empty, error) replace the grid area while preserving the same dark backdrop and central alignment, so the overall impression remains that of an immersive, image-first favorites wall anchored by a single powerful hero at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-viewport, edge-to-edge canvas with a pure black background. It centers the content horizontally with a generous max-width (around 1200–1440px) while allowing the hero image to visually hug the browser edges with only small lateral margins (about 16–24px on each side).
