Vertically, it stacks the hero at the top, then the gallery area beneath it in a continuous flow, with no visible framing or carded page sections. The underlying structure uses an implicit 12-column grid: the hero spans all 12 columns, and the favorites list below uses three equal-width groups of four columns. All other components (summary bar, filter bar, states, etc.) are positioned within this grid and on top of the dark backdrop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the structural container for the entire page; scrolls normally with the document, maintaining a continuous dark backdrop without fixed headers or footers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits in the lower band of the hero, aligned to the right side of the central content area. It shares a horizontal strip with the summary bar: together they form a low-profile control row spanning roughly 70–80% of the content width, centered within the hero and positioned about 16–24px above the hero’s bottom edge. Within this strip, the filter bar occupies the right half, with filter pills or dropdowns laid out horizontally and right-aligned, leaving a comfortable 24–32px gap between the last pill and the content edge. Vertical height for this strip is compact (about 48px total), so it reads more like an overlay on the hero rather than a separate section. The controls float over the hero image on a slightly translucent black background, ensuring legibility while preserving the cinematic feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially overlays the lower portion of the hero. As the user scrolls past the hero’s bottom edge, the filter bar (together with the summary bar) becomes a thin sticky strip pinned to the top of the viewport, spanning the same central width. In sticky mode, it sits on a solid black background, maintaining access to filters without consuming excessive vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary bar shares the same horizontal strip as the filter bar, occupying the left half of this control band in the lower third of the hero. It is left-aligned within the central content width, starting roughly 16–24px from the left edge of that width. It displays concise text such as the count of saved attractions in a single or two-line layout. The text sits on the same translucent black overlay as the filters, maintaining a unified, subtle band above the hero’s bottom edge. The vertical padding around the summary text is minimal (8–12px), matching the compact feel of the filter bar. Visually, it feels like an understated HUD element floating over the hero image rather than a chunky header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Moves in tandem with the filter bar: it starts as an overlay at the bottom of the hero and then snaps into a sticky horizontal strip at the top of the viewport once the hero scrolls out of view, staying locked to the top alongside the filter bar while the grid scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list begins just below the hero, separated by a narrow band of pure black background approximately 32–48px tall that visually buffers the hero from the grid. Within the central content width, the list arranges favorite cards into a three-column grid: each card occupies roughly one-third of the available width (four columns of the 12-column grid), with consistent 8–16px gutters horizontally between cards and 24–32px gaps vertically between rows. On a typical desktop viewport, the top of the first row aligns slightly below the hero’s bottom edge, forming a clean, straight line of three cards across. As the user scrolls, additional rows appear in a continuous vertical stack, preserving the uniform mosaic appearance. The list itself has no visible frame; it’s defined entirely by the alignment and spacing of the cards against the black background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally as a long, vertically expanding grid. On narrower viewports, the grid responsively collapses from three columns to two and then one, while preserving consistent gutters and the same relative top margin beneath the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite card is a cinematic, poster-like tile that fills its grid cell from left to right, with no internal padding and a full-bleed attraction image. The card’s width is about one third of the central content area, and its height follows a fixed aspect ratio (around 3:4 or 4:5), so all cards in a row share the same height for a tight, uniform grid. The image dominates the card; a dark gradient overlay from bottom upwards (covering roughly the lower 25–30% of the card) contains the attraction name and a minimal subtitle or label, left-aligned with 16–24px of horizontal padding. The top edge of the gradient remains soft to preserve the gritty, cinematic style. The card’s edges have just a hairline separation from adjacent cards via the 8–16px gutters, letting the black background form subtle frames between tiles. On hover, the card may slightly brighten or show an overlay call-to-action to view details, but in its default state it reads primarily as an image tile with minimal text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard grid item within the favorites list, resizing responsively with the grid. The card’s interactive layers (hover states, remove button, click-through area) remain overlaid on the image without affecting its size or the surrounding grid alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove action appears as a small, icon-based button overlaid in the upper-right corner of each favorite card, floating above the image rather than occupying layout space. It sits inset from the card’s top and right edges by about 12–16px, with a circular or pill-shaped hit area roughly 32–40px in diameter. The button uses high-contrast styling (e.g., a dim red accent or pale icon on a nearly black circular background) that is clearly visible but does not overpower the card’s imagery. The remove control aligns consistently across all cards, forming a neat diagonal line when multiple cards are viewed in a row. On hover over the card, the remove action can slightly increase in opacity or brightness to signal interactivity, but its position remains fixed relative to the card’s top-right corner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position relative to each card’s top-right corner, overlaying the image. It does not affect the card’s size or the grid layout, and it scrolls naturally with the card as part of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no favorites, the hero remains at the top as usual. Directly below, instead of the standard three-column grid, the empty state occupies the same region allocated to the list, centered both horizontally and vertically within the first viewport of that space. It appears as a single, wider panel roughly equal to the width of two grid columns (about 60–70% of the content width), aligned to the center. The panel sits on the black background and may include a subtle illustrative image or icon plus a short message and a call-to-action, stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. There is ample breathing room around the panel (at least 48px of black space on all sides), so it stands out clearly against the otherwise image-heavy layout while still feeling like part of the same dark, cinematic world.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the favorites list region when active. It scrolls naturally, but in most cases will fit entirely within the initial post-hero viewport, minimizing the need to scroll when the state is empty.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: During loading, the hero still appears at the top, but the area where the grid would normally render is occupied by a single, centered loading indicator. This indicator sits just below the hero, aligned to the center of the content width, and is vertically offset from the hero’s bottom edge by about 32–48px. The spinner or progress animation is compact (roughly the size of a small card title area) and may be paired with a short loading label beneath it, with 8–12px spacing. Surrounding it is empty black space stretching across the width, preserving the clean, cinematic feel while making the loading state obvious without adding heavy UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the visible cards within the favorites list area while data is loading. It scrolls with the page, but generally appears within the first fold of the list region so users notice it immediately after the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the event of an error, a concise error message panel appears at the top of the favorites list area, directly beneath the hero and any sticky filter/summary strip. The panel is horizontally centered and spans roughly the width of two cards (about 50–60% of the content width), with 16–24px padding on all sides and a slightly lighter, textured dark background to distinguish it from the pure black page. The message text is left-aligned within the panel, and any retry button is aligned to the right or stacked below, with 12–16px of internal spacing. Below this panel, there is either empty black space (if no content can be shown) or a dimmed placeholder grid, but the error panel itself is the first element the eye encounters after the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in place of or directly above the favorites list grid. It scrolls with the list content, remaining at the top of the grid region rather than being fixed, ensuring it’s part of the natural reading order just below the hero.

name: Map & Parking
title: Map & Parking
description: Page for locating venues on an interactive map, finding nearby parking areas, viewing their details, and adding parking passes to the shopping cart.

core_features: Venue search using a map search bar that calls searchVenuesOnMap(query) and highlights matching venue pins on the map.
Map viewport centered on a selected venue (via click or URL parameter) with a venue details panel populated by getVenueDetail(venueId).

‘Find nearby parking’ action for the selected venue using findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters), with results shown as a structured parking list and optionally associated with map context.
Distance-based parking filter (max_distance_meters) that updates the parking results list and URL parameter without page reload.
Parking area details panel using getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId), showing pricing, restrictions, accessibility, and capacity state.
Parking pass options form (date and arrival_time) tied to the selected parking area, submitting to addParkingPassToCart(parkingAreaId, date, arrival_time).
Post-add-to-cart confirmation state that exposes a ‘View Cart’ navigation control linking to cart.html with highlightItemId and from=parking parameters.
Deep linking via URL parameters (venueId, parkingAreaId, max_distance_meters) to preselect a venue, focus a parking area, and apply a default distance filter.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Find parking for a venue from a deep link (venueId in URL): 1) User arrives on map_parking.html?venueId=... optionally with max_distance_meters=...; 2) Page reads venueId and calls getVenueDetail(venueId); 3) Map centers on the venue and shows a venue pin; 4) Venue details panel opens with data from getVenueDetail; 5) User clicks ‘Find nearby parking’; 6) Page uses current max_distance_meters state (from URL or default UI selection) and calls findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters); 7) Parking list panel populates with results; 8) User adjusts distance filter (if desired), which updates max_distance_meters in the URL and triggers a refetch via findNearbyParkingAreas; 9) User selects a parking area from the list, opening parking details via getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId); 10) User opens the parking pass form, selects date and arrival_time, and submits; 11) Page calls addParkingPassToCart(parkingAreaId, date, arrival_time); 12) On success, the page shows confirmation with updated cart summary and enables a ‘View Cart’ button that navigates to cart.html?highlightItemId={cart_item_id}&from=parking.

Workflow 2 – Search for a venue by name and then book parking: 1) User visits map_parking.html with no venueId; 2) User types a venue name in the search bar and submits; 3) Page calls searchVenuesOnMap(query) and renders a list of matching venues plus map pins for those venues; 4) User selects a venue either from the search results list or by clicking its map pin; 5) Page stores selectedVenueId in internal state, updates URL parameter venueId, and calls getVenueDetail(venueId) to populate the venue details panel; 6) User clicks ‘Find nearby parking’ or the parking tab in the venue panel; 7) Page reads max_distance_meters from URL or the distance filter control, then calls findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters); 8) Parking list shows available areas; user may sort/filter locally (e.g., by distance or accessibility); 9) User clicks a specific parking area to view details via getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId); 10) User opens the parking pass form, sets date and arrival_time, and submits; 11) Page calls addParkingPassToCart and, on success, updates any cart indicator and shows a ‘View Cart’ button that navigates to cart.html with the returned cart_item_id as highlightItemId.

Workflow 3 – Return from cart to adjust/view a specific parking area (parkingAreaId in URL): 1) User clicks ‘Edit parking’ or a similar link in cart.html that points to map_parking.html?parkingAreaId=...; 2) On load, page reads parkingAreaId from URL; 3) Page calls getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId) and opens the parking details panel; 4) If a venueId is also present in URL, the map centers on that venue via getVenueDetail(venueId) and, if parking areas are being viewed, findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, current max_distance_meters) may be called to show it in context; 5) User reviews parking restrictions and pricing and may open the parking pass form to create an additional pass or a replacement; 6) On submit, page calls addParkingPassToCart(parkingAreaId, date, arrival_time); 7) On success, user can click ‘View Cart’ to navigate back to cart.html with highlightItemId set to the new cart_item_id and from=parking, or stay on the page for further adjustments.
interactions: Search bar interaction: User enters text and submits; onSubmit triggers searchVenuesOnMap(query); results are bound to a venue list and map pins; selecting a result updates selectedVenueId, updates the venueId URL parameter, and triggers getVenueDetail(venueId).
Map pin interaction: When the user clicks a venue pin, onPinClick sets selectedVenueId, syncs venueId in the URL, and calls getVenueDetail(venueId) to display the venue details panel.

‘Find nearby parking’ interaction: Within the venue details panel, user clicks ‘Find nearby parking’; handler reads selectedVenueId and current max_distance_meters (from URL or default UI value) and calls findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters); parking results are displayed and any previous parking selection is cleared.

Distance filter interaction: User adjusts a max-distance input (e.g., slider, preset buttons, or numeric field); onMaxDistanceChange updates the internal max_distance_meters state, writes it to the URL query parameter, and refetches parking via findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters).

Parking list item interaction: User clicks a parking area entry in the list; onParkingItemClick sets selectedParkingAreaId, writes parkingAreaId into the URL, and calls getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId); parking details panel opens; if the map supports parking pins, the corresponding pin is highlighted via internal map state.

Parking details interaction: In the parking details panel, user can read description, pricing, capacity, restrictions, and accessibility; if status or remaining_capacity indicate unavailability, the ‘Add Parking Pass’ action is disabled by front-end logic based on getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId) output.

Parking pass form interaction: User opens the add-pass form from the parking details panel; selects or edits date and arrival_time; onSubmit validates required fields client-side, then calls addParkingPassToCart(parkingAreaId, date, arrival_time); while waiting, the form shows a loading state and prevents duplicate submissions; on success, it receives parking_pass_id, cart_item_id, and cart_summary and transitions into a confirmation state.

View Cart navigation interaction: After addParkingPassToCart succeeds, a ‘View Cart’ button becomes available; onViewCartClick navigates to cart.html?highlightItemId={cart_item_id}&from=parking using the returned cart_item_id, so the cart page can highlight the newly added parking item.

URL-driven initialization interaction: On initial page load or when the URL is updated (e.g., via browser back/forward), the page reads venueId, parkingAreaId, and max_distance_meters from the URL; based on what is present, it calls getVenueDetail, getParkingAreaDetail, and/or findNearbyParkingAreas to rehydrate the correct state (selected venue, parking area details, and distance filter) without relying on local storage.

Error and retry interaction: If any interface call (searchVenuesOnMap, getVenueDetail, findNearbyParkingAreas, getParkingAreaDetail, addParkingPassToCart) fails, the corresponding component shows an error message and an action button (e.g., ‘Try again’); clicking this button re-invokes the same interface with the last used parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by the URL parameters venueId, parkingAreaId, and max_distance_meters and by ephemeral in-memory UI state. On load, the page parses URL parameters and performs the following logic: (1) If venueId is present: set selectedVenueId to venueId and call getVenueDetail(venueId); use the returned coordinates to center the map and display a venue pin. (2) If max_distance_meters is present and valid: set the distance filter state to this value; otherwise use a UI default that is NOT persisted externally until the user first changes it, at which point it is written to the URL. (3) If venueId and max_distance_meters are both present: after getVenueDetail resolves, the page may immediately call findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters) to pre-populate the parking list (this must be implemented so it only occurs when a venue is successfully loaded). (4) If parkingAreaId is present: set selectedParkingAreaId to parkingAreaId and call getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId); open the parking details panel; if a venueId is also present, that venue is selected in parallel as described above. (5) User actions keep the URL in sync: selecting a venue from the search results or map pins updates the venueId parameter; adjusting the distance filter updates max_distance_meters; selecting a parking area updates parkingAreaId. These updates must use the browser history API or equivalent so that back/forward navigation restores previous selections by re-reading URL parameters; no localStorage or similar client persistence is used. (6) When addParkingPassToCart succeeds, the server returns parking_pass_id and cart_item_id, but these identifiers are not written to the URL by this page; instead, they are used only for the immediate confirmation state and to construct the navigation URL for the ‘View Cart’ button: cart.html?highlightItemId={cart_item_id}&from=parking. (7) All derived UI states like which panel is expanded, current sort order within the parking list, or whether confirmation is shown are maintained only in memory and recomputed from interface results and URL parameters as needed, rather than being persisted to any external storage.

components.functionality: Parses and maintains synchronization between URL query parameters (venueId, parkingAreaId, max_distance_meters) and in-page state, triggers initial data loading calls to getVenueDetail, getParkingAreaDetail, and findNearbyParkingAreas based on those parameters, and updates the URL when the user changes venue selection, parking selection, or the distance filter.

Displays an interactive map centered on either a default event area or the coordinates returned from getVenueDetail for the selected venue; renders pins for venues obtained from searchVenuesOnMap and highlights the currently selected venue; when the user interacts with the map (e.g., clicking a pin), it updates the selected venue state.

Provides a text input for searching venues by name; on submission, calls searchVenuesOnMap(query) and passes the results to the venue-search-results and map-viewport components; also supports clearing the search to reset the venue list and map pins.

Shows venues returned by searchVenuesOnMap(query) as a selectable list; selecting an item sets selectedVenueId, updates the venueId URL parameter via url-state-manager, and triggers loading of details through getVenueDetail(venueId).

Displays detailed information about the currently selected venue using getVenueDetail(venueId), including name, description, address, and notes; offers a primary action to ‘Find nearby parking’ that triggers the first call to findNearbyParkingAreas for this venue using the current max_distance_meters state.

Manages parking filtering options with a focus on max_distance_meters; exposes controls for selecting a maximum distance and any client-side toggles (such as only showing accessible parking) and communicates max_distance_meters changes to url-state-manager, which in turn triggers findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters).

Displays the list of parking areas returned by findNearbyParkingAreas(venueId, max_distance_meters), including name, distance_to_venue_meters, pricing_type, rate information, remaining_capacity, is_accessible, and status; supports sorting and client-side filtering based on this data and allows the user to select a parking area to view more details.

Shows full details for the selected parking area using getParkingAreaDetail(parkingAreaId), including description, pricing structure (flat_rate or hourly_rate), max_capacity, remaining_capacity, restrictions, accessibility, and status; conditionally enables or disables the ‘Add Parking Pass’ button based on remaining_capacity and status; exposes this button to open the parking-pass-form.

Collects the date and arrival_time for the parking pass associated with the currently selected parkingAreaId and, on submission, calls addParkingPassToCart(parkingAreaId, date, arrival_time); manages form validation, loading state, handling of success and error messages, and passes cart_item_id and cart_summary from the response to downstream components for confirmation and navigation.

Becomes visible or updates after a successful addParkingPassToCart call; uses the returned cart_item_id and cart_summary to show a brief confirmation and a ‘View Cart’ button; clicking the button navigates to cart.html with highlightItemId set to the cart_item_id and from=parking as query parameters.

Wraps data-dependent components (venue-search-results, venue-details-panel, parking-list-panel, parking-details-panel, parking-pass-form) to manage loading and error states consistently for each interface call (searchVenuesOnMap, getVenueDetail, findNearbyParkingAreas, getParkingAreaDetail, addParkingPassToCart); provides retry mechanisms that rerun the last attempted call with the same parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s simple vertical stack: a single, dominant hero at the top (here, the interactive map viewport) followed by a dense, symmetric 3-column grid of panels. The hero is almost entirely visual with minimal overlaid UI, echoing the image-first design. All supporting functionality (lists, details, filters, forms) is organized into two rows of three equal-width panels beneath the hero, mirroring the 3×2 gallery under the hero image in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s simple vertical stack: a single, dominant hero at the top (here, the interactive map viewport) followed by a dense, symmetric 3-column grid of panels. The hero is almost entirely visual with minimal overlaid UI, echoing the image-first design. All supporting functionality (lists, details, filters, forms) is organized into two rows of three equal-width panels beneath the hero, mirroring the 3×2 gallery under the hero image in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The map hero occupies roughly 45–50% of the initial viewport height and spans the full content width (within small page margins), just like the oversized hero image. Below it, the remaining vertical space is devoted to two rows of three equal-width panels. Each column takes about one-third of the content width, with narrow 8–16px gutters. Vertical spacing between the hero and the panel grid is modest (around 48px of black background), maintaining continuity of the dark canvas while clearly separating sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense layout: the map and panels nearly fill the width with thin black gutters between them. Inside each panel, padding is functional but not spacious (16–24px) to keep everything feeling tight and immersive. Negative space is primarily the black background surrounding the hero and between the grid panels, not large margins. This gives a content-rich, cinematic feel without tipping into clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the mockup: the eye first locks onto the bright, neon-tinted map hero at the top center. A compact search form floats over the lower portion of the map, acting as a focal control. From there, attention moves down into the 3-column grid, scanning left-to-right across the first row (venue search results → venue details → parking list), then down into the second row (parking filters → parking details → parking pass form). A small fixed cart pill in the bottom-right provides a persistent but non-dominant CTA, while any loading/error overlay temporarily takes over the center of the screen.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a dark, cinematic control room for surviving a zombie attraction night. At the very top, a full-width, high-contrast map hero dominates the screen, styled in a dark theme with neon accents for roads and venues. It stretches almost edge-to-edge with only slim side margins, taking up about half the viewport height. Hovering near the bottom center of this map is a compact, glowing search bar that lets visitors find venues without breaking the immersive imagery. Scrolling just past the hero reveals a tight, two-row grid of six equal-width panels, each framed in deep black with minimal gaps between them, echoing the three-column photo gallery of the mockup. The first row focuses on where to go (venue search results, venue details, parking list), while the second row focuses on how to park (parking filters, parking details, parking pass form). All panels share a dark chrome with neon highlights, reading more like cinematic information tiles than traditional UI cards. A small cart link pill floats fixed at the bottom-right of the viewport, glowing subtly against the darkness as a constant way to view the shopping cart. When loading or errors occur, a centered overlay temporarily mutes the scene with a dimmed veil and a bold status message, then disappears to return users to the immersive map-plus-grid layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visual footprint. It sits behind the scenes in the page structure, typically mounted near the top of the React tree, managing URL parameters for selected venue, selected parking, filter states, and map position. It does not occupy any dedicated space in the visual layout and does not affect spacing or alignment of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; behaves as an invisible controller that synchronizes state with the URL without rendering any DOM visible to the user.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map viewport functions as the cinematic hero. It is positioned at the very top of the page’s content area, spanning nearly the full browser width while respecting small left and right margins (approximately 24px on each side on desktop). Vertically, it occupies about 45–50% of the initial viewport height, with a minimum height around 420–480px to maintain a dramatic, immersive feel. The map itself uses a dark, horror-appropriate theme, with neon-like highlights for streets, venues, and parking icons. There is no visible border; it appears as a continuous, edge-to-edge panel against the black page background, with any map controls (zoom buttons, layer toggles) tucked into corners in a subtle, minimal style so that the map imagery remains dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow: it anchors the top of the page and scrolls away when the user scrolls down. Internally, map tiles and controls stay fixed within its bounds. On smaller screens, its height can shrink proportionally (down to ~40vh) to ensure the grid below remains reachable without excessive scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The venue search form is overlaid on top of the map hero rather than occupying a separate block. It is horizontally centered within the map viewport and anchored slightly above the map’s bottom edge, leaving about 24–32px of map visible beneath it. It spans roughly 40–50% of the content width on desktop, appearing as a single, dense bar or compact card with a dark, translucent background and neon-accented input and submit button. Vertical padding inside the form is about 12px, with 20–24px horizontal padding around the text field and button, giving it a pill-like, HUD-style feel. This placement makes it visually prominent without breaking the hero’s full-width map presence; it reads like a control overlay rather than a separate section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned within the map-viewport container, centered horizontally and pinned near the bottom. On smaller screens, it expands closer to 80–90% of the width, staying centered, and may shift slightly upward to avoid overlapping critical map controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The venue search results occupy the leftmost panel in the first row of the grid beneath the map. After a modest vertical gap of about 48px of pure black space below the hero, the grid starts. This panel takes roughly one-third of the content width (about 4 columns of a 12-column grid) and aligns flush with the grid’s left margin. Its height matches the other first-row panels, around 320–400px, giving the row a consistent, mosaic-like appearance. Inside the panel, results are listed vertically with tight vertical spacing (8–12px between items) and 16–24px padding from the panel edges, maintaining the dense, portfolio-like feel. The background is a deep, nearly-black card with a subtle edge glow or neon accent line at the top to echo the cinematic theme.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid, participating in the responsive 3-column layout. On narrower viewports (e.g., tablets), it stacks above or below other panels according to responsive rules but maintains its role as the first column in its row when three columns are possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The venue details panel lives directly to the right of the venue search results, forming the center tile of the first grid row. It occupies the middle third of the content width and shares the same height and top alignment as its neighboring panels so the row looks like a continuous strip of three equal modules. This panel acts as a cinematic info tile: a dark background with possibly a large venue name in neon typography at the top and key details (address, hours, warnings, scare level) stacked vertically with 16–24px internal padding. It visually feels like one of the uniform gallery images in the mockup but rendered as text and icons, not photos, still framed by the same narrow black gutters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the center column of the first row. On smaller screens, it stacks beneath the search results panel but maintains a similar width ratio and padding relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parking list panel forms the rightmost tile of the first grid row, occupying the last third of the content width. Its top aligns perfectly with the venue search results and venue details panels, and it shares their height, making the entire first row read as three equal cards. Inside, a vertically scrollable list of parking locations is displayed with compact spacing and 16–24px panel padding, using a dark-chrome list styling with bright, high-contrast labels or icons for availability and distance. The outer edges of this panel line up with the overall content margins, leaving only a slim gutter between it and the venue details panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the rightmost column of the first grid row. Internally, the list area may scroll independently if the content exceeds the panel height, keeping the overall row height consistent with its siblings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parking filters bar anchors the leftmost position of the second grid row, directly beneath the venue search results panel. It maintains the same width as the column above (about one-third of the content width) but can be slightly shorter in height if filters are minimal. To preserve the gallery-like grid, its minimum height should be sufficient to visually echo the tile proportions above (for example, around 240–320px), even if part of the interior is just dark negative space with filter chips or toggles clustered near the top. Controls are arranged horizontally or in compact groups with 16–24px internal padding, and the bar carries the same dark card style with neon accents for selected filters. This preserves the continuity of the 3×2 mosaic while dedicating this tile to refining parking options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the left column of the second row. On smaller screens, it stacks below the first-row panels, appearing just before the parking details and parking pass form in vertical order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parking details panel occupies the center column of the second row, directly below the venue details panel. It shares the same width as the center column above and is visually aligned so that the left and right edges line up precisely, reinforcing the vertical grid. Its height is comparable to the panels around it (roughly 320–400px), giving enough space for rich detail: name, distance, walking time to the venue, pricing tiers, and any warnings or notes. The interior uses 16–24px padding, with content stacked top-to-bottom in a dense but legible way. The dark background and possible neon highlight for key metrics (e.g., price, distance) keep it consistent with the cinematic theme while functioning like one of the equal-sized image tiles from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the second row’s middle column. It does not scroll independently under normal circumstances; instead, it grows modestly or constrains content with internal scrolling if necessary, preserving the row’s visual balance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parking pass form fills the rightmost tile of the second grid row, aligned directly under the parking list panel. It uses the same width (about one-third of the content width) and roughly the same height as the other second-row panels, completing the 3×2 mosaic reminiscent of the mockup’s gallery. Inside, it presents quantities, date/time selectors (if needed), and a prominent "Add Parking Pass" call-to-action button, all stacked vertically with 16–24px padding. The button itself is visually strong—bright, neon-colored against the black card—to draw attention as the primary action, yet the overall card remains tightly framed within the grid. This tile feels like the final step in the flow created by the first-row list panels: discover venues, find parking, then lock in a pass here.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the right column of the second row. On smaller screens where the layout collapses to one or two columns, it stacks after the parking details, remaining near the bottom of the page but still above any footer content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link bar is a compact, ever-present control that floats above the main content rather than consuming grid space. It appears as a small, horizontally oriented pill or bar in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, offset inward by about 24px from the right edge and 24–32px from the bottom edge. Its width is modest (around 200–260px), and height around 40–48px, with a dark, slightly translucent background and a bright/neon cart icon plus short label (e.g., "View Cart (2)"). This placement keeps it visually accessible without competing with the hero map or the grid panels, matching the minimal chrome philosophy of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom-right of the viewport so it remains visible as the user scrolls. It floats above all scrollable content with a high z-index but avoids overlapping the venue-search-form on typical desktop sizes; on smaller screens it may slightly shift upward or shrink to avoid covering critical interactive elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading/error boundary presents itself only during loading states or when an error occurs. When active, it overlays the main content area with a dim, semi-opaque black veil that slightly mutes the map and grid below, while a central message panel appears roughly in the middle of the viewport. This panel is relatively compact (around 320–400px wide), vertically centered, with 24px padding and clear, high-contrast text such as "Summoning map data…" or an error message, optionally with a retry button. Its styling follows the same dark card plus neon accent language, but the overlay effect ensures it temporarily takes precedence over all other elements without permanently altering the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When triggered, it behaves as a full-screen (or full-content) overlay fixed to the viewport, covering the map-viewport, grid panels, and cart-link-bar. When inactive, it renders nothing and has no impact on the page’s spatial arrangement.

name: About the Event
title: About the Event
description: Informational page that loads and displays dynamic event details (story, experiences, dates, location, accessibility, safety) and routes users to Tickets, Schedule, and Map & Parking pages.

core_features: Load event information using getAboutEventContent when the page initializes.
Display the event story as rich HTML content from story_html.
Summarize the different experience types available using attraction_types (e.g., maze, escape room, shows, workshops) without hardcoding labels or counts.
Show high-level dates information from dates_info.
Show high-level location information from location_info.
Show accessibility-related information from accessibility_info.
Show safety-related information from safety_info.
Provide in-page navigation to jump between story, experiences, dates, location, accessibility, and safety sections, synchronized with a URL parameter.
Provide navigation links to Tickets, Schedule, and Map & Parking pages using URL parameters where required.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Read overall event concept and story
1. User opens about.html.
2. Page initialization triggers a call to getAboutEventContent.
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state (no static placeholder content related to the event itself).
4. On successful response, the story_html field is rendered in the story section.
5. If the URL contains ?section=story, the page scrolls/focuses to the story section and marks it as active in the section navigation.
6. User can scroll normally or use the section navigation to move to other parts of the page.

Workflow: Understand types of experiences offered
1. User opens about.html (or is already on it from another page).
2. getAboutEventContent has been called and attraction_types is available in memory.
3. The Experiences section component reads attraction_types and renders a list or equivalent representation solely based on the values returned.
4. If the URL contains ?section=experiences, the page automatically scrolls/focuses to the Experiences section and marks it as active.
5. Within the Experiences section, the user may click contextual navigation links such as 'Book Tickets' or 'See full schedule' that route to tickets.html?view=tickets or schedule.html?view=all_events respectively.

Workflow: Find practical visit information (dates, location, accessibility, safety)
1. User opens about.html, possibly with a specific section parameter (e.g., ?section=dates, ?section=location, ?section=accessibility, or ?section=safety).
2. getAboutEventContent is called and returns dates_info, location_info, accessibility_info, and safety_info.
3. The page renders each corresponding section with the provided content.
4. If a section is specified in the URL, the page scrolls to that section and marks it as selected in the section navigation.
5. In any of these sections, the user can click 'View map & parking' to navigate to map_parking.html.
6. From dates or experiences-related areas, the user can click 'Book Tickets' to go to tickets.html?view=tickets, or 'See full schedule' to go to schedule.html?view=all_events.

Workflow: Navigate to Tickets, Schedule, or Map & Parking from About page
1. User is reading a section (e.g., Experiences or Dates).
2. User clicks a 'Book Tickets' link.
3. The click handler constructs the URL tickets.html?view=tickets.
4. Browser navigates to tickets.html with the view parameter set, allowing the Tickets page to select the correct view mode.
5. Similarly, clicking 'See full schedule' navigates to schedule.html?view=all_events.
6. Clicking 'View map & parking' navigates to map_parking.html without additional URL parameters.
interactions: Page load: on initial load of about.html, trigger getAboutEventContent. The response is stored in page state and distributed to all content sections. Loading and error states are managed by the loader component.

Section navigation click: when a user clicks a section navigation item (e.g., Story, Experiences, Dates, Location, Accessibility, Safety), the page scrolls/focuses to the corresponding section, updates the active state in the navigation, and updates the URL query parameter ?section={sectionKey} without reloading the page.

Deep-link handling: when about.html is opened with a section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=accessibility), the page, after content load, scrolls/focuses to the specified section and sets it as active in the section navigation.
External navigation click - Tickets: clicking any 'Book Tickets' link triggers navigation to tickets.html with the query parameter view=tickets.
External navigation click - Schedule: clicking any 'See full schedule' link triggers navigation to schedule.html with the query parameter view=all_events.
External navigation click - Map & Parking: clicking any 'View map & parking' link triggers navigation to map_parking.html.
Error retry: if getAboutEventContent fails, an error state is shown with a retry button; clicking the retry button calls getAboutEventContent again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page uses URL query parameters to manage view state and navigation, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client storage.

URL parameters used by about.html:
- section: Optional, controls which content section should be focused/highlighted when the page loads.
  - Expected values are keys that match internal section identifiers associated with the API response fields (for example: 'story', 'experiences', 'dates', 'location', 'accessibility', 'safety'). No specific values are hardcoded at the content level; the mapping between keys and existing sections is purely structural.

State behavior:
1. Initial load:
   - The page reads the 'section' query parameter from the URL.
   - The page calls getAboutEventContent.
   - After the content is loaded, if 'section' is present and corresponds to a known section, the page scrolls to that section and marks it as active in the section navigation component.
   - If 'section' is missing or does not match a known section, the page uses a default section (e.g., the first available section) as active without changing the URL.

2. Section navigation changes:
   - When a user clicks a section navigation item, the page updates its internal activeSection state and scrolls to the corresponding section.
   - The page also updates the URL query parameter 'section' to the selected value using history APIs, without reloading the page.

3. Outgoing navigation parameters:
   - 'Book Tickets' links always navigate to tickets.html with the query parameter view=tickets, ensuring the Tickets page selects its 'tickets' view mode.
   - 'See full schedule' links always navigate to schedule.html with the query parameter view=all_events, ensuring the Schedule page shows all events by default.
   - 'View map & parking' links navigate to map_parking.html without additional parameters.

No other page-level state is persisted via URL parameters, and no data is stored in localStorage or similar mechanisms.

components.functionality: Handles the initial call to getAboutEventContent, manages loading and error states, and provides the loaded data to child content sections.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when about.html loads. Calls getAboutEventContent with no parameters. Stores the returned object in page state and signals child components to render.
onLoadSuccess: Triggered when getAboutEventContent resolves successfully. Hides any loading indicator and passes the data object to all dependent components.
onLoadError: Triggered when getAboutEventContent fails. Stores error state and shows a retry UI instead of event content.
onRetryClick: Triggered when the user clicks a retry button in the error UI. Calls getAboutEventContent again and re-enters loading state.

components.functionality: Provides an in-page navigation list for jumping between the available content sections (story, experiences, dates, location, accessibility, safety) and keeps the active section in sync with the URL parameter.
data_binding: getAboutEventContent.story_html (used only to determine that a Story section is available)
getAboutEventContent.attraction_types (used only to determine that an Experiences section is available)
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the 'section' query parameter from the URL and sets the initial active section key if it matches a section for which content is available.

onNavItemClick: When a section item is clicked, updates the active section state, scrolls/focuses to the associated content section, and updates the 'section' query parameter in the URL without reloading the page.
onScrollSync (optional): As the user scrolls, can update the active navigation item when a new section becomes the primary section in view, and update the 'section' parameter accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays the main story or narrative of the event using the HTML content returned from story_html.
event_handlers: onRender: Injects story_html into the section using safe HTML rendering without altering or hardcoding any part of the content.

onInternalLinkClick: If story_html contains hyperlinks (e.g., to other pages or sections), handles clicks so that links to internal sections update the 'section' query parameter appropriately and links to other pages navigate normally.

components.functionality: Summarizes the types of attractions and experiences offered by the event using the attraction_types array, without hardcoded labels or counts.
event_handlers: onRender: Iterates over attraction_types and renders a list or equivalent representation purely from the returned values.
onBookTicketsClick: When a user clicks a 'Book Tickets' contextual link from this section, navigates to tickets.html?view=tickets.
onSeeFullScheduleClick: When a user clicks a 'See full schedule' contextual link from this section, navigates to schedule.html?view=all_events.

components.functionality: Displays high-level information about event dates using the dates_info field.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders dates_info as text or formatted content without adding any static date values.
onBookTicketsClick: When a user clicks a 'Book Tickets' link from this section, navigates to tickets.html?view=tickets.
onSeeFullScheduleClick: When a user clicks a 'See full schedule' link from this section, navigates to schedule.html?view=all_events.

components.functionality: Displays high-level location and venue information using the location_info field and links users to the Map & Parking page.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders location_info as text or formatted content without any hardcoded addresses or coordinates.
onViewMapAndParkingClick: When a user clicks 'View map & parking' from this section, navigates to map_parking.html.

components.functionality: Displays accessibility-related information for the event using the accessibility_info field.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders accessibility_info as text or structured content without adding static accessibility details beyond what the API provides.
onViewMapAndParkingClick (optional): If present as a contextual link, navigates to map_parking.html for users needing accessible parking or routes.

components.functionality: Displays safety-related information for the event using the safety_info field.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders safety_info as text or structured content without adding static safety rules not included in the API response.

components.functionality: Provides a consolidated set of key navigation links at the end of the About page to guide users to Tickets, Schedule, and Map & Parking.
event_handlers: onBookTicketsClick: Navigates the browser to tickets.html?view=tickets.
onSeeFullScheduleClick: Navigates the browser to schedule.html?view=all_events.
onViewMapAndParkingClick: Navigates the browser to map_parking.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stack: a dominant, cinematic story ‘hero’ at the top, followed by a compact section navigation band, then a sequence of full-width content sections (experiences, dates, location, accessibility, safety) and finally a strong, three-column CTA footer that visually echoes the gallery grid. Each component is a full-width horizontal band on a continuous black backdrop, with content centered within a 12-column grid. There are no sidebars; all navigation and information are arranged vertically.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stack: a dominant, cinematic story ‘hero’ at the top, followed by a compact section navigation band, then a sequence of full-width content sections (experiences, dates, location, accessibility, safety) and finally a strong, three-column CTA footer that visually echoes the gallery grid. Each component is a full-width horizontal band on a continuous black backdrop, with content centered within a 12-column grid. There are no sidebars; all navigation and information are arranged vertically.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The story section at the top behaves like a hero, occupying roughly 50–65% of the initial viewport height and spanning nearly the full content width. The section navigation is a shallow band immediately beneath it, about 64–80px tall. Subsequent sections each form distinct horizontal slabs with 48–64px vertical spacing between them: experiences is the largest content block below the hero (roughly 40–60% of a viewport height), while dates, location, accessibility, and safety are slightly more compact bands. The footer link list forms a final, visually weighty strip with three equal-width CTAs reminiscent of a 3-column gallery row. Left and right margins are relatively small (content width ~88–92% of viewport, capped at a comfortable max width), with narrow gutters between any internal columns to keep the emphasis on imagery and content over whitespace.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is moderately high: large, immersive image or textured backgrounds with tight but readable spacing between textual elements. Sections are clearly separated by 48–64px of vertical breathing room, but within each section, headings, body copy, and any imagery sit relatively close together (8–24px increments), maintaining an intense, cinematic feel. When multiple columns are used (e.g., experiences grid or footer links), gutters between columns are slim (8–16px) so that content appears as a contiguous mosaic rather than isolated cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mirrors the mockup: the eye is first pulled into the bright, neon-highlighted story hero at the top center. Immediately below, the section navigation acts like a visual handrail, encouraging structured exploration. As users scroll, attention moves down through stacked bands: the experiential, image-driven section first, then increasingly practical information (dates, location, accessibility, safety). The final scan is left-to-right across the three equal CTAs at the bottom (Tickets, Schedule, Map & Parking), which echo the three-column gallery rhythm and provide clear endpoints for action. Throughout, the user’s path is primarily vertical with secondary left-to-right scanning inside each band where multiple columns exist.

layout.overall_description: The About the Event page reads like a cinematic, vertical storyboard on a continuous black stage. At the very top, a large, full-width story section behaves like a hero: a dark, neon-splashed zombie scene stretches nearly edge-to-edge and fills about half to two-thirds of the viewport height, with the core story text overlaid or centered within the middle columns of a 12-column grid. Directly beneath this hero, a slim horizontal navigation band spans the page, its section labels laid out in a tight row that feels like a control strip under a movie poster. As you scroll down, full-width sections stack one after another: an image-forward experiences section arranged in a three-column rhythm reminiscent of the original gallery, followed by more compact slabs for dates, then location, then accessibility and safety, each with text concentrated in the central columns to preserve immersion and focus. The black background flows continuously between sections, with only modest vertical gaps and subtle shifts in background texture or lighting to mark transitions. At the bottom, the page resolves into a strong, three-column footer band: three equal-width, high-contrast link cards for Tickets, Schedule, and Map & Parking sit side-by-side, echoing the gallery grid and giving the layout a clear, action-oriented finale. The entire composition is dense and image-driven but tightly controlled, guiding the eye from top hero, through structured information, to decisive calls to action in a single, downward motion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader appears as a full-screen overlay that completely covers the underlying About page from edge to edge. It is fixed to the viewport, occupying 100% of the width and 100% of the height regardless of scroll position. The background is a solid or subtly textured black that blends with the site’s dark theme, with the loading indicator (e.g., an animated mark or logotype) centered both vertically and horizontally in the middle of the screen. No other components are visible or interactable beneath this layer while it is active. When the event data finishes loading, this overlay fades out to reveal the top story section in its place, without shifting the position of any underlying content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-viewport overlay that sits on top of all other components while loading; removed from the layout flow once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation band sits immediately below the story section, forming a shallow, full-width strip that visually hugs the bottom of the hero-like story area. It spans nearly the full browser width, with content constrained to a centered container that follows the 12-column grid and occupies about 88–92% of the viewport width. Within this band, the navigation items (Story, Experiences, Dates, Location, Accessibility, Safety) are arranged horizontally from left to right, each roughly taking up 1–2 columns so the row visually echoes the tight alignment of a gallery. Vertical height of this band is modest—around 64–80px—keeping it compact and dense. There is a small gap of 16–24px of black background between the bottom of the story content and the top of this band, matching the mockup’s narrow separation between hero and gallery. When the user scrolls past its initial position, the navigation band becomes sticky and pins to the top edge of the viewport, maintaining its full-width presence while slightly reducing any top padding so it reads like a thin control bar overlaying the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially part of the normal document flow just under the story section, then becomes sticky and fixed to the top of the viewport once scrolled past its original position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The story section is the first main content block and visually functions as the page hero. It occupies the full available width of the page, with its background image or textured dark panel running edge-to-edge across the viewport. On desktop, it fills approximately 50–65% of the initial viewport height, ensuring it dominates the first screen. Within this full-width backdrop, a centered content container aligned to the 12-column grid spans about 90% of the viewport width, leaving only narrow black margins on the far left and right. The core story copy and any event title lock-up are placed centrally, using roughly the middle 6–8 columns so that text remains readable while the surrounding columns show more of the atmospheric imagery. Vertical padding inside this section is generous at the top and bottom (32–64px within the image area) but the overall impression remains dense because the background is continuous and the text is tightly grouped. The bottom edge of this story section transitions directly to the navigation band with a modest gap of dark space, mirroring the mockup’s small black band between hero and gallery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard, full-width section in the normal document flow that behaves like a hero; scrolls normally beneath any sticky navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The experiences section appears directly beneath the section navigation, separated by about 48–64px of vertical black space so it reads as the next major block. It spans the full width of the page with a continuous dark background, while its content is centered in a container following the 12-column grid and occupying roughly 88–92% of the viewport width. Inside this container, the layout reflects the mockup’s three-column gallery: experiences are arranged into three equal-width columns, each approximately 4 grid columns wide, with slim gutters of 12–16px between them. On a typical desktop, this creates a row of three experience cards or image-backed panels across the width. If there are more experiences, additional rows stack beneath, maintaining the same three-column rhythm and tight vertical spacing (24–32px between rows). Section heading and intro copy sit above the grid, aligned to the left or center within the same container, with a compact vertical gap (16–24px) before the first row of experiences. Overall, this section occupies roughly 40–60% of a viewport height depending on content volume, forming the densest, most gallery-like area after the story hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in normal flow, internally structured as a three-column grid that can grow vertically with additional rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dates section is stacked directly below the experiences section, separated by a clear but modest vertical gap of about 48–56px of black background. Like other sections, it runs full width, with its content centered within a 12-column grid container that uses around 80–88% of the viewport width to feel slightly more focused than the gallery-like experiences block. The section heading sits at the top of this band, centered or left-aligned within the middle 8 columns, with the key dates and times grouped just beneath in a compact vertical stack. To echo the grid feel, dates and schedule details can be laid out in two or three vertical columns within those middle columns (for example, three narrow columns of bullet points, each about 3–4 grid columns wide) separated by 8–16px gutters. Vertical padding within the section is moderate (24–32px above and below the content), making it visually dense but clearly distinct from neighboring sections. This entire block is shorter than the hero and experiences sections, typically occupying about 30–40% of a viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in normal document flow with a compact, columnar internal layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location section follows the dates section after roughly 48–64px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width like previous bands, with content aligned to the central 12-column grid. Inside, the content is organized into a horizontally oriented layout to balance the page: for example, a map image or visual marker on the left occupying about 5–6 columns and supporting text (address, directions, transit notes) on the right occupying the remaining 6–7 columns. The total content width is around 88–92% of the viewport, maintaining small side margins. A section heading sits above this two-column arrangement, aligned with the grid and separated by about 16–24px. The vertical padding of the section is moderate (24–40px top and bottom), giving enough space for the map or imagery to breathe while preserving the overall dense, immersive look. This section’s height is comparable to or slightly taller than the dates section, depending on the map/imagery dimensions, generally in the range of 40–50% of a viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in normal flow, with an internal two-column layout that maintains alignment to the 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility section is positioned beneath the location section, separated by a vertical gap of about 48–56px against the same continuous black backdrop. It again spans the full width, but the text content is more constrained to emphasize readability of detailed information: the central content column uses approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, aligned to the middle 8–10 grid columns, leaving slightly larger side margins. The section heading appears at the top of this band, followed by structured accessibility details arranged in either one or two text columns: for example, a primary column of bullet points and small subheadings, with an optional secondary column for icons or highlights, each separated by tight gutters (8–16px). Vertical spacing between subgroups of content is modest (16–24px), keeping the information dense but scannable. The section’s overall height is relatively compact compared to the hero and experiences sections, typically taking up about 30–40% of a viewport height depending on text length.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in normal flow, primarily text-driven, with content centered and slightly narrower than the more visual sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The safety section sits directly beneath the accessibility section with a similar vertical separation of around 48–56px. It continues the full-width, black-background band treatment, with content centered in a container that uses about 80–88% of the viewport width. The heading is prominent and appears at the top of the section, followed by safety guidelines and protocols laid out in a structured manner—often using two or three narrow columns within the middle 8–10 grid columns to echo the three-column rhythm of the mockup while keeping individual lines short and readable. Columns are separated by 12–16px gutters, creating a compact grid of safety points. Vertical padding above the heading and below the last piece of text is moderate (24–32px), giving the section a solid but not overwhelming presence. In height, this section is similar to or slightly taller than the accessibility section, depending on the amount of safety content, and typically occupies 35–50% of a viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in normal flow with a multi-column text layout aligned to the underlying grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary links footer forms the final, anchor-like band at the very bottom of the About page. It is separated from the safety section by a more generous vertical gap of about 56–64px to signal a transition from informational content to action. This footer band spans 100% of the width, with a slightly darker or subtly differentiated black background to set it apart. Within a centered container that aligns to the 12-column grid and uses about 88–92% of the viewport width, three primary link blocks (Tickets, Schedule, Map & Parking) are laid out side-by-side in a single row, each occupying roughly 4 grid columns. They are equal in width and height, echoing the mockup’s three-column gallery tiles, with narrow horizontal gutters (12–16px) between them. These link blocks are relatively tall for buttons—approximately 96–140px in height—reading as clickable mini-cards rather than simple text links. The entire row is vertically centered within the footer band, with 24–40px of padding above and below, so that the user’s eye naturally scans left-to-right across this final trio of calls-to-action before reaching the page end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard footer section in normal flow at the bottom of the page, remaining static and non-sticky; internally arranged as a three-column CTA row.

name: Contact & Support
title: Contact & Support
description: Page for viewing dynamic support contact details and submitting general inquiries, with optional topic preselection via URL and quick navigation to FAQ and Policies.

core_features: Load and display support contact information (email, phone, venue address, FAQ and policy highlights) using the getContactInfo interface.
Provide a general contact form that collects name, email, optional phone, subject, and message, and submits inquiries via the submitContactForm interface.
Use the optional URL parameter `topic` to prefill or adapt the contact form subject so that inquiries are context-aware when arriving from FAQ or Policies.
Offer quick navigation links to the FAQ and Policies pages, using dynamic highlight text from getContactInfo for link descriptions or labels.
Display real-time feedback (success or failure messages) after a form submission, based on the response from submitContactForm.
user_workflows:
Workflow: User views contact information
1. User navigates to contact.html (optionally with a `topic` URL parameter).
2. On page load, the contact-info-loader component calls getContactInfo.
3. While waiting, the contact-info-panel may show a loading state.
4. When getContactInfo returns successfully, the contact-info-panel displays the support email, support phone, and venue address.
5. The quick-links component uses faq_highlight and policies_highlight from getContactInfo to label or describe the FAQ and Policies links.

Workflow: User submits a general inquiry
1. User opens contact.html (with or without a `topic` URL parameter).
2. The url-state-manager reads the `topic` parameter and passes its value to the contact-form as initial state (for example, pre-populating the subject field).
3. The user fills in required fields: name, email, subject, and message; optionally provides phone.
4. On form submission, the contact-form validates that required fields are not empty and that email is syntactically valid.
5. If validation passes, contact-form calls submitContactForm with { name, email, subject, message, phone } (where subject and/or message may internally include the topic value if provided).
6. While the request is in progress, the form disables inputs and submit button and may show a “sending” state.
7. When submitContactForm responds, the form-status-banner displays the returned message and indicates success or failure based on the success boolean.
8. On success, the form can optionally clear fields or keep them filled, depending on configuration, without reloading the page.

Workflow: User arrives from FAQ or Policies with a preselected topic
1. User clicks a contact-related link on faq.html or policies.html that navigates to contact.html including a `topic` URL parameter.
2. On load, the url-state-manager parses the `topic` parameter and stores it in page state as currentTopic.
3. The contact-form initializes its subject field using currentTopic (for example, by setting the initial subject value to the `topic` string) while still allowing the user to edit it.
4. The user reviews or adjusts the subject, completes other fields, and submits the form.
5. The submitContactForm call includes the potentially topic-influenced subject (and optionally includes the topic embedded in the message body, handled purely on the client side).

Workflow: User navigates to FAQ or Policies from Contact & Support
1. User scrolls to or sees the quick-links section.
2. User clicks the FAQ quick link; the browser navigates to faq.html without additional parameters.
3. Alternatively, user clicks the Policies quick link; the browser navigates to policies.html.
4. If faq.html or policies.html include their own logic to return to Contact & Support with a `topic` parameter, that is handled on those pages; contact.html simply respects the parameter when present.
interactions: On page load, the contact-info-loader component automatically calls getContactInfo and propagates the result to contact-info-panel and quick-links components.
On URL change or initial load, the url-state-manager reads the `topic` parameter and exposes the value to other components as currentTopic.
When the user edits form fields (name, email, phone, subject, message) in the contact-form, the component updates its internal form state and validation status.
When the user clicks the form submit button, the contact-form validates inputs and, if valid, invokes submitContactForm with the current field values and triggers a loading state.
When submitContactForm resolves, the contact-form forwards the returned success and message values to the form-status-banner, which updates the visible status message.
When the user clicks the FAQ quick link, the browser navigates to faq.html using a standard link interaction.
When the user clicks the Policies quick link, the browser navigates to policies.html using a standard link interaction.

When displaying contact details, clicking the email field may trigger a mail client (using a mailto link) and clicking the phone field may trigger a call action on compatible devices (using a tel link); both use dynamic data from getContactInfo rather than any hardcoded values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by a combination of URL parameters, interface responses, and in-memory component state. The URL parameter `topic` is read once on initial load (and on any subsequent navigation that changes the URL) by the url-state-manager component. If `topic` is present, its exact string value is exposed as currentTopic and used by the contact-form to initialize the subject field (and, if desired, to append contextual information inside the message body before submission). If `topic` is absent, currentTopic is null or empty, and the subject field starts empty. No topic values are hardcoded; whatever string is passed via the URL is used as-is. The contact information state is obtained solely from the getContactInfo interface. Until getContactInfo returns, components that depend on it stay in a loading or fallback state, and on error they can show a generic error state without relying on static fallback data. The form submission state tracks whether a submitContactForm request is in-flight; while true, the submit button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions. After submitContactForm resolves, the returned success boolean and message string are stored in in-memory state and drive the content and style of the form-status-banner. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage; all state is derived from the current URL and the live interface responses.

components.functionality: On page initialization, invokes the getContactInfo interface, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting contact data (support_email, support_phone, venue_address, faq_highlight, policies_highlight) to child components that display contact details and quick links.

Displays the dynamically loaded support_email, support_phone, and venue_address. Provides functional interactions such as clicking the email to open a mail client, clicking the phone to initiate a call on supported devices, and optionally copying these values to the clipboard, all driven by the data returned from getContactInfo.

Renders navigational links to the FAQ (faq.html) and Policies (policies.html) pages. Uses faq_highlight and policies_highlight from getContactInfo to display context or descriptions for each link. Clicking each link triggers standard browser navigation to the respective pages.

Parses the current page URL to extract the `topic` parameter, exposes it as currentTopic to other components, and updates this value if the URL changes (for example, via internal navigation that modifies query parameters). It does not modify the URL itself; it only reads from it to determine initial form state.

Provides inputs for name, email, optional phone, subject, and message. Initializes the subject field using the currentTopic provided by url-state-manager (if any). Performs client-side validation to ensure required fields are filled and email is syntactically valid. On submit, calls submitContactForm with the current field values, optionally augmenting subject and/or message with the currentTopic value purely on the client side. Manages loading state during submission and forwards the interface response to the form-status-banner.

Shows the current status of the contact form submission. Before any submission, it can remain hidden or neutral. After submitContactForm returns, it displays the returned message text and differentiates between success and error states based on the success boolean. It updates in real time whenever a new submission completes.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s vertical, media-first structure: a cinematic, full-width hero image for the Contact & Support page sits at the very top (not part of the given components), followed by a single, dense interaction zone that behaves like the 3-column image gallery. Within this interaction zone, the contact-info-panel, contact-form, and quick-links are arranged side-by-side in a three-column grid, each acting like an image tile/card. Non-visual components (data-loader, url-state-manager) operate behind the scenes without occupying visual space. The form-status-banner lives inside the contact-form area as a thin bar near the top of the form, rather than as a separate section, to preserve the simple hero + grid pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s vertical, media-first structure: a cinematic, full-width hero image for the Contact & Support page sits at the very top (not part of the given components), followed by a single, dense interaction zone that behaves like the 3-column image gallery. Within this interaction zone, the contact-info-panel, contact-form, and quick-links are arranged side-by-side in a three-column grid, each acting like an image tile/card. Non-visual components (data-loader, url-state-manager) operate behind the scenes without occupying visual space. The form-status-banner lives inside the contact-form area as a thin bar near the top of the form, rather than as a separate section, to preserve the simple hero + grid pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top hero consumes roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and the full content width. Below it, a dark gap of about 32px–48px separates the hero from the interactive grid section. The three main cards (contact-info-panel, contact-form, quick-links) each occupy roughly one-third of the content width on desktop, aligning to an implicit 12-column grid (4 columns each) with narrow horizontal gutters of 8–16px. Vertical padding inside the cards is 16–24px; the form card is allowed to become taller than the adjacent cards due to field content. The form-status-banner uses only a slim vertical slice (around 48px) at the top of the form card. There is a 48–64px bottom margin after this grid section before any subsequent content or footer, maintaining the stacked, section-based rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately dense feel: components are large and almost edge-to-edge against a black background, with only narrow gutters between columns and modest vertical gaps between sections. Cards have minimal external whitespace, focusing on immersive content rather than airiness. Inside each card, spacing is functional rather than lavish (16–24px padding). This mirrors the gallery’s packed, image-first density, but applied to functional UI elements instead of photos.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is first drawn to the bright, neon-styled hero at the top center. From there, the visual flow drops straight down to the interaction grid. Within the grid, attention is pulled strongest to the central contact-form column (primary action), then scans left to the contact-info-panel, and right to the quick-links, following a left-to-right reading pattern. The form-status-banner appears near the top of the form column, so it is seen immediately after the user’s eye lands on the form. Overall, the experience is a top-to-bottom, then left-to-right scan, with clearly defined visual anchors at each step.
