layout.overall_description: The Contact & Support page adopts the cinematic, image-driven feel of the mockup: a dark, full-width hero at the top sets a moody, neon-lit zombie-event tone, occupying nearly half of the initial viewport and centering a bold “Contact & Support” title against a black, atmospheric background. Just below, after a thin band of pure black, the page resolves into a single, dense block of interaction that echoes the original 3-column photo gallery. Three tall, card-like panels sit side-by-side against the black backdrop: on the left, live-updated contact information; in the center, a narrow but prominent inquiry form; on the right, quick links to FAQs and policies. The cards almost touch the edges of the screen, separated only by slim black gutters, reinforcing the immersive, content-packed feel. Within the center card, a slim, high-contrast status banner appears and disappears depending on the form’s state, sitting just under the form’s title like a glowing warning strip in a horror set. Non-visual logic components work silently in the background, keeping the visual field clean and focused. On smaller screens, this same set of elements collapses gracefully into a single-column vertical stack—hero on top, then contact info, form (with its banner), and quick links—preserving the strong top-down narrative of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-loader runs as an invisible, non-rendering layer associated with the left-hand card area where the contact-info-panel appears. It sits logically near the top of the page structure, triggering data fetches as soon as the page loads, but it occupies no visible space, does not create margins or gaps, and does not affect the visual grid. From a layout perspective, it behaves as if it were a behind-the-scenes controller for the contact-info-panel’s content, fully contained within the same conceptual column but not taking any physical portion of the 12-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; executes data loading logic without rendering UI. It does not influence layout flow or spacing and is effectively transparent in the page’s visual geometry.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel occupies the leftmost third of the main interactive grid, directly beneath the hero section. On large screens, it spans approximately 4 of 12 columns, aligned to the left edge of the content area, with a narrow black gutter (8–16px) separating it from the central form column. Its width visually matches the other two columns to emulate the uniform photo tiles of the mockup. Vertically, it begins about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the hero image, forming the first card in the row. The panel appears as a dark, matte card that blends into the black background but is defined by subtle edges or shadows; internal padding of 16–24px keeps text and icons away from the borders. Contact channels (phone, email, emergency support, operating hours) are stacked vertically inside, using the base spacing scale (8–16px) between rows. On taller viewports, the card may be shorter than the adjacent form card; it simply ends where its content ends, allowing the center column to extend further downward without breaking the grid alignment at the top. On smaller screens, this panel becomes the first card in a vertical stack below the hero, spanning close to 100% of the content width, with 24–32px vertical spacing separating it from the form card that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element participating in the main 3-column grid. It scrolls with the page, with no fixed or sticky behavior, and aligns its top edge with the top of the adjacent central and right-hand cards to maintain a clean horizontal row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links component sits in the rightmost third of the main interaction grid, mirroring the contact-info-panel on the opposite side. It spans roughly 4 of 12 columns, aligned to the right edge of the content area, with a slim black gutter (8–16px) separating it from the central contact-form column. Vertically, its top edge aligns with the top edges of the contact-info-panel and contact-form, forming a clean, level first row of three equal-width cards reminiscent of the three-column image gallery. The quick-links card features a deep black background with subtle separation from the page via a thin outline or drop shadow. Inside, a short title or label (e.g., “FAQ & Policies”) sits at the top, followed by a vertical list of chunky, pill-like links or buttons (e.g., “FAQ,” “Event Rules,” “Refund Policy,” “Safety & Warnings”), each separated by 8–12px. Internal padding is 16–24px on all sides. The card’s height is generally less than the central form card, ending where the last link does, so the bottom edges of the three cards do not need to align perfectly; the top alignment and equal widths carry the visual rhythm. On small screens, this component moves below the contact-form in a single-column layout, spanning nearly full width and separated from the form by about 24–32px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable block within the main 3-column grid. No fixed or sticky behavior; it maintains its position relative to the contact-form and contact-info-panel and collapses to full-width beneath them on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager operates entirely behind the scenes, coordinating URL-driven topic preselection for the contact-form and possibly emphasizing relevant links in the quick-links area. It has no visible UI or dedicated space on the page, so it does not appear as a box, card, or strip. Conceptually, it is associated with the central form column, but it is layered logically rather than visually; it does not alter the physical layout, only the initial state and focus of existing components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It does not occupy any width or height and does not affect flow or spacing. It responds to URL parameters and updates other components’ states without moving or resizing them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the central third of the main grid and serves as the primary focal point of the interactive area. On large screens, it spans approximately 4 of 12 columns, centered between the contact-info-panel on the left and the quick-links on the right, each separated by narrow gutters of 8–16px. Its top edge aligns with the top edges of the other two cards, maintaining the uniform row that evokes the three-image gallery in the mockup. The card is slightly more visually prominent: a deep black panel with a stronger highlight or glow effect, perhaps reflecting the neon accent colors from the hero, signaling it as the main action area. Inside the card, a header (“Send us a message” or similar) sits at the top, followed immediately by the form-status-banner area (when present) and then the form fields (topic selector, message, contact details) stacked vertically with 8–16px between them. Internal padding is 20–24px to give the form a bit more breathing room than pure informational cards. Because forms are taller by nature, this column often extends lower on the page than the left and right cards, creating a central vertical pillar that the user’s eye follows downward. On smaller screens, the form becomes the second card in the vertical stack (after the contact-info-panel), spanning nearly the full width of the screen with 16–24px horizontal padding to keep fields readable and comfortable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block within the grid. It does not fix to the viewport but may extend taller than adjacent cards. The form-status-banner is nested at the top of this card’s content area, moving with it as the user scrolls. On narrow viewports, it reflows to full-width and stacks in a single column between contact-info-panel and quick-links.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status-banner appears as a thin, high-contrast strip inside the contact-form card, immediately below the form’s title and above the first input field. It spans the full internal width of the form card, matching its padding on the left and right, and has a modest height (approximately 40–56px), enough to hold a single line or two of status text (e.g., success, error, or in-progress messages) along with an icon if desired. The banner’s background color stands out against the dark card—using a saturated neon or warning tone consistent with the cinematic theme—creating a glowing bar that feels like a HUD warning in a horror environment. When inactive, the banner area collapses entirely so it does not leave extra spacing; the header then transitions directly into the first form field with standard vertical spacing (16–24px). On large screens, the banner never breaks the 3-column grid; it remains fully contained within the center column. On small screens, it retains this same relationship, simply stretching to match the full-width form card that now spans the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and dynamic within the contact-form card. It does not have independent scrolling or fixed behavior; it shows and hides in place, pushing form fields down when visible and collapsing without leaving a gap when hidden. It never floats above other components or detaches from the form’s position in the layout.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: FAQ page that loads frequently asked questions via getFAQEntries, allows category-based filtering and text search, and routes users to related functional pages like Tickets, Map & Parking, Schedule, and Policies.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries from the getFAQEntries interface when the page initializes.
Derive available FAQ categories from the returned data and highlight or focus on the category indicated by the URL parameter `category` when present.
Allow users to switch FAQ categories (e.g., tickets, parking, accessibility) through an on-page category selector that updates the `category` URL parameter.
Provide a client-side search field that filters the currently loaded FAQ entries by matching text in the question and answer_html content.
Display FAQs in an expandable/collapsible list so users can quickly scan questions and reveal individual answers on demand.

Within each FAQ (or adjacent to it), show contextual navigation links to other functional pages based on the FAQ entry’s category (e.g., link to tickets.html with the appropriate query parameter from tickets-related FAQs).
Handle empty states gracefully when no FAQs match the selected category or search query and provide guidance to adjust filters.
Ensure all state that should be shareable (such as the currently selected FAQ category) is represented in the URL via query parameters, without using localStorage.
user_workflows: Browse all FAQs without filters: 1) User navigates to faq.html (no URL parameters). 2) The page controller calls getFAQEntries and stores the returned list. 3) Since no `category` parameter is present, the active category is set to a default that shows all FAQs. 4) The category filter component shows all available categories derived from the FAQ data and highlights the default state. 5) The FAQ list component displays all entries, each collapsed by default. 6) The user clicks any question to expand or collapse its answer and can follow any internal links in the answer_html to other pages.

Open FAQs focused on a specific category from another page: 1) User clicks an FAQ-related link on index.html or contact.html that navigates to faq.html with `?category=<some-category>`. 2) The FAQ page reads the `category` parameter from the URL on load. 3) The page controller calls getFAQEntries and, once data is available, sets the active category to the value from the URL if at least one FAQ entry has that category; otherwise it falls back to a default view. 4) The category filter component highlights the active category. 5) The FAQ list shows only entries whose category matches the active category. 6) The user can switch to another category using the UI, which updates the URL parameter and re-filters the list.

Search within FAQs: 1) User is on faq.html with some category active (from URL or default). 2) The user types a search term into the FAQ search field. 3) On each change or on submit (depending on configuration), the page filters the current category’s FAQ entries by matching the search term against question text and answer_html (after stripping HTML tags for matching). 4) The FAQ list updates to show only matching entries, or an empty state message if none match. 5) Clearing the search term returns the view to the full set for the current category, without changing the `category` URL parameter.

Navigate from a FAQ to other functional pages: 1) User expands an FAQ that is associated with a particular category (for example, a tickets-related entry). 2) The FAQ item component renders one or more contextual links appropriate for that category (for example, a link to tickets.html with `?view=tickets` for tickets-related entries, or a link to map_parking.html for parking-related entries). 3) When the user clicks such a link, the browser navigates to the target page with the specified URL parameters. 4) The user completes the relevant self-service action on the target page (such as viewing tickets, checking the map & parking, reading policies, or viewing the schedule) without needing additional support.

Adjust category via UI and share the state: 1) User lands on faq.html (with or without an initial `category` parameter). 2) The category selector displays all categories derived from getFAQEntries. 3) User clicks a different category in the selector. 4) The page updates the `category` URL parameter (e.g., via a client-side navigation like updating window.location or using the History API) so the URL reflects the new category. 5) The FAQ list re-filters to show only entries with that category. 6) The user can copy the URL and share it; when another person opens it, the same category is preselected based on the URL.
interactions: On page load, the controller component reads the `category` query parameter (if present) and immediately calls getFAQEntries; once the promise resolves, it sets internal state for the full FAQ list and determines the initial active category.

When the user clicks a category in the category selector, the page updates the `category` query parameter in the URL and re-applies the category filter to the already loaded FAQ data instead of recalling getFAQEntries unnecessarily.

When the user changes the search field (onInput or onSubmit), the search term is stored in component state, and the displayed FAQ list is filtered by applying the search term on top of the current category filter.
When the user clicks a FAQ question, an expand/collapse handler toggles a boolean state associated with that FAQ entry so its answer_html is shown or hidden; only the UI state changes, not the URL.

When the user clicks a contextual link in or below an FAQ answer (e.g., links that go to tickets.html, map_parking.html, schedule.html, or policies.html), the browser navigates to the specified target URL and, where required, includes the appropriate query parameters such as `view=tickets` or `view=all_events`.

If the user navigates directly by changing the URL’s `category` parameter (such as using the back button after switching categories), the controller listens for URL changes and re-computes the active category and filtered FAQ list to stay in sync.

If the filtered result set becomes empty (because of category and/or search filters), the FAQ list component displays an empty-state message and, optionally, a link or instruction to adjust filters or navigate to other relevant pages for more information.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main shareable state on this page is the active FAQ category, represented by the URL query parameter `category`. On initial page load, the controller parses the current URL and stores the raw value of `category` (if present). It then calls getFAQEntries to retrieve all FAQs. Once data is loaded, it computes the set of valid categories from the `category` field of each returned FAQ entry. If the URL `category` matches one of these categories, that value becomes the activeCategory; otherwise, activeCategory falls back to a default state that shows all entries (or the first valid category, depending on the implementation policy). This activeCategory is passed down to the category selector and FAQ list components. When the user changes category via the selector, the page updates activeCategory and also updates the `category` parameter in the URL (adding, replacing, or removing it as appropriate) without using localStorage. The FAQ list always derives its displayed entries by applying two filters to the full data set: first by activeCategory (unless in an 'all categories' state) and then by the current search term (if any). The search term is not required to be in the URL, since it is considered transient. The expand/collapse state for each FAQ item is kept only in memory for the session and is not encoded in the URL. Navigation to other pages (tickets.html, map_parking.html, schedule.html, policies.html) includes the required query parameters specified by the outgoing_connections definition (e.g., tickets.html?view=tickets, schedule.html?view=all_events). The page does not use localStorage or any other persistent client storage; all state relevant for sharing or reloading is encoded via the URL query string.

components.functionality: Initializes the FAQ page, orchestrates data loading from getFAQEntries, parses URL parameters, determines the active category and search term, and provides filtered FAQ data and state to child components.

Displays all available FAQ categories derived from the FAQ entries, highlights the currently active category (based on the controller state and `category` URL parameter), and lets users switch categories, updating the URL and notifying the controller.

Provides an input field for users to enter a search term to filter FAQ questions and answers; passes the search term to the controller so the FAQ list can be filtered in combination with the active category.

Receives the full FAQ dataset from the controller along with the active category and search term; computes or receives a filtered list of FAQs to render; handles the empty state when no entries match, and delegates expand/collapse behavior to individual FAQ items.

Represents a single FAQ entry with a clickable question and a collapsible answer section that renders answer_html; includes contextual navigation links to other functional pages based on the FAQ entry’s category (e.g., linking to tickets.html with a `view=tickets` parameter for tickets-related entries, or to schedule.html with `view=all_events` for schedule-related entries).

Displays when the filtered FAQ list is empty, explaining that no FAQs match the current category and/or search term and may provide links or guidance to change filters or navigate to other pages like tickets.html, map_parking.html, schedule.html, or policies.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: The FAQ page follows the same hero‑plus‑grid narrative as the mockup. A full‑width, cinematic FAQ hero occupies the top of the page, spanning the full 12‑column width and ~40–50% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the interactive FAQ tools (category filter and search form) form a compact control band that reads as the transition from hero to content. The FAQs themselves are laid out as a three‑column grid of accordion cards, mirroring the mockup’s 3‑column photo gallery. There is no sidebar; everything flows vertically: hero → filter/search band → FAQ grid (or empty state).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The FAQ page follows the same hero‑plus‑grid narrative as the mockup. A full‑width, cinematic FAQ hero occupies the top of the page, spanning the full 12‑column width and ~40–50% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the interactive FAQ tools (category filter and search form) form a compact control band that reads as the transition from hero to content. The FAQs themselves are laid out as a three‑column grid of accordion cards, mirroring the mockup’s 3‑column photo gallery. There is no sidebar; everything flows vertically: hero → filter/search band → FAQ grid (or empty state).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero dominates the initial fold, taking roughly the top half of the viewport and nearly the full browser width, with minimal side margins. The control band (filter + search) occupies a relatively shallow strip (about 15–20% of the visible height after the hero) but spans most of the content width. The FAQ grid below fills the remaining vertical space, using three equal‑width columns (each ~4 grid columns) with narrow gutters. Section spacing follows the scale: ~48px between hero and control band, ~32px between control band and FAQ grid, and compact 16–24px padding inside cards where text appears.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense, echoing the image‑rich mockup. The dark background bleeds edge‑to‑edge with slender black gutters between FAQ cards. Filters and search are tight but readable, with minimal dead space. The FAQ grid packs cards closely together with 16px horizontal and ~24px vertical gaps, creating a continuous mosaic of question tiles rather than a sparse, text‑heavy layout. Only the hero has a more open feel to preserve its cinematic impact.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first pulled to the neon‑lit FAQ hero title centered at the top. From there it drops directly to the control band: category filter first (left‑aligned, easy to scan), then the large central search bar. After setting context via filter/search, the eye flows down into the FAQ grid, scanning left‑to‑right across each of the three columns and then down row by row, similar to browsing an image gallery. Expanded accordion items extend vertically within their column, keeping the primary reading pattern top‑to‑bottom, then left‑to‑right.

layout.overall_description: The FAQ page looks like a cinematic companion to the zombie event’s main landing. At the very top, a wide, high‑contrast hero image fills the screen from left to right, sitting on a pure black backdrop and rising to roughly half the viewport height. Over this image, in the center, glows a bold, neon‑styled “FAQ” title and brief supporting copy, as if stenciled into the fog of an abandoned horror set. As you scroll slightly, the hero gives way to a dark control band that stretches across the centered content area: first a row of category pills hugging the left and flowing toward the center, then a thick, bright‑edged search bar centered below them, spanning most of the width. Under this band, the FAQs themselves appear as a three‑column grid of dark accordion cards, each card the size of a gallery thumbnail but filled with terse question text and neon accents instead of imagery. The cards sit close together with only narrow black gutters, creating a dense, tile‑like mosaic that echoes the mockup’s 3‑column photo gallery. When a card is opened, it expands downward within its column, pushing the cards beneath it further down while neighboring columns maintain their rhythm. If no results match, the grid is replaced by a single, centered informational block—still on the same dark stage—that occupies the middle of the screen and keeps the page feeling full but focused. Throughout, the layout remains linear and immersive: a strong hero at the top, a compact band of controls, and a rich grid of interactive FAQ tiles below, all unified by the dark cinematic theme.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full‑bleed, black canvas on which the entire experience sits. At the very top of the document flow, it hosts a full‑width hero section that spans all 12 columns and roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height. This hero uses a high‑contrast, zombie‑themed background image set to cover, with minimal side margins (content max‑width around 1200–1440px, but the image itself visually extends close to the browser edges). The hero content (page title and introductory text) is centered both horizontally and slightly above the vertical midpoint, echoing the mockup’s central hero focus. Directly beneath the hero, within the same page controller, the main FAQ section is centered on the page and constrained to the same max width, aligned to a 12‑column grid. There is approximately 48px of vertical black space between the bottom of the hero and the top of the FAQ controls (filter/search), keeping the sections distinct but visually continuous. The FAQ grid then fills the rest of the vertical page space with a three‑column structure. The page controller scrolls normally; nothing is fixed to the viewport by default. It simply orchestrates the vertical stack: hero at top, then control band, then FAQ list or empty state, all on a continuous dark backdrop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main scrolling container for the page in standard document flow. The hero area is anchored to the top and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down; no elements are sticky or floating at the page-controller level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter sits immediately below the hero, as the first element of the FAQ control band. It is left‑aligned within the centered content width and spans nearly the full width of the 12‑column grid (roughly 10–12 columns, leaving only small side margins). Vertically, it appears in a shallow strip about one to one and a half lines of pills tall, with additional rows wrapping if there are many categories. The top of the filter row is offset by about 48px from the bottom edge of the hero image, so it feels clearly separate but still visually connected. The pills themselves form a low‑profile horizontal band, with categories flowing from left to right and wrapping onto a second line if needed, preserving a tight, dense feel similar to a row of small image thumbnails. There is about 16px of vertical space between the bottom of the filter row and the top of the search form below, making them read as part of a single control zone. On wider screens, the overall filter block occupies roughly the top third of the control band’s height, visually anchoring the user’s first interaction after scanning the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders inline in the normal flow, within the control band. It scrolls with the page; it is not sticky, though it sits high enough beneath the hero that a small scroll always reveals it fully.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is positioned directly beneath the category filter, forming the second and more visually prominent line of the control band. It is horizontally centered within the content area and spans roughly 60–70% of the page width (about 7–8 of the 12 grid columns), giving it a wide, cinematic feel similar to a central title strip. There is about 16px of vertical black space between the bottom of the filter pills and the top of the search input. The input itself is a single, large horizontal bar with a modest height (around 48px), visually framed by a subtle neon outline to stand out against the black. It hugs the surrounding elements fairly closely, with 24px of padding inside and minimal external padding, in line with the layout’s moderate density. Below the search bar, about 24–32px of vertical space separates it from the top row of FAQ cards, clearly delineating the control band from the grid while preserving an overall compact feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow between the filter row and the FAQ list. It scrolls off the screen as the user moves down; no fixed or floating behavior is applied by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies the large content region immediately below the control band, starting about 32px beneath the search form. It is laid out as a three‑column grid, echoing the mockup’s photo gallery. Within the 12‑column grid, each FAQ column spans 4 columns, with consistent horizontal gutters of about 16px between them. The list spans almost the full content width, leaving only modest side margins so the cards feel large and immersive. Vertically, the list extends downward as many rows as needed; each row consists of up to three FAQ cards aligned along the same baseline. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between rows of cards, making the grid feel tightly packed but still legible. The dark background shows through only as narrow gaps between cards, creating a continuous mosaic from top to bottom. On wider screens, the first one or two rows are usually visible above the fold, reinforcing the gallery‑like impression. As accordion items expand, the affected card’s height increases, pushing cards in the same column downward while maintaining column alignment and the overall three‑column rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a responsive grid in normal scroll. Each FAQ entry occupies a fixed column width but variable height. Expansion and collapse of items reflow the grid vertically without overlaying or detaching content from the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item is a card‑like accordion tile occupying one cell in the three‑column grid. It stretches the full width of its 4‑column track, with no internal gutters to its left or right. The card sits on the black background with a slightly lighter, textured dark surface, bordered by a subtle glow or line to echo the image frames from the mockup. At rest (collapsed), the card’s height is compact, just tall enough for a single line or two of question text plus padding—roughly 16–24px of padding on all sides of the text, in line with the spacing system. The question text is aligned toward the top-left of the card, with a small neon accent or icon indicating expandability on the right, balancing the composition. When expanded, the card grows downward, revealing the answer content beneath the question within the same card frame. The answer text maintains the same side padding and extends toward the bottom of the tile, increasing its height while preserving its width. Neighboring cards in the same row shift down as needed, but the three columns remain aligned, ensuring the grid still feels like a uniform mosaic of tiles, some taller than others. The visual emphasis is on compactness and density, so margins between cards remain narrow (about 16px horizontally and 24–32px vertically).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses in place within the grid cell, pushing subsequent cards in its column downward. No overlay or modal behavior; the item remains anchored to its grid position as the layout reflows vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears in the exact zone normally occupied by the FAQ list, replacing the grid when no results match the current filter/search. It is centered horizontally within the content area and spans a medium width—around 6–8 of the 12 grid columns—so it feels substantial but not as wide as the full grid. There is about 32px of vertical space separating it from the search form above, mirroring the spacing that would otherwise separate the control band from the top row of cards. The block itself is a single, dark informational panel with 24–32px of internal padding, optionally featuring a faint, misty horror illustration behind the text to stay in line with the cinematic theme while keeping the layout simple. The text inside is center‑aligned, with the primary message near the top and any suggested actions (like links to Tickets or Policies) below, stacked vertically. Vertically on the page, if there are few surrounding elements, the empty state panel can be given extra top and bottom padding (48–64px) to visually center it within the available viewport area beneath the controls, preventing the page from feeling empty while still maintaining the dark, immersive backdrop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders in normal document flow at the location of the FAQ grid, only when there are zero results. It does not overlay other sections; instead, it replaces the list in the layout and scrolls naturally with the page.

name: Policies (Terms, Privacy, Refunds)
title: Policies
description: Displays legal policies (terms, privacy, refunds) and lets users switch between them, deep-link to specific policy types via URL parameters, and navigate to the contact page for policy questions.

core_features: Load and display the requested policy content (terms, privacy, or refunds) by calling getPoliciesContent with the policy_type URL parameter.
Allow users to switch between available policy types via in-page controls that update the policy_type URL parameter and refetch content through getPoliciesContent.
Show policy metadata such as title and effective date returned by getPoliciesContent alongside the HTML body of the policy.
Provide a dedicated navigation action to open the contact page preconfigured for policy-related inquiries (contact.html?topic=policies).
Handle loading and error states for the getPoliciesContent API so users understand when content is being fetched or cannot be displayed.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Visit policies page from footer without a specific focus
1. User clicks the 'Policies' link in the footer.
2. Browser navigates to policies.html without a policy_type parameter.
3. The page controller determines a selectedPolicyType value. If a policy_type URL parameter is present and supported, it uses that; otherwise it uses a default policy type defined by the implementation (e.g., terms, privacy, or refunds) without hardcoding specific policy content.
4. The page controller calls getPoliciesContent with the selectedPolicyType.
5. While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown in the content area.
6. When getPoliciesContent resolves, the page updates the title area, effective date, and main content using the returned title, effective_date, and content_html.
7. The tab or selector corresponding to selectedPolicyType is highlighted as active so users can see which policy is displayed.
8. User can read the policy, then optionally click the 'Contact us about this policy' link, which opens contact.html?topic=policies in the same or a new tab depending on implementation.

Workflow: Open directly to a specific policy from checkout or FAQ
1. From checkout.html or faq.html, the user clicks a link such as 'View refund policy'.
2. Browser navigates to policies.html with a policy_type URL parameter (for example, policy_type=refunds as provided by the source page).
3. On page load, the controller reads policy_type from the URL and sets selectedPolicyType to this value if it is recognized.
4. The controller calls getPoliciesContent with that policy_type.
5. Loading state is displayed while waiting.
6. On success, the content area shows the policy details returned by getPoliciesContent, with the matching tab/selector marked active.
7. The user may switch to a different policy type using the in-page navigation; this updates the URL parameter and triggers a new call to getPoliciesContent.

Workflow: Switch between policy types using in-page navigation
1. A user on policies.html sees controls for selecting different policy types (for example, tabs or a dropdown), each corresponding to a policy_type value supported by the backend.
2. The currently active control reflects the policy_type in the URL.
3. The user clicks or selects a different policy type control.
4. The controller updates the URL to policies.html?policy_type=<new_type> using navigation or history APIs.
5. The controller sets selectedPolicyType to the new value and calls getPoliciesContent with this new policy_type.
6. The content area enters a loading state and, once data is returned, updates title, effective_date, and content_html.
7. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the controller detects the change in policy_type in the URL and repeats steps 5–6 so that history navigation correctly restores previous policy views.

Workflow: Handle errors when loading a policy
1. The page loads or the user switches policy_type, triggering a getPoliciesContent call.
2. If getPoliciesContent fails (for example, due to network or server issues, or an unsupported policy_type), the controller sets an error state for the page.
3. The content component hides any stale policy content and displays an error message or indicator based on this error state.
4. An optional 'Retry' control (if implemented) lets the user attempt to refetch the current selectedPolicyType, calling getPoliciesContent again.
5. Once a subsequent call succeeds, the error state is cleared and the policy content is displayed again.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Browser loads policies.html with or without a policy_type URL parameter.
- Behavior: The page controller reads the policy_type parameter from the URL, determines selectedPolicyType, and calls getPoliciesContent(policy_type: selectedPolicyType). It then updates the page with the returned title, effective_date, and content_html, and sets the active state on the matching policy-type control.

Interaction: Change policy type via tabs or selector
- Trigger: User clicks on a policy tab or changes the value of a policy-type dropdown.
- Behavior: The controller updates the URL to reflect the newly selected policy_type and triggers a new call to getPoliciesContent(policy_type: newlySelectedType). While waiting, it sets a loading state and once the response arrives, updates the displayed content and active control state.

Interaction: Contact about this policy
- Trigger: User clicks 'Contact us about this policy'.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to contact.html with the query parameter topic=policies so that the contact page can preconfigure itself for policy-related questions. Optionally, the implementation may append the current policy_type as an additional parameter if supported by the contact page, but the only required parameter is topic=policies.

Interaction: Retry after load error
- Trigger: If a getPoliciesContent request fails and a 'Retry' action is available, the user clicks the retry control.
- Behavior: The controller re-invokes getPoliciesContent with the current selectedPolicyType, re-enters the loading state, and replaces the error state with the fetched content on success.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User presses the browser back or forward buttons after having switched between policy types.
- Behavior: The controller detects the new policy_type in the URL, updates selectedPolicyType accordingly, and calls getPoliciesContent(policy_type: selectedPolicyType) to ensure the displayed policy matches the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is the currently selected policy type, represented in the URL as the policy_type query parameter and mirrored in the internal state variable selectedPolicyType.

On initial load:
- The page reads policy_type from the URL query string.
- If policy_type is present and matches a supported policy type, selectedPolicyType is set to that value.
- If policy_type is absent or not recognized, selectedPolicyType is set to a default value determined by the implementation (for example, defaulting to one of the available policies such as terms, privacy, or refunds), without embedding specific policy content in the page itself.
- The page calls getPoliciesContent with policy_type = selectedPolicyType.

On policy_type change from user interaction:
- When the user selects a different policy via in-page controls, the page updates the URL to include the new policy_type (e.g., policies.html?policy_type=privacy) and updates selectedPolicyType.
- A new call is made to getPoliciesContent(policy_type: selectedPolicyType).
- The content and metadata are refreshed based on the API response.

On history navigation (back/forward):
- The page listens for URL changes caused by browser history navigation.
- When policy_type in the URL changes, selectedPolicyType is updated to match.
- The page calls getPoliciesContent with the new selectedPolicyType and updates the UI so that the policy content and active control reflect the URL.

Additional state flags:
- isLoading: true while awaiting a response from getPoliciesContent; controls display of loading indicators and prevents conflicting UI updates.
- errorState: stores whether the last getPoliciesContent call failed and any non-sensitive error information to control display of an error message and optional retry action.

All persistent view state is derived from the URL parameters and API responses; no localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates the overall behavior of policies.html: reads and responds to the policy_type URL parameter, calls getPoliciesContent with the selected policy type, manages loading and error states, and informs child components of the current selectedPolicyType and policy data.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URL.query.policy_type, set internalState.selectedPolicyType, call getPoliciesContent with this value, and propagate the results to child components.

onPolicyTypeChange(newPolicyType): Triggered by child components (e.g., tabs or dropdown). Updates URL.query.policy_type to newPolicyType, sets internalState.selectedPolicyType, sets isLoading=true, clears errorState, calls getPoliciesContent({ policy_type: newPolicyType }), then updates internalState with the response or error.

onUrlChange: Triggered by browser history events. Re-reads URL.query.policy_type, updates internalState.selectedPolicyType, and refetches policy data via getPoliciesContent if the value differs from the current state.
onRetryLoad: Re-invokes getPoliciesContent with internalState.selectedPolicyType after an error and updates isLoading and errorState accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides in-page controls (such as tabs or a dropdown) that represent the available policy types (e.g., terms, privacy, refunds). Indicates which policy type is currently active based on selectedPolicyType and allows the user to switch policies, triggering URL and content updates.
data_binding: supportedPolicyTypes[] (implementation-defined mapping from policy_type values to user-facing labels; not hardcoded policy content)
event_handlers: onClickPolicyType(policyType): When the user selects a policy type, emits an event to policy-page-controller.onPolicyTypeChange(policyType).
onSyncFromUrl: When the controller updates selectedPolicyType due to a URL change, updates the active/selected item in the switcher to match internalState.selectedPolicyType.

components.functionality: Displays the currently loaded policy, including its title, effective date, and HTML body. Handles three visual states: loading (no content yet), error (display errorState), and loaded (render content_html). It does not generate or modify policy content; it only renders what getPoliciesContent returns.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataUpdate: Triggered when policy-page-controller receives new data from getPoliciesContent; updates displayed title, effective date, and content_html.
onLoadingStateChange: Triggered when isLoading changes; toggles between loading indicators and content display.
onErrorStateChange: Triggered when errorState changes; switches between error messaging and normal content.
onRetryClick: If displayed, forwards a retry action to policy-page-controller.onRetryLoad.

components.functionality: Provides a clear way for users to navigate to the contact page for policy-related questions. It constructs a link to contact.html with topic=policies.
data_binding: internalState.selectedPolicyType (optional, only if the implementation chooses to append it as an additional parameter supported by contact.html)
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates the browser to contact.html with query parameter topic=policies, using standard navigation or history APIs.

components.functionality: Shows concise metadata about the currently displayed policy, such as its title and effective date, so users can confirm which policy they are reading and when it became effective.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataUpdate: Triggered when new data arrives from getPoliciesContent; updates text fields for title and effective date.
onPolicyTypeChange: Optionally updates any contextual labels or icons that depend on the selected policy type.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid, visually realized as a 3-column content grid for the photo gallery (each thumbnail approximating 4 columns wide), with a full-width hero spanning all columns at the top.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s hero-plus-gallery pattern using text components: a full-width, cinematic hero at the top (owned by the policy-page-controller), followed immediately by a three-segment horizontal band that visually echoes a 3-column gallery (the policy-type-switcher representing Terms, Privacy, and Refunds). Beneath this, a single, dominant content block (policy-content-viewer + policy-metadata-bar + policy-contact-link) forms a long, scrollable reading area. No sidebars; everything stacks in a clean, top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s hero-plus-gallery pattern using text components: a full-width, cinematic hero at the top (owned by the policy-page-controller), followed immediately by a three-segment horizontal band that visually echoes a 3-column gallery (the policy-type-switcher representing Terms, Privacy, and Refunds). Beneath this, a single, dominant content block (policy-content-viewer + policy-metadata-bar + policy-contact-link) forms a long, scrollable reading area. No sidebars; everything stacks in a clean, top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height and spans the full content width, with narrow side margins to keep it immersive. Below it, the tab switcher horizontally uses about 80–90% of the page width, visually divided into three equal columns. The policy content viewer sits centered below, using roughly 60–70% of the page width for comfortable reading, with generous internal padding but relatively tight outer margins so the dark background remains mostly filled. Vertical gaps between hero → tabs → metadata bar → content → contact link follow the 24–64px scale, with 48–64px between major sections and 24–32px between related elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense feel similar to the gallery: dark background, content blocks visually touching or separated by narrow gutters (8–16px). The hero is image-heavy with minimal text; the tab row is compact and blocky like three gallery tiles; the policy content card is text-dense but framed by a dark, slightly raised panel with comfortable 24–32px internal padding. Surrounding whitespace is modest so the page feels immersive and filled, not airy or minimalist.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention starts at the neon-style title and tagline in the hero, centered near the middle of the screen. From there, the eye drops down to the three horizontally arranged policy tabs, reading left-to-right like a photo gallery row (Terms → Privacy → Refunds). Selecting a tab reinforces downward flow into the main policy-content-viewer, which reads top-to-bottom. Within this text area, the eye naturally moves from the metadata bar at the top, through the policy body, and finally to the contact link anchored near the bottom-right of the content column. Overall flow is hero (top-center) → tabs (center, left-to-right) → content (center, top-to-bottom).

layout.overall_description: The Policies page opens on a dark, cinematic hero band that fills the top 40–50% of the viewport, stretching almost edge-to-edge with only narrow black margins. A gritty, neon-lit horror image sits behind a large, glowing page title like ‘Policies’ centered horizontally and vertically within this space, giving the page the same immersive, event-promo feel as the original mockup. Just below this hero, a tight row of three wide tab tiles spans most of the page width, centered and evenly divided, echoing the mockup’s three-column gallery: each tile represents a policy type and feels like a clickable poster, separated by slim black gutters. Below that row, a single, tall content panel forms the main reading area, centered on the page and narrower than the full width for legibility. This panel has a subtly elevated, near-black background, with a thin metadata bar at its top edge and the main policy text flowing downward inside generous padding. At the very bottom of this column, a right-aligned contact link or button sits after a bit of breathing room, acting as the final call-to-action. Everything is stacked in one vertical column, with modest gaps and a continuous black background that makes the text blocks and tabs feel like illuminated tiles floating in darkness, mirroring the hero + gallery rhythm of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-page-controller manifests as the topmost hero region and overall page container. It occupies the full width of the viewport with minimal side margins (approximately 24–32px per side on large screens), and its height covers about 40–50% of the initial viewport (e.g., 40–50vh). The background is a full-bleed, dark horror/zombie-themed image or gradient that fills this area, with content overlaid in the center. The main heading (e.g., ‘Policies’) is centered horizontally and vertically within this hero band, with a large, neon-style type treatment. A short subtitle or description can sit directly below the title with 8–16px vertical spacing, but the emphasis remains on the cinematic image and title rather than dense text. The bottom edge of this hero aligns just above the policy-type-switcher, with a modest gap of about 24–32px of pure black background before the tabs row begins, maintaining a continuous dark frame around the content while keeping the transition compact. On scroll, this entire hero zone scrolls away naturally, giving way to the tab row and content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored to the top of the page; scrolls out of view normally as the user scrolls down. Acts as the visual ‘hero’ container for the rest of the components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-type-switcher sits immediately below the hero, centered on the page, and visually mimics a three-column image gallery row. On desktop, it spans roughly 80–90% of the page width and is horizontally centered, leaving narrow black gutters on the left and right. Within this band, three equal-width tab tiles (Terms, Privacy, Refunds) sit side by side, each effectively occupying about one-third of the content width (approximately 4 columns in an implied 12-column grid). The tiles share a common height of ~72–96px, giving them a stout, poster-like feel rather than just thin tab labels. Between each tile is a small gap of 8–16px of black background, echoing the narrow gutters between images in the mockup’s gallery. The tiles themselves are dark, slightly elevated rectangles with neon-accented labels, making them pop against the black background. They are aligned along a single horizontal baseline with minimal padding above and below within their band (about 16–24px total vertical padding), so the row feels compact and dense. On tablets and smaller screens, the tiles can wrap: first two on the top row, third on the row below, and on very small screens they stack vertically with 8–16px gaps, but still centered. The row sits about 24–32px below the hero and about 24–32px above the metadata bar/content area, maintaining the tight, image-grid-like rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static; on larger screens it may optionally become sticky just below the viewport top after the hero scrolls away, but the default behavior is to scroll with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-metadata-bar is a slim horizontal strip that sits directly above the main policy-content-viewer, visually attached to it like a header bar. Horizontally, it is aligned to the same central column as the content viewer: about 60–70% of the page width (roughly 8–10 of 12 columns) and centered, so its left and right edges line up exactly with the text panel below. Vertically, it is positioned approximately 24px below the bottom edge of the policy-type-switcher row, creating a clear but compact separation. The bar has a near-black background slightly lighter or more textured than the pure page background, with a height of about 40–56px. Inside, metadata such as ‘Last updated’, version info, and links to download PDFs are left-aligned, with 16–24px horizontal padding on each side and 8–12px vertical padding, keeping the bar dense and minimal. The bottom edge of the metadata bar touches or is separated by a small 8–16px gap from the main content card, so the bar feels like the top part of the content block rather than a standalone section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the primary content column. It visually anchors the start of the policy text but does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-content-viewer forms the main reading panel beneath the metadata bar, functioning as the large, central ‘tile’ analogous to a single oversized image in the gallery. It is centered horizontally and shares its width with the metadata bar above: approximately 60–70% of the page width (8–10 columns) with equal left and right margins of dark background. This creates a clear column of focus down the middle of the page. The top of the content viewer either aligns directly under the metadata bar or is separated by a subtle 8–16px gap, giving the impression of a continuous block. The content viewer has a slightly elevated, dark card-like background that contrasts gently with the pure black page background. Inside, the policy text is padded by 24–32px on all sides, maintaining comfortable readability while preserving the page’s overall dense feel. Headings, paragraphs, and lists stack vertically with roughly 16–24px spacing between sections, so the text flows top-to-bottom in a clear rhythm. The panel extends downward as long as needed, becoming the dominant scrolling content on the page; as users scroll, this card-like column is the main element they see, with the surrounding edges of black background acting as a frame, similar to how the mockup’s gallery images sit within a dark surround.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content area. The panel itself scrolls with the page; there is no internal scroll container, keeping the reading experience continuous.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-contact-link is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, acting as a final call-to-action after users finish reading a policy. It sits within the same central width as the policy-content-viewer, aligned to its right edge for a decisive visual endpoint. Vertically, there is a clear gap of about 24–32px between the end of the last policy paragraph and the contact link, providing a brief breathing space. The link can appear as an underlined neon-accent text link or a compact button-like pill (about 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding) that floats visually in the lower-right corner of the content card area. On wide screens, the rest of the line behind it is empty, reinforcing its anchored position at the right edge; on small screens, it remains right-aligned within the narrower text column. Below the link, a final 32–48px of vertical padding or margin separates it from the page footer, so it feels like the natural final element of the policies page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the main content column; scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the policy text.
